Equally, any locus that DOES couple samādhi/jhāna to sotāpanna would be decisive the other way. The 'doer-to-a-measure in concentration' grading (AN 3.86/87, AN 9.12) places the sotāpanna+sakadāgāmī BELOW the concentration-fulfiller (anāgāmī) on the very axis in question — so the canon's own gradient predicts a sub-full-jhāna concentration suffices for the lower two fruits; the task is to find what the floor actually IS, and whether it is ever named at all for the first fruit.
avenues.avenue: Commentarial upacāra-samādhi vs khaṇika-samādhi (momentary concentration) — the later layer that fills the canonical gap. Confirm its term-distribution to date the concept and show its canonical absence.
avenues.rationale: The commentary (esp. Visuddhimagga, Atthasālinī, the ṭīkā) introduces upacāra-samādhi (access concentration) and khaṇika-samādhi (momentary concentration) and the sukkhavipassaka ('dry-insight' practitioner) — the precise vocabulary that names the unquantified canonical 'measure'. Their distribution should be commentary/abhidhamma-only.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: If upacāra/khaṇika/sukkhavipassaka appear ONLY in commentary/ṭīkā and ZERO times in the mūla canon (as the brief asserts for upacāra), that is the philological proof that the 'access concentration' answer to the floor is a post-canonical construction — the canon left the measure unnamed and the commentary supplied a name.
avenues.inference_path: The PRESENCE of sukkhavipassaka / khaṇikasamādhi in the commentary is itself decisive evidence about the canon: the commentators INVENTED a 'dry-insight, momentary-concentration' category precisely BECAUSE they recognized the canon admits awakening (including lower fruits) without full jhāna and needed a technical name for the minimal concentration involved. The commentary's own dry-insight category is a tradition-internal admission that the canonical floor is sub-jhānic. So even the pro-jhāna Theravāda commentarial tradition concedes, by coining sukkhavipassaka, that the canon does not mandate jhāna for the lower path — the disagreement is only over how LITTLE concentration is needed, not whether full jhāna is required. Date the term-strata (canon=0, commentary>0) and the inference writes itself.

avenues.lens: LENS 2 — What concentration (if any) does sotapatti actually REQUIRE? The question is whether jhāna is ever NAMED as a prerequisite for stream-entry, or whether stream-entry is constituted entirely by non-concentration factors (the four sotāpattiyaṅga: aveccappasāda in Buddha/Dhamma/Saṅgha + ariyakanta-sīla, the breaking of the three saṃyojana, the two entry-modes dhammānusārī/saddhānusārī). avenues.avenue: The 'consolation to the sick/dying' suttas and laypeople instructed at the point of death — where jhāna is practically impossible, yet stream-entry / non-decline is affirmed.
avenues.rationale: Mahānāma (SN 55.21-22) is frightened he'll die unmindful; the Buddha assures him a mind 'long nurtured in faith, virtue, learning, generosity, wisdom' does not fall. Dīghāvu (SN 55.3), Anāthapiṇḍika's deathbed, Nakulapitā — these instruct dying lay disciples and affirm their attainment WITHOUT any jhāna instruction.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Deathbed assurances of stream-entry / non-decline that reference faith+virtue+learning+generosity+wisdom but NOT samādhi/jhāna show the canon certifies the first fruit on a profile that excludes (because circumstance precludes) jhāna.
avenues.inference_path: A dying, frightened, pain-wracked layman CANNOT enter jhāna — yet the Buddha confirms his security in the stream and even guides Dīghāvu from stream-entry toward the higher fruits via the six factors (the four pasāda + two insight-contemplations: aniccasaññā etc.), still with no jhāna. The deathbed genre is a natural experiment isolating the first fruit from concentration: it holds jhāna fixed at ~zero and finds the fruit intact. This is among the cleanest inferential routes — circumstance controls the confound.

avenues.avenue: Stream-entry attained while merely LISTENING (sota = 'ear'/'stream' pun) and the role of hearing the Dhamma — the saddhā-route's input channel, orthogonal to meditation.
avenues.rationale: The four sotāpattiyaṅga AS CAUSES (SN 55.5) are: associating with good people, hearing the true Dhamma, wise attention, practice in accordance with Dhamma. NONE is jhāna. The causal antecedents of stream-entry the canon names are social/cognitive, not concentrative.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: SN 55.5 gives the four factors-FOR-stream-entry (the causes, distinct from the four factors-OF a stream-enterer). If the canonical CAUSES of the first fruit are sappurisasaṃseva / dhammassavana / yonisomanasikāra / dhammānudhammapaṭipatti and jhāna is not among them, the causal account excludes concentration as a named prerequisite.
avenues.inference_path: Two distinct four-lists govern stream-entry: the factors-OF (what a sotāpanna possesses: the four pasāda) and the factors-FOR (what causes it: good friend, hearing Dhamma, wise attention, practice). Jhāna appears in NEITHER list. The canon thus gives a complete causal AND constitutive account of stream-entry with zero samādhi/jhāna content. When BOTH the cause-side and the constitution-side of a phenomenon omit X, the omission is not accidental gap but structural — the strongest form of the near-absence argument. (The 'practice in accordance with the Dhamma' factor is the only place jhāna COULD hide; test whether dhammānudhammapaṭipatti is ever unpacked as jhāna or as insight — it is typically the latter.)

avenues.lens: LENS 2 — What concentration (if any) does sotapatti actually REQUIRE? avenues.avenue: Anāgāmī-by-jhāna passages as the contrastive control — the texts where jhāna IS explicitly the operative cause of a fruit, to show by contrast where the canon DOES make jhāna load-bearing (and it's the third fruit, not the first).
avenues.rationale: AN 4.123-126 etc. describe one who attains jhāna / the brahmavihāras and, on that basis, is reborn in the form realms and attains final nibbāna there (anāgāmī by jhāna). This is the canon EXPLICITLY making jhāna the operative cause — of the THIRD fruit's rebirth-destiny, never of the first.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: That when the canon DOES tie jhāna to a fruit, it ties it to anāgāmī/Brahmā-rebirth, confirming the AN 3.86 grading (concentration-fulfilment = non-returner). Jhāna's canonical job is the third fruit and form-realm rebirth, not stream-entry.
avenues.inference_path: The contrastive control closes the argument: the canon is NOT shy about naming jhāna as decisive WHEN it is decisive — it does so loudly for the anāgāmī's brahmā-realm rebirth and for the formless attainers among the seven persons. The fact that this same canon, so willing to name jhāna where it matters, NEVER names it for the first fruit is therefore a designed silence, not an oversight. A near-absence in a corpus that elsewhere speaks freely on the topic is high-confidence evidence of deliberate non-requirement. This is the methodological keystone: the omission means something precisely because the inclusion is well-attested elsewhere.

avenues.lens: LENS 2 — What concentration (if any) does sotapatti actually REQUIRE? The question is whether jhāna is ever NAMED as a prerequisite for stream-entry, or whether stream-entry is constituted entirely by non-concentration factors (the four sotāpattiyaṅga: aveccappasāda in Buddha/Dhamma/Saṅgha + ariyakanta-sīla, the breaking of the three saṃyojana, the two entry-modes dhammānusārī/saddhānusārī). avenues.avenue: Cūḷa/structural texts on the minimum: the 'one who has even a limited/little' (mattaso, pamāṇa) language and the sekha (trainee) who is 'secure' though not complete.
avenues.rationale: The word mattaso ('to a measure', 'in part') is the exact term in AN 3.86. Tracing its other uses and the sekha-trainee vocabulary maps how the canon talks about partial/incomplete attainment without quantifying it — the linguistic home of 'the measure'.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: How the canon habitually expresses 'partial concentration' — and whether it EVER converts mattaso into a number, a jhāna-count, or an upacāra-like named threshold. It does not; mattaso stays qualitative.
avenues.inference_path: The persistence of qualitative 'mattaso / to-a-measure' language with NO numerical or jhāna-indexed gloss anywhere in the mūla canon is the final positive finding: the canon CHOSE not to quantify the concentration-floor for the lower fruits. The 'measure' is intentionally a gradient-position ('less than the fulfiller'), not a threshold ('at least jhāna-1'). The commentary's upacāra-samādhi converts this open gradient into a named threshold — an act of completion the canon declined. So the honest answer to 'how much concentration does the path require for the lower fruits' is: the canon says 'some, partial, sub-fulfilment, virtue-born, never jhāna-mandated, and deliberately unquantified' — and every avenue above converges on that.

avenues.lens: LENS 3 — The seven/nine ariya-puggala as a concentration map. The noble persons are sorted along two cross-cutting axes: (a) faculty-dominance (paññā vs saddhā vs samādhi/kāya) and (b) possession-vs-non-possession of the formless/peaceful liberations (santā vimokkhā atikkamma rūpe āruppā). The decisive structural fact is that the canon itself splits the ariyas into a WITH-attainments column (ubhatobhāgavimutta, kāyasakkhī) and a WITHOUT-attainments column (paññāvimutta, diṭṭhippatta, saddhāvimutta, dhammānusārī, saddhānusārī). If genuine ariyas — including arahants (paññāvimutta) — can lack the eight liberations, then the formless and even (on the classic reading) the higher rūpa-jhānas are NOT the concentration floor for any noble fruit. The whole lens turns on what 'paññāvimutta WITHOUT the formless liberations' is permitted to lack, and whether the floor it still must clear (sammā-samādhi as the right-jhāna factor) is jhāna proper or something below it. Every grade is a probe into where the canon draws the with/without line and what it tacitly leaves standing on both sides.
avenues.avenue: The two liberation-types as the master with/without split: ubhatobhāgavimutta vs paññāvimutta (MN 70 Kīṭāgiri, AN 9.44, DN 15, Pp 1.30-31)
avenues.rationale: This is the keystone pair. Paññāvimutta is, by the canon's own definition in MN 70 and Pp, an arahant who does NOT touch the eight liberations with the body (na kāyena phusitvā viharati) but whose taints are destroyed by seeing-with-wisdom. The grade exists precisely to certify arahantship without the formless attainments. The definitional clause 'ye te santā vimokkhā atikkamma rūpe āruppā, te na kāyena phusitvā viharati' is the single most load-bearing sentence for the dry/jhana debate.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: An explicit canonical arahant-grade that the texts define by ABSENCE of the formless (and on the strict reading the 'peaceful liberations') attainments — i.e. the canon names a fully-awakened person who lacks the higher samādhi tier.
avenues.inference_path: If paññāvimutta = arahant lacking the formless liberations, then the formless attainments are above the floor for the HIGHEST fruit, a fortiori above the floor for stream-entry/once-return. A near-absence of any clause re-imposing jhāna on the paññāvimutta would itself be evidence the floor sits lower than 4th jhāna. Co-occurrence of 'paññāvimutta' with the jhāna-pericope (vivicceva kāmehi…) in the SAME passage would conversely show the canon still wants jhāna under the paññāvimutta; its absence is informative either way.

avenues.lens: LENS 3 — The seven/nine ariya-puggala as a concentration map. The noble persons are sorted along two cross-cutting axes: (a) faculty-dominance (paññā vs saddhā vs samādhi/kāya) and (b) possession-vs-non-possession of the formless/peaceful liberations (santā vimokkhā atikkamma rūpe āruppā). If genuine ariyas — including arahants (paññāvimutta) — can lack the eight liberations, then the formless and even (on the classic reading) the higher rūpa-jhānas are NOT the concentration floor for any noble fruit. The whole lens turns on what 'paññāvimutta WITHOUT the formless liberations' is permitted to lack, and whether the floor it still must clear (sammā-samādhi as the right-jhāna factor) is jhāna proper or something below it. Every grade is a probe into where the canon draws the with/without line and what it tacitly leaves standing on both sides.
avenues.avenue: Kāyasakkhī (body-witness) as the WITH-attainments non-arahant — the grade that proves possessing the formless liberations is NOT sufficient for the highest fruit
avenues.rationale: Kāyasakkhī touches all eight liberations with the body yet has only SOME taints destroyed (ekacce asavā parikkhīṇā) — a sekha, not an arahant. The pairing kāyasakkhī (max samādhi, partial wisdom) vs paññāvimutta (no formless, full wisdom) is the canon's explicit demonstration that samādhi-depth and liberation-from-taints are decoupled.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The canon ranks a person WITH the full eight liberations BELOW an arahant who lacks them, proving deep samādhi is neither necessary nor sufficient for the decisive break.
avenues.inference_path: Decoupling of samādhi-attainment from taint-destruction across the kāyasakkhī/paññāvimutta pair means the concentration measure that DOES gate awakening must be something other than the liberation-attainments — pushing the gating factor toward the bare sammā-samādhi (right jhāna-as-path-factor) or below. This is the strongest single structural argument that the floor is sub-formless.

The whole lens turns on what 'paññāvimutta WITHOUT the formless liberations' is permitted to lack, and whether the floor it still must clear (sammā-samādhi as the right-jhāna factor) is jhāna proper or something below it. Every grade is a probe into where the canon draws the with/without line and what it tacitly leaves standing on both sides.
avenues.avenue: The full seven-person list of MN 70 and its WITH/WITHOUT annotation per person
avenues.rationale: MN 70 gives the canonical seven and explicitly says some are NOT to be told 'work with diligence' (because done) and some ARE (because not yet done). The diṭṭhippatta/saddhāvimutta pair (sekhas without the formless liberations) and the dhammānusārī/saddhānusārī pair (the two entering-stream candidates) localize exactly where the with/without line falls for the LOWER fruits — which is the operator's specific target.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: A graded ladder in which the lower-fruit persons (diṭṭhippatta, saddhāvimutta, and the two anusārī stream-enterers-to-be) are explicitly the WITHOUT-formless-liberations column.
avenues.inference_path: If every lower-fruit grade sits in the without-column, the formless attainments are categorically absent from the stream-entry/once-return floor by the canon's own sorting. The dhammānusārī/saddhānusārī definitions become the place to read the MINIMUM: whatever faculty-act (paññā-nijjhāna-khanti vs saddhā/pema) suffices to enter the fixed-course, with no jhāna clause attached, sets the actual lower-fruit floor.

avenues.lens: LENS 3 — The seven/nine ariya-puggala as a concentration map. The noble persons are sorted along two cross-cutting axes: (a) faculty-dominance (paññā vs saddhā vs samādhi/kāya) and (b) possession-vs-non-possession of the formless/peaceful liberations (santā vimokkhā atikkamma rūpe āruppā). The decisive structural fact is that the canon itself splits the ariyas into a WITH-attainments column (ubhatobhāgavimutta, kāyasakkhī) and a WITHOUT-attainments column (paññāvimutta, diṭṭhippatta, saddhāvimutta, dhammānusārī, saddhānusārī). If genuine ariyas — including arahants (paññāvimutta) — can lack the eight liberations, then the formless and even (on the classic reading) the higher rūpa-jhānas are NOT the concentration floor for any noble fruit. The whole lens turns on what 'paññāvimutta WITHOUT the formless liberations' is permitted to lack, and whether the floor it still must clear (sammā-samādhi as the right-jhāna factor) is jhāna proper or something below it. Every grade is a probe into where the canon draws the with/without line and what it tacitly leaves standing on both sides.
avenues.avenue: Faculty-dominance as the sorting key (paññindriya/saddhindriya/samādhindriya) — and the ABSENCE of samādhindriya-dominance grades for the lower fruits
avenues.rationale: SN 48 (Indriyasaṃyutta) and AN sort noble persons by which faculty leads. The striking fact: the canon names a paññā-led arahant (paññāvimutta) and a saddhā-led sekha (saddhāvimutta/saddhānusārī) but does NOT erect a parallel 'samādhi-led' noble grade defined by jhāna-possession as the entry criterion — the samādhi axis surfaces only via the liberation-attainments (kāyasakkhī) which are explicitly non-decisive.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Two of the three 'cognitive' faculties (paññā, saddhā) generate primary noble-person grades; samādhi does not generate a peer grade for the lower fruits.
avenues.inference_path: A near-ABSENCE — the lack of a samādhi-dominance noble-grade as the gate for stream-entry — is strong negative evidence that depth-of-concentration is not the sorting variable the canon uses to certify lower attainment. It treats samādhi as a shared substrate (everyone needs SOME) rather than a graded gate (more = higher fruit), which is exactly the 'a measure suffices' reading of samādhismiṃ mattaso kārī. Co-occurrence of indriya-vemattatā with the fruit-grades shows the canon's actual variance theory is faculty-balance, not jhāna-count.

avenues.lens: LENS 3 — The seven/nine ariya-puggala as a concentration map. The noble persons are sorted along two cross-cutting axes: (a) faculty-dominance (paññā vs saddhā vs samādhi/kāya) and (b) possession-vs-non-possession of the formless/peaceful liberations (santā vimokkhā atikkamma rūpe āruppā). The whole lens turns on what 'paññāvimutta WITHOUT the formless liberations' is permitted to lack, and whether the floor it still must clear (sammā-samādhi as the right-jhāna factor) is jhāna proper or something below it. Every grade is a probe into where the canon draws the with/without line and what it tacitly leaves standing on both sides.
avenues.avenue: The four stream-enterer sub-grades (ekabījī, kolaṅkola, sattakkhattuparama) and once-returner — graded by REMAINING REBIRTHS / fetter-attenuation, never by jhāna
avenues.rationale: The stream-enterer's internal grading (ekabījī one more birth, kolaṅkola 2-3 families, sattakkhattuparama 7 max) is given by AN 3.86-88, AN 10.64, SN 55, Pp. The grading variable is residual rebirths and fetter/faculty maturity — pointedly NOT a samādhi metric. AN 3.86/3.87 (the operator's anchor) defines all three as 'fulfiller of virtue, doer-to-a-measure of samādhi and paññā' for the lower two trainings.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The canon's own fine-graining of the lowest fruit is by rebirth-count and fetter-status, with samādhi held at 'a measure' (mattaso) flat across all three sub-grades.
avenues.inference_path: If the sub-grading of the stream-enterer never invokes jhāna-depth and explicitly fixes samādhi at 'mattaso' for all of them, then the GRADIENT in the lower fruits runs on wisdom/fetter-cutting, not on concentration. The mattaso-kārī clause sitting flat across ekabījī→sattakkhattuparama means the 'measure' of samādhi is a threshold to be cleared once, not a dial that distinguishes the lower fruits — direct support for a fixed, modest concentration floor.

avenues.lens: LENS 3 — The seven/nine ariya-puggala as a concentration map. The noble persons are sorted along two cross-cutting axes: (a) faculty-dominance (paññā vs saddhā vs samādhi/kāya) and (b) possession-vs-non-possession of the formless/peaceful liberations (santā vimokkhā atikkamma rūpe āruppā). The decisive structural fact is that the canon itself splits the ariyas into a WITH-attainments column (ubhatobhāgavimutta, kāyasakkhī) and a WITHOUT-attainments column (paññāvimutta, diṭṭhippatta, saddhāvimutta, dhammānusārī, saddhānusārī). If genuine ariyas — including arahants (paññāvimutta) — can lack the eight liberations, then the formless and even (on the classic reading) the higher rūpa-jhānas are NOT the concentration floor for any noble fruit. The whole lens turns on what 'paññāvimutta WITHOUT the formless liberations' is permitted to lack, and whether the floor it still must clear (sammā-samādhi as the right-jhāna factor) is jhāna proper or something below it. Every grade is a probe into where the canon draws the with/without line and what it tacitly leaves standing on both sides.
avenues.avenue: AN 3.86/3.87/3.88 (sikkha-suttas) — the mattaso-kārī gradient itself, read as a concentration map
avenues.rationale: This IS the operator's hinge text. The stream-enterer & once-returner are 'fulfiller in virtue, doer-to-a-measure (mattaso) in samādhi and wisdom'; the non-returner fulfils samādhi but is doer-to-a-measure in wisdom; the arahant fulfils all three. Reading the ariya-puggala onto this grid shows samādhi-fulfilment (samādhismiṃ paripūrakārī) first becomes mandatory at the non-returner, NOT before.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: An explicit canonical statement that full concentration is required only at the third fruit; the lower two fruits require samādhi merely 'to a measure'.
avenues.inference_path: The exact phrase samādhismiṃ mattaso kārī, mapped to the ariya grades, is the quantitative answer the canon DOES give: the concentration floor for stream-entry/once-return is sub-maximal by definition, and 'fulfilment' (which the commentary glosses as jhāna) is deferred to the non-returner. If adhicitta is glossed elsewhere (search adhicitta + jhāna co-occurrence) as the four jhānas, then 'mattaso in adhicitta' = something-less-than-the-four-jhānas for the lower fruits — pinning the floor below full jhāna. A near-absence of any text raising the lower-fruit samādhi above 'mattaso' confirms the ceiling.

avenues.avenue: AN 9.12 Saupādisesa — the nine grades that survive 'doer-to-a-measure', proving lower fruits attainable WITHOUT samādhi-fulfilment
avenues.rationale: AN 9.12 is the locus classicus where the Buddha rebukes the assumption that only arahants escape lower destinies, listing nine ariya-grades who, despite being 'doers-to-a-measure' (not perfect), are saved. It cross-walks the five non-returner sub-types (the antarā-/upahacca-/sasaṅkhāra-/asaṅkhāra-/uddhaṃsota set) with the lower-fruit grades.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Explicit canonical sanction that 'incomplete' practitioners (mattaso-kārī) attain real, irreversible noble fruit short of arahantship — i.e. the lower fruits do not demand the full training stack.
avenues.inference_path: The very existence of nine grades 'with remainder' who succeeded while imperfect is the canon conceding a sub-maximal floor. The five non-returner sub-grades, distinguished by WHEN/HOW final nibbāna comes (antarā vs uddhaṃsota), are graded by momentum/fetter-residue — and uddhaṃsota-akaniṭṭhagāmī (going upstream through the pure abodes) is the only one with an overt jhāna correlate, which localizes jhāna-relevance to the rebirth-destination of non-returners, NOT to the lower fruits' attainment-floor.

avenues.avenue: Sammā-samādhi defined AS the four jhānas (the magga-factor floor) vs samādhi-as-attainment — the definitional question under the whole debate
avenues.rationale: DN 22 / MN 141 / SN 45.8 define sammā-samādhi as the four jhānas; but MN 117's 'noble right concentration with its supports' makes samādhi the cittekaggatā girdled by the other seven path-factors, which some read as one-pointedness not necessarily absorption. The commentary's tripartite khaṇika/upacāra/appanā schema (with upacāra at 0 canon) is the gap-filler the operator names.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Whether the canon ever permits sammā-samādhi to be cittekaggatā-short-of-jhāna, or always pins it to the four-jhāna pericope; and whether khaṇika/upacāra terminology has ANY canonical footing (predicted near-zero).
avenues.inference_path: If sammā-samādhi is rigidly the four jhānas everywhere it is DEFINED, and the lower fruits require the path (which includes sammā-samādhi), there is tension with the mattaso-kārī grading — the resolution scholars propose is that the path-factor is 'present in measure' / momentarily for the stream-enterer and only 'fulfilled' (sustained jhāna) for the non-returner. A canonical zero for upacāra/khaṇika confirms the commentary INVENTS the sub-jhānic floor; the canon's own move is instead mattaso (a measure of the real factor) — a quantitative-by-degree, not a kind-different, lowering.

avenues.avenue: MN 64 Mahāmālukya / AN 9.36 Jhānasutta — destroying the lower fetters FROM WITHIN jhāna, and whether the FIRST jhāna suffices for the lower fruits
avenues.rationale: MN 64 and AN 9.36 say the five LOWER fetters (whose abandonment = non-returner; the first three = stream-entry) can be cut in dependence on the FIRST jhāna (and up through the dimension of nothingness). Crucially AN 9.36 adds that the two highest formless attainments are NOT a basis for the breakthrough (yāvatā saññāsamāpatti tāvatā aññāpaṭivedho).
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The canon caps the USEFUL concentration for the liberating breakthrough at the lower jhānas/lower formless, and explicitly excludes the two peak attainments — and ties even the lower-fetter work to as little as the first jhāna.
avenues.inference_path: If the first jhāna is sufficient as the platform to cut even the higher of the lower fetters, then for the stream-entry fetters (the first three) the required platform is at most the first jhāna — and the texts that let stream-entry occur on hearing a discourse (e.g. via the gradual talk anupubbikathā) suggest the platform can be momentary unification rather than sustained attainment. The AN 9.36 exclusion of the top attainments mirrors AN 9.36/kāyasakkhī: peak samādhi is decorative for the breakthrough. This bounds the floor: at most first jhāna, possibly its momentary analogue, for the lower fruits.

avenues.avenue: The 'samādhi-developing' typologies — AN 4.94 / AN 10.54-55 (samatha-vipassanā yokings) and the cetosamatha-without-paññā person — to locate where insufficient samādhi vs insufficient wisdom blocks which fruit
avenues.rationale: AN 4.94 sorts practitioners by whether they have internal tranquility (cetosamatha) but lack higher-wisdom-insight, or vice versa, and how to round out. AN 4.170 gives the four yoke-orders (samatha-first, vipassanā-first, yoked, or dhamma-uddhacca-restlessness). These show the canon treats samatha as a separable, sometimes-deficient component that one ADDS — implying it is not an all-or-nothing precondition baked into every fruit.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Canonical recognition of practitioners who have wisdom-insight but deficient tranquility (and the reverse), each still on the path — a person with sub-par samādhi is not thereby barred from progress.
avenues.inference_path: If the canon names someone who lacks internal cetosamatha yet has insight and is told merely to 'develop' the missing samatha (not that they have no attainment), then deficient concentration delays/limits but does not nullify the lower realizations. The vipassanā-pubbaṅgama (insight-led) order is the textual seed of the 'dry-insight' reading — its mere presence as a sanctioned route weakens the claim that jhāna is a universal gate. Conversely, if EVERY yoke-order still terminates in magga requiring sammā-samādhi, the floor reasserts itself at the path-moment — telling us the floor bites at realization, not throughout cultivation.

avenues.avenue: The two anusārī (dhammānusārī / saddhānusārī) entry definitions — the absolute minimum to enter the fixed course (the floor's floor)
avenues.rationale: SN 25 (Okkantasaṃyutta) defines saddhānusārī (one who has mere faith/affection toward the teaching that the aggregates are impermanent) and dhammānusārī (one for whom those teachings are 'accepted to a measure by wisdom'). These are the verge-of-stream-entry persons — and their definitions invoke saddhā or paññā-nijjhāna-khanti, with NO samādhi clause at all.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The minimal entry to the irreversible noble path is defined by faith or wisdom-acceptance, with zero stated concentration requirement.
avenues.inference_path: This is the cleanest near-absence in the whole lens: if the canon's own DEFINITION of the lowest noble-bound person omits any samādhi term, the formal floor for entering the fixed course is not stated in concentration terms at all. Either (a) samādhi is presupposed as 'a measure' carried in from the path-factor, or (b) it is genuinely not gated here. Either reading caps the stream-entry concentration floor at 'whatever measure the eightfold path tacitly carries' — never at jhāna-attainment. The saddhānusārī especially (faith-led, possibly low samādhi) is the canon's existence-proof that the lowest fruit's verge does not require demonstrated absorption.

avenues.avenue: Negative/control searches — terms the COMMENTARY uses to fill the gap, to confirm their canonical absence (the upacāra-samādhi zero)
avenues.rationale: The operator names upacāra-samādhi as '0 canon'. Establishing the canonical zero (or near-zero) of upacāra, sukkhavipassaka ('dry-insight worker'), parikamma/uggaha/paṭibhāga-nimitta, and lokuttara-jjhāna is essential: it shows the entire sub-jhānic-floor apparatus is a post-canonical construction, AND that 'dry insight' as a named category is likewise commentarial.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Whether the gap-filling vocabulary (upacāra, sukkhavipassaka, the nimitta-progression) is present anywhere in canon/Abhidhamma proper or only in Aṭṭhakathā/Ṭīkā.
avenues.inference_path: A canon/Abhidhamma zero with commentary-only hits is itself the finding: BOTH sides of the modern debate (jhāna-required AND dry-insight) partly rest on commentarial categories the canon does not state, which means the canon's actual answer must be read off the mattaso-kārī gradient and the with/without-attainments split, NOT off either later category. The DISTRIBUTION of the zero (e.g. sukkhavipassaka appears first in which commentary) dates when the dry-path was reified. If upacāra appears in the Abhidhamma Vibhaṅga even once, the floor-lowering has earlier roots than assumed — a sharp, datable inference from a single hit.

avenues.avenue: Stream-entry attained mid-discourse / by lay hearers — circumstantial evidence the floor is low enough to clear while listening
avenues.rationale: The stock conversion scene (e.g. of laypeople, of the 60 in the Vinaya, of Upāli, Yasa's father) has the dhamma-eye arise during a gradual talk culminating in the four truths, with the mind described only as 'ready, malleable, free of hindrances, uplifted, confident' (kallacitta muducitta vinīvaraṇacitta) — a low-grade, momentary composure, NOT a stated jhāna.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Stream-entry repeatedly occurs in a setting whose only stated concentration descriptor is hindrance-free malleability of mind during listening.
avenues.inference_path: If the recurring narrative floor for stream-entry is 'mind free of the five hindrances and receptive' rather than 'emerged from jhāna', the canon's PRACTICE-floor for the lowest fruit is the temporary suppression of hindrances (which the commentary would call upacāra) — i.e. access-grade composure, not absorption. This is the strongest behavioral-frequency evidence: the SET of conversion scenes co-occurring with kallacitta-style descriptors and NOT with the jhāna-pericope quantifies, by sheer distribution, that the lower-fruit floor sits at hindrance-suppression. The vinīvaraṇa term is the canonical bridge to what later becomes upacāra.

The whole lens turns on what 'paññāvimutta WITHOUT the formless liberations' is permitted to lack, and whether the floor it still must clear (sammā-samādhi as the right-jhāna factor) is jhāna proper or something below it. Every grade is a probe into where the canon draws the with/without line and what it tacitly leaves standing on both sides.
avenues.avenue: Hindrance-suppression (nīvaraṇa) as the real functional floor, cross-cut with the jhāna pericope — is 'temporary abandoning of hindrances' the canon's actual samādhi-threshold?
avenues.rationale: Every jhāna formula is preceded by 'having abandoned the five hindrances'. If lower-fruit realization requires only the hindrance-free state (the doorway to but short of first jhāna), the floor is exactly the threshold the first jhāna pericope opens with — which is what the commentary later names upacāra. The Sāmaññaphala graduated path makes nīvaraṇa-pahāna the gateway before jhāna.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Whether the canon treats 'hindrances abandoned' as a state realizable and useful for insight independently of completing the jhāna it normally introduces.
avenues.inference_path: If insight/breakthrough is ever predicated directly on the hindrance-free mind WITHOUT the rest of the jhāna formula, the canon itself recognizes a functional sub-jhānic concentration floor — vindicating the upacāra concept's PHENOMENON while denying its NAME. Conversely, if hindrance-abandoning is never decoupled from at least first jhāna, the floor is pinned at first jhāna minimum. The co-occurrence pattern of 'pañca nīvaraṇe pahāya' with insight-verbs (pajānāti, yathābhūtaṃ) versus exclusively with the jhāna-formula is the discriminating test. This converts the debate into a measurable distribution question.

The whole lens turns on what 'paññāvimutta WITHOUT the formless liberations' is permitted to lack, and whether the floor it still must clear (sammā-samādhi as the right-jhāna factor) is jhāna proper or something below it. avenues.avenue: Puggalapaññatti's systematic ariya enumeration and the Abhidhamma's own with/without-attainment definitions (the canonical Abhidhamma vote, distinct from later commentary)
avenues.rationale: Pp is canonical (Abhidhamma piṭaka) and restates the seven/nine with the SAME with/without-attainment clauses as MN 70 — so it is an independent, early-systematizing witness to the split. It also adds the samasīsī and finer non-returner subdivisions, letting us see how the Abhidhamma itself does (or does NOT) inject a samādhi metric.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The Abhidhamma piṭaka's own coding of the noble persons — whether it preserves the bare with/without-liberations split or already begins to graft a concentration metric onto the lower fruits.
avenues.inference_path: Because Pp is the bridge between canon and commentary, its handling is chronologically diagnostic. If Pp keeps the floor at the bare faculty/fetter definitions and STILL omits upacāra, the gap-filling is purely Aṭṭhakathā-era — dating the sub-jhānic floor's invention to after the Abhidhamma. If Pp already nudges (e.g. defines a grade by jhāna), the lowering started in the canonical Abhidhamma. Either way Pp's text is a stratigraphic marker on WHEN the concentration-floor question got reinterpreted, which is exactly the canon-vs-commentary axis the operator's debate turns on.

avenues.avenue: Samādhi-bhāvanā fourfold and the 'jhāyī' epithet distribution — does the canon ever attach jhāna-mastery as an entry-marker to the LOWER ariyas specifically?
avenues.rationale: Testing the converse: search whether 'jhāyī'/jhāna-attainment is ever predicated of stream-enterers/once-returners AS their defining feature. The expectation is that jhāna-epithets cluster on arahants, non-returners, and meditation-exemplars (Anuruddha, the abhibhāyatana masters) but NOT on the lower-fruit definitions.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The distribution of jhāna-language across the ariya grades — whether it pools at the top/non-returner or also tags the lower fruits.
avenues.inference_path: A skew where jhāna-mastery epithets attach to non-returners/arahants and meditation-virtuosos but are ABSENT from stream-enterer/once-returner definitions is positive-by-distribution evidence that the canon associates deep concentration with the HIGHER fruits and rebirth-destinations (esp. the pure-abode-bound non-returner), leaving the lower fruits jhāna-unmarked. This dovetails with mattaso-kārī: concentration-fulfilment is a non-returner feature. A surprise hit (jhāna defining a stream-enterer) would be a strong counter-finding and must be hunted to be fair to the jhāna-required side.

avenues.lens: LENS 3 — The seven/nine ariya-puggala as a concentration map. The noble persons are sorted along two cross-cutting axes: (a) faculty-dominance (paññā vs saddhā vs samādhi/kāya) and (b) possession-vs-non-possession of the formless/peaceful liberations (santā vimokkhā atikkamma rūpe āruppā). The decisive structural fact is that the canon itself splits the ariyas into a WITH-attainments column (ubhatobhāgavimutta, kāyasakkhī) and a WITHOUT-attainments column (paññāvimutta, diṭṭhippatta, saddhāvimutta, dhammānusārī, saddhānusārī). If genuine ariyas — including arahants (paññāvimutta) — can lack the eight liberations, then the formless and even (on the classic reading) the higher rūpa-jhānas are NOT the concentration floor for any noble fruit. The whole lens turns on what 'paññāvimutta WITHOUT the formless liberations' is permitted to lack, and whether the floor it still must clear (sammā-samādhi as the right-jhāna factor) is jhāna proper or something below it. Every grade is a probe into where the canon draws the with/without line and what it tacitly leaves standing on both sides.
avenues.avenue: The samasīsī and the 'liberated-in-both-ways without formless' edge cases — testing whether ubhatobhāgavimutta REQUIRES the formless or only the rūpa-jhānas
avenues.rationale: There is a live scholarly question (and a canonical ambiguity) whether ubhatobhāgavimutta needs all eight liberations (incl. the formless and saññāvedayitanirodha) or whether 'both-ways' just means liberated by serenity AND wisdom with at least one immaterial/peaceful attainment. Pinning this fixes the TOP of the samādhi map and, by contrast, sharpens what paññāvimutta lacks.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The exact attainment-content the canon requires of the both-ways-liberated, hence the precise width of the with/without gap.
avenues.inference_path: Defining the ceiling (ubhatobhāgavimutta = serenity-liberation via the formless/cessation PLUS wisdom-liberation) by contrast measures the floor: everything below that ceiling, including the entire lower-fruit column, is certified WITHOUT those peaceful liberations. If even the both-ways grade can be satisfied by a rūpa-jhāna-based 'peaceful liberation' rather than strictly the formless, the samādhi requirement compresses downward across the whole map. The nine anupubbavihāra (jhānas + formless + cessation) provide the canonical ruler against which every grade's stated attainments are measured — co-locating each grade on that 0-to-9 ruler is the literal 'concentration map' the lens asks for.

avenues.lens: LENS 4 — Is jhāna required for awakening at all? The question is whether the canonical (CST Pāli: nikāya + Vinaya + Abhidhamma + aṭṭhakathā + ṭīkā) record makes the four rūpa-jhānas a NECESSARY condition for any of the four fruits (especially the lower two, stream-entry and once-return), or whether it leaves room for a "dry-insight"/bare-insight (sukkhavipassaka) path. The lens reasons from BOTH directions: (a) pro-jhāna loci — sammāsamādhi DEFINED as the four jhānas, the "depending on the first jhāna the destruction of taints" frame, the jhāyatha exhortations, the jhāna→nibbāna path framings; and (b) anti-necessity / dry-path loci — the paññāvimutta arahant who lacks the formless (and arguably the jhānas), the "sixty bhikkhus" liberated-by-wisdom passages, the susīma-style admissions, the gradation that demands only "a measure" of samādhi for the lower fruits, and the commentarial sukkhavipassaka/upacāra-samādhi machinery that fills a canonical gap. Strategy: don't just count jhāna-mentions; mine the QUALIFIER structure (who is said to attain what WITHOUT what), the negative space (fruits attained where jhāna is conspicuously unmentioned), and the commentarial neologisms that betray a felt canonical gap. A near-absence is as probative as a hit: if the commentaries must coin upacāra-samādhi and sukkhavipassaka, the canon did not supply them — that silence is the finding.
avenues.avenue: sammāsamādhi explicitly DEFINED as the four jhānas (the strongest pro-jhāna structural argument: if right-concentration = jhāna and right-concentration is a path-factor for all noble persons, jhāna is structurally required)
avenues.rationale: DN 22, SN 45.8 (Vibhaṅgasutta), MN 141 define sammāsamādhi as exactly the four jhāna pericope. Since the Noble Eightfold Path is the path to ALL four fruits, this is the canonical backbone of the pro-jhāna case. The exact pericope wording is what to match.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Confirms the canon repeatedly equates right-concentration with the four jhānas verbatim, making jhāna a definitional path-factor; the question then becomes whether the path-factor must be fully cultivated for the lower fruits or only 'to a measure'.
avenues.inference_path: A hit is expected and strong for the pro-jhāna side. The INFERENCE leverage is in co-occurrence: search whether any text ever defines sammāsamādhi WITHOUT the jhāna pericope (e.g. as cittassa ekaggatā alone). If an alternate, non-jhānic definition of sammāsamādhi exists anywhere (esp. Abhidhamma/commentary), that fractures the 'jhāna is definitionally required' argument and licenses the dry-path reading. Near-absence of any non-jhānic sammāsamādhi definition in the canon = the definitional argument holds at the canonical layer and the dry path must be smuggled in via the commentary's ekaggatā/upacāra reframing.

avenues.lens: LENS 4 — Is jhāna required for awakening at all? The question is whether the canonical (CST Pāli: nikāya + Vinaya + Abhidhamma + aṭṭhakathā + ṭīkā) record makes the four rūpa-jhānas a NECESSARY condition for any of the four fruits (especially the lower two, stream-entry and once-return), or whether it leaves room for a "dry-insight"/bare-insight (sukkhavipassaka) path. A near-absence is as probative as a hit: if the commentaries must coin upacāra-samādhi and sukkhavipassaka, the canon did not supply them — that silence is the finding.
avenues.avenue: Alternate / minimal definitions of samādhi as one-pointedness (cittass'ekaggatā) decoupled from the jhāna formula — the lever that lets commentary admit sub-jhānic concentration
avenues.rationale: If samādhi can be glossed as mere ekaggatā (one-pointedness of any kusala citta) rather than the jhāna pericope, then 'a measure of concentration' for stream-entry need not be jhānic. khaṇika-samādhi (momentary concentration) and upacāra-samādhi (access) are the commentarial bridge concepts; their presence/absence by layer is decisive.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: A hit on khanikasamadhi / upacarasamadhi / appanasamadhi in a given layer dates the dry-path machinery. The prompt asserts upacāra = 0 in canon; confirming that boundary (only Abhidhamma/commentary/ṭīkā hits, never mūla) is itself the key finding.
avenues.inference_path: STRATIFICATION IS THE INFERENCE. If upacarasamadhi/khanikasamadhi appear ONLY in aṭṭhakathā+ṭīkā (and Visuddhimagga) and NEVER in the four nikāyas or Vinaya, that near-absence proves the canon never named a sub-jhānic concentration grade — the commentary INVENTED a category to fill the gap left by 'samādhismiṃ mattaso kārī'. That manufactured category is the strongest evidence the canon itself left the measure unquantified and did NOT explicitly license a dry path; the dry path is a commentarial reconstruction, not a stated canonical doctrine. Conversely a single mūla hit on upacāra would overturn the prompt's premise.

The question is whether the canonical (CST Pāli: nikāya + Vinaya + Abhidhamma + aṭṭhakathā + ṭīkā) record makes the four rūpa-jhānas a NECESSARY condition for any of the four fruits (especially the lower two, stream-entry and once-return), or whether it leaves room for a "dry-insight"/bare-insight (sukkhavipassaka) path. A near-absence is as probative as a hit: if the commentaries must coin upacāra-samādhi and sukkhavipassaka, the canon did not supply them — that silence is the finding.
avenues.avenue: paññāvimutta (liberated-by-wisdom) arahant explicitly contrasted with ubhatobhāgavimutta (both-ways-liberated) — the canon's own admission that some arahants lack the (formless, and arguably rūpa) attainments
avenues.rationale: MN 70 (Kīṭāgiri) and DN 15 / AN classifications define paññāvimutta as the arahant who does NOT touch the eight deliverances / formless with the body but is liberated by seeing with wisdom. The exact phrase 'na heva kho aṭṭha vimokkhe kāyena phusitvā viharati, paññāya cassa disvā āsavā parikkhīṇā honti' is the canonical anchor of the wisdom-liberated type.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Establishes that the canon explicitly recognizes arahants lacking the formless attainments — so the formless are NOT required. The contested residue is whether paññāvimutta also lacks the four rūpa-jhānas or merely the four arūpa.
avenues.inference_path: Co-occurrence test: does the paññāvimutta definition mention only the eight vimokkhā/formless (aruppa) as what's lacked, or does it ever say the person lacks jhāna entirely? The canon's silence on jhāna in the paññāvimutta definition cuts BOTH ways and that ambiguity IS the scholarly debate (Gombrich/Cousins: paññāvimutta still has rūpa-jhāna; the dry-path reading: he need not). Check whether commentary on MN 70 (Papañcasūdanī) glosses paññāvimutta to INCLUDE the sukkhavipassaka who lacks even rūpa-jhāna — if Ps explicitly subdivides paññāvimutta into jhāna-possessing vs sukkhavipassaka, the commentary is reading the dry path INTO a canonical category that left it open. That is the inference chain from a commentarial gloss back to a canonical silence.

The question is whether the canonical (CST Pāli: nikāya + Vinaya + Abhidhamma + aṭṭhakathā + ṭīkā) record makes the four rūpa-jhānas a NECESSARY condition for any of the four fruits (especially the lower two, stream-entry and once-return), or whether it leaves room for a "dry-insight"/bare-insight (sukkhavipassaka) path. A near-absence is as probative as a hit: if the commentaries must coin upacāra-samādhi and sukkhavipassaka, the canon did not supply them — that silence is the finding.
avenues.avenue: sukkhavipassaka / suddhavipassanāyānika — the commentary's explicit name for the bare-insight practitioner (the term whose canonical absence is the whole question)
avenues.rationale: sukkhavipassaka ('dry-insight worker') and the samathayānika/vipassanāyānika pair (concentration-vehicle vs insight-vehicle practitioner) are the commentarial terms that NAME the dry path. Their stratification is the cleanest test of whether the dry path is canonical or constructed.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Where these terms first appear by layer. If sukkhavipassaka is purely commentarial/ṭīkā, the canon never named a dry-insight practitioner.
avenues.inference_path: NEAR-ABSENCE IS THE FINDING. The prompt's framing ('the commentary fills the gap') predicts sukkhavipassaka = 0 in the four nikāyas/Vinaya/Abhidhamma-mūla and positive only in aṭṭhakathā+ṭīkā+Visuddhimagga. Confirming that boundary shows the dry path is a commentarial category answering a canonical underdetermination — the canon graded concentration ('mattaso kārī') but never authorized a jhāna-free route by name; the commentary did. The samathayānika/vipassanāyānika pair appearing in AN 4.170 (Yuganaddha)'s commentary but the SUTTA itself only listing four modes (samatha-then-vipassanā, vipassanā-then-samatha, yoked, mind-seized-by-dhamma-excitement) without the dry label, would show the canon describes sequencing flexibility but stops short of authorizing zero-samatha.

avenues.lens: LENS 4 — Is jhāna required for awakening at all? The question is whether the canonical (CST Pāli: nikāya + Vinaya + Abhidhamma + aṭṭhakathā + ṭīkā) record makes the four rūpa-jhānas a NECESSARY condition for any of the four fruits (especially the lower two, stream-entry and once-return), or whether it leaves room for a "dry-insight"/bare-insight (sukkhavipassaka) path. A near-absence is as probative as a hit: if the commentaries must coin upacāra-samādhi and sukkhavipassaka, the canon did not supply them — that silence is the finding.
avenues.avenue: AN 4.170 Yuganaddha (the four ways to arahantship: samatha-first, vipassanā-first, yoked, dhamma-uddhacca) — the canonical seed the dry-path reading grows from
avenues.rationale: AN 4.170 (Ānanda to the bhikkhus) lists vipassanā-preceded-by-samatha as one valid order — read by dry-path advocates as licensing insight-led practice. But note it still requires samatha to develop; it sequences, it does not eliminate.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The exact canonical wording of the four modes — crucially whether 'vipassanāpubbaṅgamaṃ samathaṃ bhāveti' still ends in samatha being developed (sequencing) versus dispensed with.
avenues.inference_path: The INFERENCE turns on what the fourth mode (dhammuddhacca-viggahita, 'mind seized by excitement about the dhamma') resolves to. If the sutta says even there 'the mind settles, becomes concentrated' (cittam santitthati sannisidati ekodi hoti samadhiyati), the canon insists concentration ARISES in every route — no route is concentration-free. That co-occurrence (every one of the four ways converges on samādhiyati) would be strong evidence the canon requires concentration to arise even on the insight-led path, undercutting a strictly dry reading while still permitting insight-LED sequencing. The commentary's importation of sukkhavipassaka onto this sutta would then be visible as an over-reading.

avenues.lens: LENS 4 — Is jhāna required for awakening at all? The question is whether the canonical (CST Pāli: nikāya + Vinaya + Abhidhamma + aṭṭhakathā + ṭīkā) record makes the four rūpa-jhānas a NECESSARY condition for any of the four fruits (especially the lower two, stream-entry and once-return), or whether it leaves room for a "dry-insight"/bare-insight (sukkhavipassaka) path. The lens reasons from BOTH directions: (a) pro-jhāna loci — sammāsamādhi DEFINED as the four jhānas, the "depending on the first jhāna the destruction of taints" frame, the jhāyatha exhortations, the jhāna→nibbāna path framings; and (b) anti-necessity / dry-path loci — the paññāvimutta arahant who lacks the formless (and arguably the jhānas), the "sixty bhikkhus" liberated-by-wisdom passages, the susīma-style admissions, the gradation that demands only "a measure" of samādhi for the lower fruits, and the commentarial sukkhavipassaka/upacāra-samādhi machinery that fills a canonical gap. A near-absence is as probative as a hit: if the commentaries must coin upacāra-samādhi and sukkhavipassaka, the canon did not supply them — that silence is the finding.
avenues.avenue: Susīma-sutta (SN 12.70) — the paññāvimutta arahants who deny the formless attainments; the single most-cited canonical proof-text for a non-jhānic path
avenues.rationale: SN 12.70: arahants tell Susīma they are paññāvimutta and do NOT have the iddhis or the formless 'peaceful liberations transcending form'. Then the Buddha says 'first the knowledge of the stability of the dhamma (dependent origination), afterward the knowledge of nibbāna'. Dry-path camp: proof arahantship without jhāna. Jhāna camp: they deny only iddhi + formless, not rūpa-jhāna.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Exact list of what the Susīma arahants deny (iddhi, dibba-sota, cetopariya, pubbenivāsa, dibba-cakkhu, formless vimokkhā) — note rūpa-jhāna is NOT in the denied list.
avenues.inference_path: ARGUMENT FROM THE STRUCTURE OF THE DENIAL LIST. The arahants deny the five abhiññā + the formless deliverances but the rūpa-jhānas are conspicuously NOT named in the denial. Two inferences compete: (a) silence = they still had rūpa-jhāna (jhāna camp, Bodhi); (b) the point is they had no supernormal attainments at all and jhāna simply wasn't the topic (dry camp). The probative move is to check the COMMENTARY (Sāratthappakāsinī on SN 12.70): if Spk explicitly says these were sukkhavipassaka arahants lacking even jhāna, the commentary commits to the dry reading and we can date that commitment. If Spk instead supplies jhāna to them, the commentary resists the dry reading. Either way the canonical text's silence about rūpa-jhāna is the load-bearing gap — the commentary's choice reveals which way the tradition leaned.

The question is whether the canonical (CST Pāli: nikāya + Vinaya + Abhidhamma + aṭṭhakathā + ṭīkā) record makes the four rūpa-jhānas a NECESSARY condition for any of the four fruits (especially the lower two, stream-entry and once-return), or whether it leaves room for a "dry-insight"/bare-insight (sukkhavipassaka) path. A near-absence is as probative as a hit: if the commentaries must coin upacāra-samādhi and sukkhavipassaka, the canon did not supply them — that silence is the finding.
avenues.avenue: 'depending on the first jhāna the destruction of the taints' (AN 9.36 Jhāna-sutta / Aṭṭhakanāgara MN 52 / AN 11.16) — the pro-jhāna text that makes jhāna the PLATFORM for taint-destruction
avenues.rationale: AN 9.36 and MN 52 (Aṭṭhakanāgara) say: standing in (each of) the first jhāna up through the base of nothingness, one contemplates the factors present there as impermanent etc., and 'depending on that' destroys the taints (or, if not, becomes a non-returner). This is the strongest pro-jhāna locus: jhāna is the launchpad.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The canon does present a jhāna-based insight route to arahantship, and explicitly to non-return as the fallback — directly relevant to the lens.
avenues.inference_path: DOUBLE INFERENCE. (1) Pro-jhāna: insight is performed ON the jhāna factors, so here jhāna is required. BUT (2) note the structural detail that the SCHEME STARTS AT THE FIRST JHĀNA and runs only through ākiñcaññāyatana, conspicuously OMITTING neither-perception-nor-non-perception and cessation (which lack discernible factors to contemplate) — and crucially says nothing about a sub-first-jhāna stage. The ABSENCE of any 'depending on access-concentration the destruction of the taints' formula here is probative: the canon's lowest insight-platform named is the FIRST JHĀNA, never anything below it. That near-absence (no sub-jhānic platform ever named as a taint-destruction base) is strong evidence the canon, where it specifies a concentration base for insight, sets the floor AT first jhāna — and the commentary's upacāra-based insight is again an extrapolation below the canonical floor.

avenues.lens: LENS 4 — Is jhāna required for awakening at all? The question is whether the canonical (CST Pāli: nikāya + Vinaya + Abhidhamma + aṭṭhakathā + ṭīkā) record makes the four rūpa-jhānas a NECESSARY condition for any of the four fruits (especially the lower two, stream-entry and once-return), or whether it leaves room for a "dry-insight"/bare-insight (sukkhavipassaka) path. A near-absence is as probative as a hit: if the commentaries must coin upacāra-samādhi and sukkhavipassaka, the canon did not supply them — that silence is the finding.
avenues.avenue: the jhāyatha exhortations and 'jhāna-loss' warnings (jhāyatha mā pamādattha; mā jhānaṃ parihāyi) — Buddha imperatively commands meditation/jhāna
avenues.rationale: Repeated closing exhortation 'There are these roots of trees... meditate, do not be negligent' (jhāyatha, mā pamādattha). The imperative jhāyatha is read by the jhāna camp as a standing command to cultivate jhāna; the dry camp reads jhāyati more broadly as 'meditate/contemplate'.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Frequency and contexts of the imperative — whether jhāyati is always the jhāna-pericope sense or sometimes a looser 'meditate'.
avenues.inference_path: SEMANTIC-RANGE INFERENCE. The force of jhāyatha for the pro-jhāna case depends on jhāyati MEANING attain-jhāna rather than generically meditate/ponder. Check co-occurrence: where jhāyati appears with a contemplation OBJECT (jhāyati X — meditate ON x, e.g. asubha) it is broad; where it stands alone as a state to enter it is technical. Also the homograph trap: jhāyati ('burns/withers', from jhāy(2)) — the 'mā jhānaṃ' loss warnings and the kāmacchanda-as-fire imagery can collide. Distribution of broad vs technical jhāyati tells us whether the exhortation literature actually commands jhāna-attainment or commands meditation-in-general; a predominantly broad usage weakens the imperative's force for strict jhāna-necessity. Orthogonal cross-check: pamāda/appamāda framing — if the contrast partner is always 'negligence' (a motivational, not technical, antonym), jhāyatha is plausibly the broad 'be diligent in meditation' sense.

avenues.lens: LENS 4 — Is jhāna required for awakening at all? The question is whether the canonical (CST Pāli: nikāya + Vinaya + Abhidhamma + aṭṭhakathā + ṭīkā) record makes the four rūpa-jhānas a NECESSARY condition for any of the four fruits (especially the lower two, stream-entry and once-return), or whether it leaves room for a "dry-insight"/bare-insight (sukkhavipassaka) path. The lens reasons from BOTH directions: (a) pro-jhāna loci — sammāsamādhi DEFINED as the four jhānas, the "depending on the first jhāna the destruction of taints" frame, the jhāyatha exhortations, the jhāna→nibbāna path framings; and (b) anti-necessity / dry-path loci — the paññāvimutta arahant who lacks the formless (and arguably the jhānas), the "sixty bhikkhus" liberated-by-wisdom passages, the susīma-style admissions, the gradation that demands only "a measure" of samādhi for the lower fruits, and the commentarial sukkhavipassaka/upacāra-samādhi machinery that fills a canonical gap. Strategy: don't just count jhāna-mentions; mine the QUALIFIER structure (who is said to attain what WITHOUT what), the negative space (fruits attained where jhāna is conspicuously unmentioned), and the commentarial neologisms that betray a felt canonical gap. A near-absence is as probative as a hit: if the commentaries must coin upacāra-samādhi and sukkhavipassaka, the canon did not supply them — that silence is the finding.
avenues.avenue: 'this is the path to jhāna, this to nibbāna' / divergent-paths framings that DISTINGUISH jhāna-mastery from liberation (the anti-necessity wedge: if jhāna and nibbāna are on different roads, jhāna isn't the road to nibbāna)
avenues.rationale: Dhammapada 75 ('one is the path to gain, another the path to nibbāna') and similar two-paths framings are used rhetorically. More importantly, look for any text contrasting a jhāna-leading practice with a nibbāna-leading practice, which would textually separate the two.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Whether the canon ever explicitly says liberation is reached WITHOUT jhāna (annatra jhānā / vināpi jhānena) — a phrase whose presence would settle the dry-path question affirmatively, whose absence is itself decisive.
avenues.inference_path: ABSENCE-AS-PROOF. The phrase 'liberation without jhāna' (vināpi jhānena / aññatra jhānā with nibbāna) would be the smoking gun for the dry path. The prediction is that NO such explicit canonical phrase exists — the canon never says 'one is freed without jhāna'. If the substring search for any vinā/aññatra + jhāna construction tied to nibbāna/āsavakkhaya returns empty in the four nikāyas, that proves the dry path is NOWHERE canonically ASSERTED, only inferred from silences (Susīma, paññāvimutta). The whole dry-path edifice would then rest on argument-from-omission, never on a positive canonical statement — a major finding about the EVIDENTIAL STATUS of the dry path. Conversely the commentary may contain vināpi-jhānena phrasing (Visuddhimagga / ṭīkā on the sukkhavipassaka), again dating the assertion to the commentarial layer.

avenues.avenue: Stream-entry (sotāpatti) attained on-the-spot by lay hearers mid-discourse, with NO meditation/jhāna interval — the dust-free-eye (virajaṃ vītamalaṃ dhammacakkhuṃ) topos
avenues.rationale: The stock conversion scene: the Buddha gives the graduated talk (dāna, sīla, sagga, danger-of-sensuality, renunciation), then the four truths, and 'the dust-free stainless dhamma-eye arose' — stream-entry attained in the seat, no jhāna training shown. This is the dry-path camp's strongest POSITIVE evidence for the LOWER fruits.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: How often the lowest fruit is depicted as arising immediately on hearing, with no concentration prerequisite narrated. Directly answers the lens's special focus on the lower fruits.
avenues.inference_path: ARGUMENT FROM NARRATIVE OMISSION + the 'prepared mind' qualifier. The dhammacakkhu scenes never narrate jhāna BUT they DO narrate a precondition: the mind made 'ready, malleable, free of hindrances, uplifted, confident' (kallacitta, mudu-citta, VINĪVARAṆA-citta) by the graduated talk. The phrase vinīvaraṇacitta ('mind free of the hindrances') is the crux: freedom-from-the-five-hindrances is precisely the doorstep of jhāna. So the canon may be depicting a brief access-like settling WITHOUT naming it jhāna. Inference: the LOWER fruits are canonically shown arising from a hindrance-free but un-named concentration state — which is EXACTLY what the commentary later codifies as upacāra-samādhi. The dhammacakkhu topos therefore furnishes the phenomenological gap the commentary names; the canon supplies the state (vinīvaraṇacitta) without the label. This is the bridge finding linking the lower-fruit evidence to the upacāra question. Co-occurrence of dhammacakkhu with vinīvaraṇacitta vs with any jhāna term is the decisive distribution.

The question is whether the canonical (CST Pāli: nikāya + Vinaya + Abhidhamma + aṭṭhakathā + ṭīkā) record makes the four rūpa-jhānas a NECESSARY condition for any of the four fruits (especially the lower two, stream-entry and once-return), or whether it leaves room for a "dry-insight"/bare-insight (sukkhavipassaka) path. A near-absence is as probative as a hit: if the commentaries must coin upacāra-samādhi and sukkhavipassaka, the canon did not supply them — that silence is the finding.
avenues.avenue: 'samādhismiṃ mattaso kārī' — the gradient text itself (AN 3.86/3.87 etc.): the trainee fulfils sīla and paññā but is only a 'doer to a measure' in samādhi, yet attains the lower fruits
avenues.rationale: AN 3.86 (and 3.87, AN 9.12 Saupādisesa, Puggalapaññatti): the noble trainee fulfils sīla fully but is only a partial-doer (mattaso kārī) in samādhi and paññā — and on that basis is a stream-enterer/once-returner. The non-returner fulfils samādhi; the arahant fulfils all three. THE gradient text of the whole question.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Exact wording of the three-grade structure and which fruits attach to partial vs full samādhi. This is the canonical anchor that grades but does not quantify the measure.
avenues.inference_path: THE CENTRAL INFERENCE OF THE LENS. The canon explicitly says the lower fruits require only a MEASURE (mattaso) of concentration, with FULL concentration (paripūra-kārī) reserved for the non-returner. Two corollaries: (1) jhāna-mastery (full samādhi) is canonically tied to NON-RETURN, NOT to stream-entry — so for the lower two fruits the canon itself denies a full-jhāna requirement. (2) But it never says the 'measure' is ZERO or sub-jhānic; mattaso could mean 'first jhāna but not all four' (Brahmali-style) rather than 'access, no jhāna' (commentary-style). The decisive follow-on is the COMMENTARY on AN 3.86 (Manorathapūraṇī): how does Mp gloss mattaso kārī? If Mp glosses the trainee's partial samādhi as upacāra/sub-jhānic, the commentary reads the dry path INTO mattaso; if Mp glosses it as 'has jhāna but not in full/all eight attainments', the commentary keeps the floor at jhāna. That single gloss is the highest-value commentarial datum in the entire question — fetch and read it. The canon GRADES (mattaso < paripūra) but the QUANTUM is set by the commentary, exactly as the prompt frames.

The question is whether the canonical (CST Pāli: nikāya + Vinaya + Abhidhamma + aṭṭhakathā + ṭīkā) record makes the four rūpa-jhānas a NECESSARY condition for any of the four fruits (especially the lower two, stream-entry and once-return), or whether it leaves room for a "dry-insight"/bare-insight (sukkhavipassaka) path. avenues.avenue: The five hindrances (pañca nīvaraṇā) as what concentration removes — and whether their removal (not jhāna per se) is the real requirement for insight
avenues.rationale: The jhāna pericope opens with 'secluded from sensuality, secluded from unwholesome states' = abandoning the five hindrances. The satipaṭṭhāna/insight pericopes also open by removing covetousness-and-grief. If the OPERATIVE requirement is hindrance-removal (a lower bar than full jhāna), the canon may require only hindrance-suppression, not jhāna-attainment, for insight.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Whether hindrance-abandonment is presented as a distinct, lower threshold reached independently of full jhāna — i.e. whether the canon recognizes a 'hindrances gone but not yet in jhāna' zone.
avenues.inference_path: THRESHOLD-DECOUPLING INFERENCE. If the canon describes states where the hindrances are abandoned but jhāna is NOT explicitly entered (e.g. satipaṭṭhāna practice 'having removed covetousness and grief', or vinīvaraṇacitta in the conversion scenes), it implicitly recognizes a sub-jhānic-but-hindrance-free concentration — which is the phenomenological content of upacāra-samādhi minus the name. The strongest dry-path reading reduces to: insight needs the hindrances gone, and hindrance-removal is achievable below full absorption. The probative search is for hindrance-abandonment formulas that do NOT continue into the jhāna pericope. Their existence and frequency measures how often the canon stops at the threshold without stepping into jhāna — quantifying the canonical room for a sub-jhānic insight state. Orthogonal angle: the simile-set for the abandoned hindrances (debt-freed, disease-recovered, prison-released, slavery-freed, desert-crossed — āṇaṇya, ārogya, bandhanā mokkha, etc.) appears BEFORE the jhāna similes; if joy/serenity (pāmojja→pīti→passaddhi→sukha→samādhi, the upanisā cascade) is the natural-concentration route, that cascade (next avenue) may bypass deliberate jhāna entirely.

avenues.lens: LENS 4 — Is jhāna required for awakening at all? The question is whether the canonical (CST Pāli: nikāya + Vinaya + Abhidhamma + aṭṭhakathā + ṭīkā) record makes the four rūpa-jhānas a NECESSARY condition for any of the four fruits (especially the lower two, stream-entry and once-return), or whether it leaves room for a "dry-insight"/bare-insight (sukkhavipassaka) path. The lens reasons from BOTH directions: (a) pro-jhāna loci — sammāsamādhi DEFINED as the four jhānas, the "depending on the first jhāna the destruction of taints" frame, the jhāyatha exhortations, the jhāna→nibbāna path framings; and (b) anti-necessity / dry-path loci — the paññāvimutta arahant who lacks the formless (and arguably the jhānas), the "sixty bhikkhus" liberated-by-wisdom passages, the susīma-style admissions, the gradation that demands only "a measure" of samādhi for the lower fruits, and the commentarial sukkhavipassaka/upacāra-samādhi machinery that fills a canonical gap. A near-absence is as probative as a hit: if the commentaries must coin upacāra-samādhi and sukkhavipassaka, the canon did not supply them — that silence is the finding.
avenues.avenue: The natural-concentration cascade (pāmojja → pīti → passaddhi → sukha → samādhi) of SN 12.23 (Upanisā) and the satipaṭṭhāna→bojjhaṅga pipeline — samādhi arising as a FRUIT of insight/virtue rather than a deliberate jhāna prerequisite
avenues.rationale: SN 12.23 makes samādhi arise causally FROM serenity FROM joy FROM virtue, and yields yathābhūta-ñāṇadassana — i.e. concentration is a NATURAL byproduct, not a separately-engineered jhāna. The bojjhaṅga sequence (mindfulness→investigation→...→passaddhi→samādhi→upekkhā) likewise has samādhi emerge mid-stream from investigation. This supports a model where adequate concentration is GENERATED by the practice rather than mastered as a precondition.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: That the canon has a causal model where samādhi is an EFFECT of joy/serenity/insight, not only a deliberately-attained jhāna platform — reframing 'how much concentration is required' as 'enough arises naturally'.
avenues.inference_path: CAUSAL-DIRECTION INFERENCE. If samādhi is canonically positioned downstream of pāmojja/passaddhi and upstream of yathābhūta-ñāṇadassana, then the 'measure' of concentration is whatever the joy-serenity cascade produces — not a separately-set jhāna bar. This dissolves the dichotomy: the dry path and the jhāna path may both run through this cascade, differing only in depth. Crucial co-occurrence: does this cascade's samādhi get specified as jhānic (does any version append the jhāna pericope to the 'sukhino cittaṃ samādhiyati' step?) or left unquantified? If the Upanisā/bojjhaṅga samādhi is NEVER pinned to the four-jhāna formula, the canon again grades-without-quantifying — consistent with mattaso kārī. The commentary (Spk on SN 12.23, Vism on the bojjhaṅgas) will quantify it; comparing the commentarial quantification to the canonical silence re-demonstrates the gap-filling pattern. Orthogonal: AN 11.1-5 (kimatthiya / what-purpose chain, sīla→avippaṭisāra→...→vimuttiñāṇadassana) is the same cascade in a virtue key — confirming the model is pervasive, not a one-off.

avenues.lens: LENS 4 — Is jhāna required for awakening at all? The question is whether the canonical (CST Pāli: nikāya + Vinaya + Abhidhamma + aṭṭhakathā + ṭīkā) record makes the four rūpa-jhānas a NECESSARY condition for any of the four fruits (especially the lower two, stream-entry and once-return), or whether it leaves room for a "dry-insight"/bare-insight (sukkhavipassaka) path. A near-absence is as probative as a hit: if the commentaries must coin upacāra-samādhi and sukkhavipassaka, the canon did not supply them — that silence is the finding.
avenues.avenue: MN 64 (Mahāmālukya) — abandoning the five lower fetters using a jhāna (up to nothingness) as the insight base; read by the jhāna camp as requiring at least first jhāna for non-return, and the 'cannot WITHOUT depending on' clause
avenues.rationale: MN 64: Ānanda asks the path to abandoning the five lower fetters; the Buddha answers via the jhānas (and formless up to nothingness) as insight bases, and frames it as 'there is no possibility... WITHOUT depending on' (the 'na tam thanam vijjati' modal). The modal-possibility clause is the jhāna camp's strongest grammatical lever — the canon says the path CANNOT be otherwise.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The exact modal framing ('it is impossible to... without this path') and whether it makes jhāna a stated necessary condition for cutting the lower fetters (i.e. for once-return/non-return).
avenues.inference_path: MODAL-NECESSITY INFERENCE — the rare place the canon uses necessity language. If MN 64 says 'na taṃ ṭhānaṃ vijjati' (there is no possibility) that the fetters are cut WITHOUT this jhāna-based path, that is the closest the canon comes to an EXPLICIT jhāna-requirement. The dry camp's counter: the modal attaches to 'this path' broadly (the gradual training), not to jhāna specifically, OR the passage concerns the HIGHER fetters/fruits, not stream-entry. The decisive checks: (1) does the necessity clause grammatically govern the jhāna clause or the whole maggo? (2) which fruit is at stake — five LOWER fetters = the work of once-returner→non-returner, NOT stream-entry, so even a strong reading here leaves stream-entry's concentration-floor untouched. This sharpens the lens: the strongest jhāna-necessity text targets the MIDDLE fruits, leaving the LOWEST fruit (the prompt's special focus) the LEAST concentration-constrained — a gradient internal to the necessity evidence itself. Compare MN 52/AN 9.36 which it parallels.

avenues.lens: LENS 4 — Is jhāna required for awakening at all? The question is whether the canonical (CST Pāli: nikāya + Vinaya + Abhidhamma + aṭṭhakathā + ṭīkā) record makes the four rūpa-jhānas a NECESSARY condition for any of the four fruits (especially the lower two, stream-entry and once-return), or whether it leaves room for a "dry-insight"/bare-insight (sukkhavipassaka) path. A near-absence is as probative as a hit: if the commentaries must coin upacāra-samādhi and sukkhavipassaka, the canon did not supply them — that silence is the finding.
avenues.avenue: Arahants/noble ones explicitly attaining the formless or full mastery — the CONTRAST class establishing that full attainment is a SUPERSET, not the baseline (ubhatobhāgavimutta, the nine progressive abidings, cessation)
avenues.rationale: The both-ways-liberated arahant and the nine progressive abidings up through cessation define the TOP of the attainment ladder. Establishing this ceiling clarifies that the contested question is the FLOOR — and that the canon's heroes span a range from sukkhavipassaka(?) to cessation-attainers, all called arahant.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The full range of named attainment-grades among the liberated, situating the dry-path question as a debate about the lower bound of a recognized spectrum.
avenues.inference_path: SUPERSET/RANGE INFERENCE. If arahantship is canonically attached to BOTH the cessation-attainer AND (via paññāvimutta) the one lacking the formless, the category 'arahant' spans a wide concentration range — proving samādhi-depth is NOT criterial for the FRUIT, only for the TYPE of liberated person. This directly supports the thesis that concentration grades the practitioner, not the fruit. The remaining question is solely how far DOWN the range extends (does it reach below first jhāna?). Orthogonal probe: cetovimutti vs paññāvimutti — if 'liberation of mind' (ceto, the samādhi-side) and 'liberation by wisdom' (paññā) are paired but separable, and arahants can be paññāvimutta WITHOUT the akuppā cetovimutti that the jhānas/formless represent, the canon structurally allows wisdom-liberation with deficient mind-liberation. The akuppā ('unshakeable') qualifier is key: search whether akuppā cetovimutti is ever REQUIRED of all arahants or only predicated of the both-ways type.

avenues.lens: LENS 4 — Is jhāna required for awakening at all? The question is whether the canonical (CST Pāli: nikāya + Vinaya + Abhidhamma + aṭṭhakathā + ṭīkā) record makes the four rūpa-jhānas a NECESSARY condition for any of the four fruits (especially the lower two, stream-entry and once-return), or whether it leaves room for a "dry-insight"/bare-insight (sukkhavipassaka) path.
