The whole evidential value here is that the canon gives a gradient but no quantum; the commentary is the layer that DOES name a quantum, so every commentarial gloss on the measure-phrase, on the sotāpanna/sakadāgāmī's concentration, and on the four named sub-jhānic concentrations is direct evidence of how the tradition resolved the underdetermination — and a near-absence (e.g. if upacāra never co-occurs with the trainee-grading pericope) is itself a finding that the "access concentration" floor is a systematic-theology import rather than an exegesis of the specific canonical phrase.
avenues.avenue: Nīvaraṇa-suppression as the functional floor — what state the commentary says is the minimum to hold the five hindrances down during insight, independent of jhāna-naming
avenues.rationale: Beneath the labels (upacāra/khaṇika), the real question is FUNCTIONAL: how much mental quieting does the tradition say insight requires? The commentary's analysis of hindrance-suppression (vikkhambhana-pahāna = the jhāna/upacāra mode vs. tadaṅga-pahāna = the insight mode) is the mechanism-level answer to 'how much concentration', bypassing the label dispute.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: If the commentary assigns hindrance-suppression to upacāra/jhāna (vikkhambhana) and only momentary-replacement (tadaṅga) to insight, it reveals two different concentration-grades doing different jobs — and whether bare insight needs the deeper one.
avenues.inference_path: If insight's mode is tadaṅga-pahāna (momentary substitution) and that is explicitly said NOT to require absorption, the commentary has authorised a genuinely shallow floor. But if the texts insist the hindrances must FIRST be suppressed by vikkhambhana (jhāna/upacāra) before insight can run, the floor is access-or-above even for the dry worker. This mechanism-level reading is orthogonal to the label search and can confirm or contradict it — a cross-check on whether khaṇika really substitutes for upacāra.

avenues.lens: LENS 10 — How the commentary (aṭṭhakathā/ṭīkā) itself fills the canonical gap: the gloss targets where the post-canonical layer specifies what the trainee's "limited measure" of concentration (samādhismiṃ mattaso kārī) actually IS, and whether it supplies a sub-jhānic floor (upacāra/khaṇika/vipassanā/magga/phala samādhi) for the lower-path attainer. The whole evidential value here is that the canon gives a gradient but no quantum; the commentary is the layer that DOES name a quantum, so every commentarial gloss on the measure-phrase, on the sotāpanna/sakadāgāmī's concentration, and on the four named sub-jhānic concentrations is direct evidence of how the tradition resolved the underdetermination — and a near-absence (e.g. if upacāra never co-occurs with the trainee-grading pericope) is itself a finding that the "access concentration" floor is a systematic-theology import rather than an exegesis of the specific canonical phrase.
avenues.avenue: Abhidhamma/Vism jhāna-factor (jhānaṅga) reduction — the doctrine that even a single moment of wholesome consciousness has the five jhāna-factors, used to argue all attainers have 'jhāna' trivially
avenues.rationale: The Abhidhamma analysis that ekaggatā (one-pointedness) is a universal mental factor (sabbacitta-sādhāraṇa) present in every citta lets the commentary claim a baseline 'jhāna-factor' concentration in any attainer. This is the maximally deflationary reading of the canon's 'measure': the measure is just the ekaggatā present in any wholesome path-moment.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Texts treating ekaggatā as ever-present and the jhāna-factors as gradable show the tradition had a way to call even minimal concentration 'samādhi' — collapsing the floor toward zero terminologically.
avenues.inference_path: If the commentary leans on universal-ekaggatā to satisfy the samādhi-indriya/bala for every attainer, then the canon's 'measure' is being read as 'whatever ekaggatā the path-citta carries' — a near-zero floor that vindicates the dry reading at the Abhidhamma level. But the same move proves too much (every wholesome thought has it), so its USE specifically for the path-faculty is the tell. Co-occurrence of ekaggatā-as-universal with samādhindriya-fulfilment in the sotāpanna context is the inference target; its absence means the tradition demanded MORE than baseline ekaggatā, i.e. a real floor.

avenues.lens: LENS 10 — How the commentary (aṭṭhakathā/ṭīkā) itself fills the canonical gap: the gloss targets where the post-canonical layer specifies what the trainee's "limited measure" of concentration (samādhismiṃ mattaso kārī) actually IS, and whether it supplies a sub-jhānic floor (upacāra/khaṇika/vipassanā/magga/phala samādhi) for the lower-path attainer. The whole evidential value here is that the canon gives a gradient but no quantum; the commentary is the layer that DOES name a quantum, so every commentarial gloss on the measure-phrase, on the sotāpanna/sakadāgāmī's concentration, and on the four named sub-jhānic concentrations is direct evidence of how the tradition resolved the underdetermination — and a near-absence (e.g. if upacāra never co-occurs with the trainee-grading pericope) is itself a finding that the "access concentration" floor is a systematic-theology import rather than an exegesis of the specific canonical phrase.
avenues.avenue: The samādhi-indriya / samādhi-bala fulfilment grading for the sotāpanna and the 'completed-in-X, partial-in-Y' faculty-maturity scheme (Paṭisambhidāmagga + Vism on indriya)
avenues.rationale: The faculties (indriya) are graded to maturity in the commentary, and the sotāpanna/sakadāgāmī are placed at specific maturity levels. Since the canon's grading phrase is about the samādhi faculty specifically being 'done to a measure', the commentarial indriya-maturity scheme is the systematic home of the answer.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Where the commentary states the maturity of the samādhi-faculty in the sotāpanna (e.g. 'faculties not yet completed' vs. the arahant's completed faculties) it quantifies the gradient the canon only gestured at.
avenues.inference_path: If the indriya-scheme places the sotāpanna's samādhi-faculty as immature/partial and only the arahant (or anāgāmī) as completed, it reproduces the canon's gradient WITHOUT adding a jhāna-floor — meaning the commentary kept the answer relational (less than the higher attainer) rather than absolute (so-many jhānas). That relational-only resolution would explain why no fixed quantum was ever canonised. Conversely a fixed maturity-to-jhāna mapping (e.g. sotāpanna = first jhāna faculty) would be the quantification the question seeks.

avenues.lens: LENS 10 — How the commentary (aṭṭhakathā/ṭīkā) itself fills the canonical gap: the gloss targets where the post-canonical layer specifies what the trainee's "limited measure" of concentration (samādhismiṃ mattaso kārī) actually IS, and whether it supplies a sub-jhānic floor (upacāra/khaṇika/vipassanā/magga/phala samādhi) for the lower-path attainer. The whole evidential value here is that the canon gives a gradient but no quantum; the commentary is the layer that DOES name a quantum, so every commentarial gloss on the measure-phrase, on the sotāpanna/sakadāgāmī's concentration, and on the four named sub-jhānic concentrations is direct evidence of how the tradition resolved the underdetermination — and a near-absence (e.g. if upacāra never co-occurs with the trainee-grading pericope) is itself a finding that the "access concentration" floor is a systematic-theology import rather than an exegesis of the specific canonical phrase.
avenues.avenue: Susīmasutta (SN 12.70) commentary on paññāvimutta arahants who deny the formless liberations — the canon's hardest dry-arahant case and how SN-a defuses it
avenues.rationale: SN 12.70 has arahants who say they are paññāvimutta and have NOT attained the formless liberations — the strongest canonical hook for a jhāna-light path. The Sāratthappakāsinī (SN-a) is forced to gloss whether these arahants had the four (form) jhānas but not the formless, or no jhāna at all. This single commentarial decision is among the most-cited in the modern debate (Gombrich, Bodhi, Brahmali).
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The SN-a reading — 'they lacked only the formless, but had the four jhānas' vs. 'they were sukkhavipassakas with no jhāna' — directly sets the commentarial verdict on the dry-arahant question.
avenues.inference_path: If SN-a reads them as sukkhavipassaka (no jhāna), the commentary officially endorses a fully dry arahant — and a fortiori a dry sotāpanna, lowering the floor decisively. If SN-a insists they had the four rūpa-jhānas (denying only the formless), the canon's apparent dry-arahant is recommentarised UP to jhāna, and the dry-path reading loses its strongest canonical anchor in the tradition's own eyes. This is the highest-leverage single locus in the entire lens.

avenues.lens: LENS 10 — How the commentary (aṭṭhakathā/ṭīkā) itself fills the canonical gap: the gloss targets where the post-canonical layer specifies what the trainee's "limited measure" of concentration (samādhismiṃ mattaso kārī) actually IS, and whether it supplies a sub-jhānic floor (upacāra/khaṇika/vipassanā/magga/phala samādhi) for the lower-path attainer. The whole evidential value here is that the canon gives a gradient but no quantum; the commentary is the layer that DOES name a quantum, so every commentarial gloss on the measure-phrase, on the sotāpanna/sakadāgāmī's concentration, and on the four named sub-jhānic concentrations is direct evidence of how the tradition resolved the underdetermination — and a near-absence (e.g. if upacāra never co-occurs with the trainee-grading pericope) is itself a finding that the "access concentration" floor is a systematic-theology import rather than an exegesis of the specific canonical phrase.
avenues.avenue: Orthogonal: the gradual-training / ānupubbī-sikkhā commentary and the jhāna-as-stage placement — does the commentary make jhāna an invariant station before the paths, or an optional one?
avenues.rationale: The threefold training (sīla→samādhi→paññā) and the gradual-path schema embed concentration as the MIDDLE training. How the commentary describes the samādhi-khandha's content within ānupubbī-sikkhā (jhāna only? or jhāna-or-upacāra?) reveals whether the system treats jhāna as a mandatory station. This is structurally adjacent to but distinct from the grading-phrase: it tests the ARCHITECTURE rather than the label.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: If the samādhi-training is glossed as 'the eight attainments' or 'the four jhānas' without a sub-jhānic alternative, the architecture demands jhāna; if it admits upacāra as fulfilling adhicitta-sikkhā, the architecture itself was opened to a lower floor.
avenues.inference_path: A commentary that defines adhicitta-sikkhā strictly as the jhānas, yet ELSEWHERE grants sotāpatti to a sukkhavipassaka, exposes an internal inconsistency between the architectural ideal and the soteriological concession — which is precisely the seam the modern debate exploits. Mapping whether the commentary ever explicitly says the trainee can be 'incomplete in adhicitta-sikkhā yet a sotāpanna' ties the gradual-path frame back to the AN grading phrase and could supply the quantification by the back door (incomplete adhicitta = below the jhāna-stage).

avenues.lens: LENS 10 — How the commentary (aṭṭhakathā/ṭīkā) itself fills the canonical gap: the gloss targets where the post-canonical layer specifies what the trainee's "limited measure" of concentration (samādhismiṃ mattaso kārī) actually IS, and whether it supplies a sub-jhānic floor (upacāra/khaṇika/vipassanā/magga/phala samādhi) for the lower-path attainer. The whole evidential value here is that the canon gives a gradient but no quantum; the commentary is the layer that DOES name a quantum, so every commentarial gloss on the measure-phrase, on the sotāpanna/sakadāgāmī's concentration, and on the four named sub-jhānic concentrations is direct evidence of how the tradition resolved the underdetermination — and a near-absence (e.g. if upacāra never co-occurs with the trainee-grading pericope) is itself a finding that the "access concentration" floor is a systematic-theology import rather than an exegesis of the specific canonical phrase.
avenues.avenue: Orthogonal: cetovimutti vs. paññāvimutti and the 'akuppā cetovimutti' — concentration-named liberation terms and whether the lower fruits are ever associated with cetovimutti
avenues.rationale: Cetovimutti is the concentration-side liberation, paired with paññāvimutti. The terms' commentarial mapping (which liberation goes with which attainer, and whether the lower fruits get any cetovimutti language) is an oblique probe of how much concentration the tradition attaches to the lower path. 'Akuppā cetovimutti' is even glossed as arahatta-phala — a concentration-named term for the top fruit, suggesting the system uses samādhi-vocabulary for attainment-strength generally.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: If cetovimutti-language is reserved for jhāna-attainers and arahants while the sotāpanna is described purely in paññā/dassana terms, that asymmetry shows the tradition did NOT load the lower fruits with concentration-requirements verbally.
avenues.inference_path: A near-absence of cetovimutti/samādhi-attainment language around the sotāpanna (who is glossed via dassana, 'seeing', and diṭṭhi-sampanna) would be indirect evidence that the lower fruits were conceived as insight-events whose concentration-floor was left deliberately light — the canon and commentary describing them by what is SEEN, not by what is absorbed. This 'argument from descriptive vocabulary' is orthogonal to the explicit-measure search and could corroborate a low floor even where the measure-phrase itself is glossed sparsely. Conversely 'ariyo/anantarika samadhi' appearing for the path-moment would re-link concentration to the lower fruits.

avenues.lens: LENS 10 — How the commentary (aṭṭhakathā/ṭīkā) itself fills the canonical gap: the gloss targets where the post-canonical layer specifies what the trainee's "limited measure" of concentration (samādhismiṃ mattaso kārī) actually IS, and whether it supplies a sub-jhānic floor (upacāra/khaṇika/vipassanā/magga/phala samādhi) for the lower-path attainer. The whole evidential value here is that the canon gives a gradient but no quantum; the commentary is the layer that DOES name a quantum, so every commentarial gloss on the measure-phrase, on the sotāpanna/sakadāgāmī's concentration, and on the four named sub-jhānic concentrations is direct evidence of how the tradition resolved the underdetermination — and a near-absence (e.g. if upacāra never co-occurs with the trainee-grading pericope) is itself a finding that the "access concentration" floor is a systematic-theology import rather than an exegesis of the specific canonical phrase.
avenues.avenue: Orthogonal: the simile-bank the commentary uses for the trainee's partial concentration — fraction/partial similes (half, portion, a little water, kahāpaṇa-fractions) that quantify 'a measure' figuratively
avenues.rationale: Commentators routinely quantify vague canonical degrees with fraction-similes (e.g. 'sixteen parts', kalā-fractions, koṭṭhāsa-portions). If 'mattaso'/'padesa-kārī' is unpacked with a portion-simile for concentration specifically, the figurative fraction IS the tradition's attempt at a quantum where it refused a literal one.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: A simile assigning the trainee, say, a fixed fraction of the full samādhi (or 'as much as suffices for insight') converts the canon's qualitative gradient into a semi-quantitative commentarial estimate.
avenues.inference_path: Even a vague fraction-simile ('a portion', 'a little') is evidence that the tradition conceived the floor as RELATIVE and small but non-zero — neither full jhāna nor literally nothing — which is exactly the middle position the modern debate keeps missing. If the commentary uses 'as much as is the basis for insight' (vipassanāpādaka-matta) language, that operationalises the floor as instrumental ('enough to support insight') rather than as a named attainment — arguably the most accurate reading of the canon and a finding in itself. The presence of 'vipassanapadaka'/'padaka-jhana' terms is the specific co-occurrence to watch.

avenues.lens: LENS 10 — How the commentary (aṭṭhakathā/ṭīkā) itself fills the canonical gap: the gloss targets where the post-canonical layer specifies what the trainee's "limited measure" of concentration (samādhismiṃ mattaso kārī) actually IS, and whether it supplies a sub-jhānic floor (upacāra/khaṇika/vipassanā/magga/phala samādhi) for the lower-path attainer. The whole evidential value here is that the canon gives a gradient but no quantum; the commentary is the layer that DOES name a quantum, so every commentarial gloss on the measure-phrase, on the sotāpanna/sakadāgāmī's concentration, and on the four named sub-jhānic concentrations is direct evidence of how the tradition resolved the underdetermination — and a near-absence (e.g. if upacāra never co-occurs with the trainee-grading pericope) is itself a finding that the "access concentration" floor is a systematic-theology import rather than an exegesis of the specific canonical phrase.
avenues.avenue: Pādaka-jhāna (basis-jhāna) doctrine — the commentarial requirement (or not) of a 'basis' attainment from which to emerge before contemplating, and whether dry workers are exempted
avenues.rationale: The commentary often requires emerging from a 'basis-jhāna' (pādaka-jjhāna) to do insight on its factors. Whether this is universalised or explicitly waived for the sukkhavipassaka is the precise place the floor is set high or low in practice-instructions.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: If pādaka-jhāna is required for the samatha-vehicle but the sukkhavipassaka is named as proceeding without one, the commentary has drawn an explicit two-floor map: jhāna for one vehicle, sub-jhāna for the other.
avenues.inference_path: The pādaka-jhāna passages are where the commentary is most operationally honest about the floor. If they consistently exempt the dry worker (insight on the bare khandhas without emerging from any attainment), the sub-jhānic floor is endorsed at the level of actual method, not just classification. If pādaka-jhāna is required even for insight-objects in all cases discussed, the practical floor is jhāna regardless of the person-type labels — meaning the 'sukkhavipassaka' is a classificatory nicety without an operational sub-jhānic method. This tests whether the dry path is a real method or only a category.

avenues.lens: probe size B
avenues.avenue: Gradient + access-lexicon (AN3.86/3.87,AN9.12,Pp)
avenues.rationale: Canon's only quantification vs the 0-canon parikamma/upacara/appana triad.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Whether mattaso-kari pairs with jhana in mula; stratum where upacara/appana/khanika enter.
avenues.inference_path: Mattaso-kari without 4-jhana pericope + Atthakatha-upacara = sub-jhanic floor commentarial; khanikasamadhi 0-canon = dry-insight vocabulary commentarial too; lokuttara-jhana = mundane/supramundane split absent in canon.

avenues.lens: probe size B
avenues.avenue: Per-scholar loci as terms (Cousins/Gombrich/Brahmali-Sujato/Shankman/Polak/Arbel/Wynne/Bodhi-Analayo)
avenues.rationale: Each scholar's cited loci as substring terms; full mapping in lens.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Samatha-without-insight persons; SN12.70 formless-only; MN117 jhana-less samma-samadhi; AN9.36 insight-in-jhana; MN26 formless-demotion; SN12.23 samadhi-without-depth.
avenues.inference_path: AN4.94=low-samadhi entry; pannavimutti-without-formless=top fruit <8, lower a fortiori; MN117 lets lower-fruit skip jhana; AN9.36=sutta-jhana isnt appana; MN26=depth separated from liberation; SN12.23=the gradient-without-a-measure.

avenues.lens: probe size B
avenues.avenue: Decisive negatives + person-taxonomy (MN70/SN25/SN48/SN55.5)
avenues.rationale: Conversion pericope+sotapattiyanga no-jhana; MN70 wisdom/faith lane; SN48 faculty-balance; fetter/aveccappasada definitions; jhayi-puthujjana->Brahma.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Whether fruit-definitions carry a concentration term; whether jhana/formless sits ONLY on kayasakkhi/ubhatobhagavimutta; whether SN48 sets a per-fruit threshold.
avenues.inference_path: Quadruple dissociation (jhana-without-fruit + fruit-without-jhana + wisdom/faith lane + no threshold) = lower fruits reach without deep concentration; measure unspecified-because-not-constitutive, the gap the commentary fills.

avenues.lens: LENS 12 — the moment of attainment itself: the breakthrough (abhisamaya), change-of-lineage (gotrabhu), the magga-/phala-citta as a momentary event, the dust-free eye arising (dhammacakkhu udapadi), and sudden/in-sentence awakenings (Bahiya). The strategy: the canon describes the breakthrough as instantaneous and akāliko, while the Abhidhamma/commentary build a precise cognitive-series (cittavīthi) around it that SPECIFIES the concentration state immediately preceding magga. The depth-of-concentration question can be inferred three ways here: (1) what jhāna-level the magga-citta itself is said to be 'counted as' (the magga is always jhānic in Abhidhamma — every path-moment has jhāna-factors and is reckoned a jhāna — which is a commentarial answer to 'how much concentration at the breakthrough', and its CANONICAL silence is itself evidence); (2) what immediately precedes the breakthrough in the narrative frames (a gradual-talk ending in 'pliant/ready mind' vs. a deep-jhāna emergence vs. a bare hearing-while-standing) — the diversity of antecedent states bears directly on the floor; (3) the speed/suddenness language (khaṇa, instantly, mid-stanza) which, if depth required slow absorption, should be impossible — so instantaneous breakthroughs are prima facie evidence AGAINST a deep-jhāna prerequisite, UNLESS the commentary inserts a hidden jhānic moment, which is exactly the upacāra/appanā move. The lens is uniquely powerful because the Abhidhamma is FORCED to assign a concentration value to the path-moment, so a near-absence of any such assignment in the canon is a sharp signal.
avenues.avenue: Abhisamaya — the breakthrough/penetration event itself, and whether it is depicted as requiring a prior absorption or as a discrete cognitive flash
avenues.rationale: Abhisamaya is the canonical word for the realization/penetration of the truths. Its grammatical aspect (often aorist/momentary) and its narrative neighbours tell you whether the breakthrough is framed as a culminating absorption or an instantaneous cognition. saccabhisamaya / dhammabhisamaya specifically tie it to the four truths.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Whether abhisamaya is ever syntactically yoked to jhāna-emergence (e.g. 'having emerged from the fourth jhāna, the penetration occurred') vs. yoked to hearing/reflection. A co-occurrence with jhāna terms within the same passage would support a deep-concentration floor; co-occurrence with savana (hearing) / yoniso manasikāra supports a low floor.
avenues.inference_path: If abhisamaya overwhelmingly appears next to discursive antecedents (hearing a graduated talk, reflecting) and almost never next to 'vutthahitvā jhānā' (emerging from jhāna), that near-absence is strong evidence the canon does not gate the breakthrough behind deep absorption. The Abhidhamma's later insistence that the abhisamaya-moment is jhānic then reads as a systematizing overlay, not a canonical requirement.

avenues.lens: LENS 12 — the moment of attainment itself: the breakthrough (abhisamaya), change-of-lineage (gotrabhu), the magga-/phala-citta as a momentary event, the dust-free eye arising (dhammacakkhu udapadi), and sudden/in-sentence awakenings (Bahiya). The strategy: the canon describes the breakthrough as instantaneous and akāliko, while the Abhidhamma/commentary build a precise cognitive-series (cittavīthi) around it that SPECIFIES the concentration state immediately preceding magga. The depth-of-concentration question can be inferred three ways here: (1) what jhāna-level the magga-citta itself is said to be 'counted as' (the magga is always jhānic in Abhidhamma — every path-moment has jhāna-factors and is reckoned a jhāna — which is a commentarial answer to 'how much concentration at the breakthrough', and its CANONICAL silence is itself evidence); (2) what immediately precedes the breakthrough in the narrative frames (a gradual-talk ending in 'pliant/ready mind' vs. a deep-jhāna emergence vs. a bare hearing-while-standing) — the diversity of antecedent states bears directly on the floor; (3) the speed/suddenness language (khaṇa, instantly, mid-stanza) which, if depth required slow absorption, should be impossible — so instantaneous breakthroughs are prima facie evidence AGAINST a deep-jhāna prerequisite, UNLESS the commentary inserts a hidden jhānic moment, which is exactly the upacāra/appanā move. The lens is uniquely powerful because the Abhidhamma is FORCED to assign a concentration value to the path-moment, so a near-absence of any such assignment in the canon is a sharp signal.
avenues.avenue: Dhammacakkhu — the dust-free, stainless Dhamma-eye arising; the stock stream-entry formula and its trigger context
avenues.rationale: The single most common stream-entry attainment marker. The formula 'virajaṃ vītamalaṃ dhammacakkhuṃ udapādi' closes hundreds of discourses, almost always immediately after a graduated discourse (anupubbikathā) — NOT after a meditation retreat. The content realized is bare conditionality ('whatever is subject to arising is subject to cessation'), an insight proposition, not an absorption.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The antecedent verb-context: what the listener was DOING when the eye arose (sitting and listening, asane nisinnassa). The total absence of any jhāna-attainment in these frames is the central datum. Also whether the eye 'arises' (udapādi, momentary) vs. 'is developed' (bhāveti, gradual).
avenues.inference_path: Hundreds of stream-entries occur 'while seated on the spot, listening' with zero mention of jhāna. If the canon believed jhāna were prerequisite to the dhammacakkhu, this is precisely where it would say so — and it does not. The near-total absence of jhāna-language in the dhammacakkhu frame is the strongest single canonical argument for a low concentration floor for stream-entry. Conversely, any frame that DOES insert jhāna before the eye would be a high-value counterexample to enumerate.

avenues.lens: LENS 12 — the moment of attainment itself: the breakthrough (abhisamaya), change-of-lineage (gotrabhu), the magga-/phala-citta as a momentary event, the dust-free eye arising (dhammacakkhu udapadi), and sudden/in-sentence awakenings (Bahiya). The strategy: the canon describes the breakthrough as instantaneous and akāliko, while the Abhidhamma/commentary build a precise cognitive-series (cittavīthi) around it that SPECIFIES the concentration state immediately preceding magga. The depth-of-concentration question can be inferred three ways here: (1) what jhāna-level the magga-citta itself is said to be 'counted as' (the magga is always jhānic in Abhidhamma — every path-moment has jhāna-factors and is reckoned a jhāna — which is a commentarial answer to 'how much concentration at the breakthrough', and its CANONICAL silence is itself evidence); (2) what immediately precedes the breakthrough in the narrative frames (a gradual-talk ending in 'pliant/ready mind' vs. a deep-jhāna emergence vs. a bare hearing-while-standing) — the diversity of antecedent states bears directly on the floor; (3) the speed/suddenness language (khaṇa, instantly, mid-stanza) which, if depth required slow absorption, should be impossible — so instantaneous breakthroughs are prima facie evidence AGAINST a deep-jhāna prerequisite, UNLESS the commentary inserts a hidden jhānic moment, which is exactly the upacāra/appanā move. The lens is uniquely powerful because the Abhidhamma is FORCED to assign a concentration value to the path-moment, so a near-absence of any such assignment in the canon is a sharp signal.
avenues.avenue: Gotrabhū — the change-of-lineage moment; the citta that immediately precedes magga
avenues.rationale: Gotrabhū is the technical name for the single mind-moment between ordinary (kāmāvacara) consciousness and the supramundane magga-citta. In the Paṭisambhidāmagga and commentary it sits in the cittavīthi right after anuloma and right before magga. Crucially, gotrabhū appears ONCE in the canon proper (Aṅguttara, the gotrabhū-puggala) with a very different, non-cittavīthi sense — so the term itself is a canon-vs-commentary fault line.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Whether gotrabhū in canonical text carries any concentration specification, vs. the Paṭisambhidāmagga/Abhidhamma where it is embedded in a fixed parikamma-upacāra-anuloma-gotrabhū-magga series whose jhānic status is explicitly debated.
avenues.inference_path: If gotrabhū-as-cittavīthi-moment is 0 in the four nikāyas and appears only in Paṭis + Abhidhamma + commentary, then the entire machinery that specifies the pre-magga concentration state is post-canonical. That localizes the 'how much concentration at the breakthrough' answer to exactly the stratum (commentary) the question suspects of inventing it. The single canonical gotrabhū (the 'lineage' person, AN) then has to be read WITHOUT the moment-theory, which removes a concentration requirement the commentary later supplies.

avenues.avenue: Magga-citta / phala-citta as momentary supramundane jhāna — the Abhidhamma assignment of a jhāna-number to the path-moment
avenues.rationale: In the Dhammasaṅgaṇī and its commentary, EVERY path-citta is classified under a jhāna (the first path is 'pathamajjhānika' etc.), and carries the five jhāna-factors including ekaggatā (one-pointedness). This is the Abhidhamma's literal answer to 'what concentration is present at the breakthrough': it is definitionally at least first-jhāna-grade because the path-moment is reckoned a jhāna by factor-count.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Whether the path-moment is given a concentration grade ONLY in Abhidhamma/commentary, and whether that grade is 'at least first jhāna' (the lokuttara-jhāna doctrine). Also surfaces the 'jhāna by factor-presence' vs 'jhāna by absorptive depth' equivocation — the path-moment has jhāna-FACTORS without necessarily having undergone absorptive jhāna-DEVELOPMENT.
avenues.inference_path: This is the lens's pivot. The Abhidhamma forces a jhāna-value onto the breakthrough. If that value is derived purely from factor-presence (every wholesome citta has ekaggatā; concentration-as-faculty ≠ concentration-as-attainment), then the 'jhāna at the path-moment' is a TECHNICAL jhāna (one-pointedness present) not an EXPERIENTIAL jhāna (prior absorption attained). That distinction dissolves the apparent commentarial requirement: the dry-insight worker can have a path-moment that 'counts as first jhāna' by factor-presence without ever having developed mundane jhāna. Enumerating where the texts equivocate between these two senses of jhāna at the path-moment is the highest-value find in this lens.

avenues.avenue: Suddenness / instantaneity language around awakening — khaṇa, mid-sentence, while-standing
avenues.rationale: If the breakthrough were gated behind a slow absorption, it could not occur 'on the spot' or 'while still standing' or 'immediately'. Bāhiya (Ud 1.10) awakens standing in the street after a few sentences; the dhammacakkhu 'arises' (aorist, momentary). Instantaneity language is structural evidence about how much pre-loading concentration the breakthrough can tolerate.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: How many breakthroughs are time-marked as immediate/standing/mid-talk, and whether ANY such case also records a jhāna-attainment in the same scene. Samanantarā / anantara is also the Abhidhamma proximity-condition term (magga is anantara-paccaya to phala) — a canon/commentary bridge.
avenues.inference_path: A high count of immediate/standing/mid-sentence awakenings with no jhāna in scene argues that the breakthrough's concentration requirement is whatever can be present in an alert, composed, listening mind — i.e. access-grade composure at most, not deep absorption. The commentary's response (a momentary jhāna inserted in the cittavīthi that the narrative doesn't see) is unfalsifiable from the narrative side; flagging that the canon's time-language leaves no ROOM for a developed absorption is the inference. If even one standing/mid-sentence case explicitly records prior jhāna, that bounds the claim.

avenues.avenue: Bāhiya / Mālukyaputta — bare-sense-restraint awakening with explicitly NO jhāna pericope
avenues.rationale: Bāhiya (Ud 1.10) and the Mālukyaputta instruction (SN 35.95) give a 'in the seen only the seen' formula that produces awakening with no jhāna scaffolding mentioned at all — the locus classicus for the bare-insight reading. The 'mattaṃ' (mere/only) construction is the searchable fingerprint.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The full set of passages using the diṭṭhe-diṭṭhamattaṃ template and whether any version embeds it in a jhāna sequence. Whether the awakening is immediate ('tato tvaṃ Bāhiya na ...').
avenues.inference_path: Bāhiya is the canonical hard case for the dry-insight side. If the 'mattaṃ' formula recurs and never co-occurs with jhāna-attainment, it shows the canon preserved at least one awakening template that runs entirely on sense-restraint + present-moment bare cognition. Even commentators must concede the narrative jhāna-silence and resort to 'he had jhāna in a past life / a hidden moment' — locating the jhāna-requirement entirely in inference, not text. The presence/absence of any such commentarial gloss IN the corpus is itself a finding.

avenues.avenue: Anupubbikathā → the 'ready/pliant/elated/clear' mind formula that PRECEDES the dhammacakkhu
avenues.rationale: Right before nearly every dhammacakkhu, the text says the listener's mind became 'ready, pliant, free of hindrances, uplifted, clear' (kallacitta muducitta vinīvaraṇacitta udaggacitta pasannacitta). This is the canon's OWN description of the mental state at the threshold of the breakthrough — and it is a hindrance-free, pliant clarity, exactly what the commentary would call upacāra-samādhi but the canon does NOT call jhāna.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The exact stock vocabulary of the pre-breakthrough mind. Critically: 'vinīvaraṇacitta' = hindrances temporarily suppressed = the functional definition of access concentration WITHOUT the word jhāna or the word upacāra.
avenues.inference_path: This is possibly the single most important avenue. The canon ALREADY specifies the threshold mental state, and it is 'hindrance-free pliant clarity', not 'emerged from the fourth jhāna'. This means (a) the breakthrough requires MORE than scattered ordinary mind (so pure no-concentration 'dry' insight is also under-supported), but (b) LESS than full absorption (no jhāna word appears). The canonical floor for stream-entry, read off the pre-dhammacakkhu formula, is therefore a hindrance-suppressed composed mind — which is precisely the experiential content the commentary later names upacāra-samādhi. The finding: the commentary's access-concentration is a NAMING of a real canonical state, even though the term upacāra is 0 in the canon. That reframes the whole debate: the floor is canonically attested; only the label is commentarial.

avenues.avenue: Phala-samāpatti and the post-attainment absorption — distinguishing the breakthrough-moment from the dwelling-in-fruit
avenues.rationale: Phala-samāpatti (attainment of fruition) is a SUSTAINED absorption an ariya can re-enter; it is jhānic. It must be carefully separated from the momentary phala-citta at first breakthrough. Conflating the two inflates the concentration estimate for the breakthrough. The vuṭṭhāna (emergence) language marks absorptions one emerges FROM — its presence or absence around the first attainment is diagnostic.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Whether the texts reserve absorption/samāpatti/emergence language for the REPEATABLE dwelling of an established ariya vs. the FIRST breakthrough. If samāpatti+vuṭṭhāna cluster around established attainers and are absent at first dhammacakkhu, the breakthrough itself is shown to be non-absorptive.
avenues.inference_path: If deep absorption (samāpatti) is documented only as a fruit one ENTERS after becoming an ariya, then absorption is a consequence of attainment, not a precondition of the breakthrough. This severs the commentary's strongest intuition ('ariyas have deep samādhi, therefore the path needs it') by showing the deep samādhi is downstream. The 'doer-to-a-measure' (AN 3.86) then makes sense: a stream-enterer/once-returner has only partial samādhi-fulfilment precisely because the deep absorption is acquired LATER, on the way to non-return.

avenues.avenue: Anantara/samanantara conditionality — the Abhidhamma's mechanical specification of what touches the path-moment
avenues.rationale: Paṭṭhāna conditionality makes the citta immediately preceding magga its anantara-paccaya. Whatever that citta is (anuloma, itself derived from upacāra), it is the mechanically-required concentration substrate of the breakthrough. The āsevana (repetition) condition is the commentary's account of how concentration 'builds up' to magga across the parikamma-upacāra-anuloma series.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The exact conditional-relations vocabulary that ties a concentration-state to the path-moment, and whether this apparatus is wholly Abhidhammic (it is). Confirms the specification of pre-magga concentration is a Paṭṭhāna/commentary construction.
avenues.inference_path: If the only place the breakthrough's antecedent concentration is mechanically specified is the Paṭṭhāna's anantara/āsevana relations, then 'how much concentration immediately before magga' is answerable ONLY in the Abhidhamma idiom. The four nikāyas give a phenomenology (pliant hindrance-free mind) but no mechanics. The gap between the two strata is the finding: the commentary did not invent the STATE (it is the kallacitta formula) but it invented the MECHANISM and the jhāna-grading.

avenues.avenue: Did the listener attain WITHOUT having been a meditator — biographical antecedents of stream-enterers
avenues.rationale: Many stream-enterers in the canon are lay householders, brahmins, or wanderers of other sects who attain on first hearing, with no recorded jhāna training. If the breakthrough required pre-developed jhāna, these biographies are impossible unless one posits unrecorded prior practice. The 'diṭṭhadhammo pattadhammo viditadhammo pariyogāḷhadhammo' completion formula marks these on-the-spot lay attainments.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: How many attainers are explicitly non-meditators (householders/outsiders) attaining at first contact, and whether any are credited with jhāna. The completion formula 'diṭṭhadhammo pattadhammo...' fingerprints the moment.
avenues.inference_path: A large class of first-hearing lay/outsider stream-enterers with no jhāna pedigree is decisive: either (a) the breakthrough does not require developed jhāna, or (b) the canon is systematically silent about a universal jhāna prerequisite at exactly the moments it most matters. Both readings undercut a hard jhāna-floor for stream-entry. The commentary's escape ('past-life jhāna', pubbahetu) is then visible as a post-hoc rescue — and its presence/frequency in the corpus is measurable.

avenues.avenue: Akāliko / timeless — the Dhamma 'gives fruit without delay' as a property of the breakthrough
avenues.rationale: The dhammānussati epithets describe the Dhamma's fruit as akāliko (not involving time/delay) and sandiṭṭhiko (visible here-and-now). If the path-fruit is structurally timeless/immediate, that is canonical theology weighing against a slow-absorption prerequisite for the fruit.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The doctrinal framing of the fruit as immediate/timeless. Whether akāliko is ever reconciled with a required preparatory absorption.
avenues.inference_path: Orthogonal but pointed: if the fruit is doctrinally 'akāliko' (immediate), a model that requires grinding through deep absorption before the fruit can manifest is in tension with the canon's own self-description of the fruit's immediacy. This is a soft, theological inference, but it converges with the instantaneity (khaṇa) and the kallacitta findings: the canon's picture of the fruit is fast and threshold-triggered, not absorption-gated.

avenues.avenue: Vipassanā-led vs samatha-led yoking, and the 'samatha-pubbangama' vs 'vipassana-pubbangama' four modes (AN 4.170, Yuganaddha)
avenues.rationale: AN 4.170 lays out four routes to arahantship including vipassanā preceding samatha and the pair yoked. This is the canon's own typology of how much concentration leads. If a vipassanā-pubbaṅgama route is canonical, then at least one path-type does not front-load deep absorption before insight — directly relevant to whether the breakthrough needs prior jhāna.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The four-mode enumeration and the dhammuddhacca (agitation-about-the-Dhamma / restlessness from over-insight) case, which presupposes insight running ahead of calm — impossible if calm must always precede.
avenues.inference_path: The existence of a canonical 'insight-first' mode shows the sequence calm→insight is not the only sanctioned order; the breakthrough can be approached with concentration following rather than leading. That weakens any claim that deep concentration is the obligatory antecedent of the path-moment. The 'doer-to-a-measure' grade then maps onto trainees in whom samādhi is the lagging, not the leading, faculty.

avenues.lens: LENS 12 — the moment of attainment itself: the breakthrough (abhisamaya), change-of-lineage (gotrabhu), the magga-/phala-citta as a momentary event, the dust-free eye arising (dhammacakkhu udapadi), and sudden/in-sentence awakenings (Bahiya). The strategy: the canon describes the breakthrough as instantaneous and akāliko, while the Abhidhamma/commentary build a precise cognitive-series (cittavīthi) around it that SPECIFIES the concentration state immediately preceding magga. The depth-of-concentration question can be inferred three ways here: (1) what jhāna-level the magga-citta itself is said to be 'counted as' (the magga is always jhānic in Abhidhamma — every path-moment has jhāna-factors and is reckoned a jhāna — which is a commentarial answer to 'how much concentration at the breakthrough', and its CANONICAL silence is itself evidence); (2) what immediately precedes the breakthrough in the narrative frames (a gradual-talk ending in 'pliant/ready mind' vs. a deep-jhāna emergence vs. a bare hearing-while-standing) — the diversity of antecedent states bears directly on the floor; (3) the speed/suddenness language (khaṇa, instantly, mid-stanza) which, if depth required slow absorption, should be impossible — so instantaneous breakthroughs are prima facie evidence AGAINST a deep-jhāna prerequisite, UNLESS the commentary inserts a hidden jhānic moment, which is exactly the upacāra/appanā move. The lens is uniquely powerful because the Abhidhamma is FORCED to assign a concentration value to the path-moment, so a near-absence of any such assignment in the canon is a sharp signal.
avenues.avenue: Samādhismiṃ mattaso kārī — the exact 'doer-to-a-measure' gradient locus and its neighbours
avenues.rationale: AN 3.86/3.87, AN 9.12 (Saupādisesa), Puggalapaññatti give the actual gradient: in higher virtue a fulfiller, in higher mind (concentration) a doer-to-a-measure → stream-enterer/once-returner; full concentration → non-returner. This is the question's home locus and must be read with its full surrounding enumeration.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Exactly which fruits are gated to 'measure' vs 'fulfilment' of samādhi, the verb mattaso kārī vs paripūrakārī, and whether any number/percentage is ever attached to 'measure' (it is not).
avenues.inference_path: This is the canonical assertion that lower fruits need only PARTIAL concentration. The whole question is that the 'measure' is never quantified. The inference: the canon deliberately leaves samādhi for stream-entry/once-return as a partial/unspecified quantity, while explicitly RESERVING fulfilled concentration (and thus, on the commentarial reading, mastered jhāna) for the non-returner. This is the strongest single canonical text placing deep absorption AFTER the lower fruits — and it directly rebuts a hard jhāna-floor for stream-entry. Every other avenue should be read as triangulating on what 'mattaso' phenomenologically is (answer from the kallacitta avenue: hindrance-free pliancy).

avenues.avenue: Saupādisesa / the indakhīla — the once-returner-and-below as still 'with residue' in the training
avenues.rationale: AN 9.12 grades nine kinds of 'with-residue' attainers; the sub-types of stream-enterer (ekabījī, kolaṅkola, sattakkhattuparama) and once-returner sit below the non-returner specifically on the samādhi axis. The indakhīla simile (stable as a city-gate post) is the stream-enterer's STABILITY image — a fixedness that is not the same as absorption.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The fine sub-gradation of lower attainers and whether any of these grades is tied to a jhāna count. The indakhīla stability metaphor as the canonical description of the stream-enterer's settledness (immovable conviction, not deep trance).
avenues.inference_path: The stream-enterer's stability is canonically rendered as indakhīla (unshakable as a gatepost) — an image of unwavering CONVICTION/view, not of absorptive depth. So the 'concentration' a stream-enterer has secured may be better read as samādhi-as-unwavering-settledness-of-view than as jhāna. This reframes 'samādhismiṃ mattaso' as 'enough collectedness to stabilize right view', a low-to-moderate floor, consistent with the kallacitta threshold.

avenues.avenue: Sudden vs gradual framing — anupubba (gradual) language vs immediate-attainment, the seamstress/ocean-shelf similes
avenues.rationale: MN 70 / the ocean's gradual shelving (anupupubba-ninna) and 'gradual training, gradual doing, gradual practice; not a sudden penetration of gnosis' (na āyataken'eva aññāpaṭivedho) frame the PATH as gradual but leave open whether the final penetration is sudden. The tension between gradual cultivation and momentary breakthrough is exactly the lens's crux.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The canonical reconciliation: cultivation is gradual, penetration is at-a-point. Whether the gradualness is located in concentration-building or in conduct/study.
avenues.inference_path: If the canon says the TRAINING is gradual but the PENETRATION is not sudden 'all-at-once' (na āyatakena), the gradualness may be primarily moral/cognitive (sīla, hearing, view) with concentration as one component — not a deep-jhāna ramp specifically. The breakthrough at the end is then a threshold event on a pliant mind. This supports reading the concentration-requirement as 'enough to cross the threshold', set by the kallacitta state, rather than as a deep absorption that must be fully developed first.

avenues.avenue: Counter-evidence lane — frames where the breakthrough IS preceded by jhāna or where jhāna is called the proximate cause of insight
avenues.rationale: Honest enumeration must include the strong samādhi-first texts: 'samāhito yathābhūtaṃ pajānāti' (the concentrated one sees as it is), the Buddha's own awakening via the four jhānas → three knowledges, 'evaṃ samāhite citte ... abhininnāmesi' (with concentrated mind he directed it to knowledge). These are the jhāna-side's best canonical anchors.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The set of passages that DO place jhāna immediately before liberating knowledge, and whether they describe ARAHANTSHIP (full liberation) or the FIRST breakthrough (stream-entry). The catuttha-jhāna → tevijjā template is for the Buddha's full awakening, not stream-entry.
avenues.inference_path: Critical disaggregation: if the strong jhāna-first texts are about ARAHANTSHIP / the Buddha's complete awakening and the three knowledges, while the LOWER-fruit attainments (dhammacakkhu, first-hearing stream-entry) lack them, then the canon supports a SPLIT floor — deep jhāna for the higher reaches (consistent with 'fulfiller in concentration = non-returner/arahant') but only threshold composure for stream-entry. This split is the most defensible synthesis and exactly matches the AN 3.86 gradient. The lens thus predicts: jhāna-before-insight texts cluster at the arahant/Buddha end; dhammacakkhu texts cluster at the stream-entry end with kallacitta-only. Confirming that clustering is the decisive test.

avenues.avenue: Upacāra / appanā / parikamma — the commentarial concentration-tier vocabulary, to measure its canonical absence
avenues.rationale: The whole commentarial answer to 'how much concentration' is the upacāra/appanā/khaṇika tier-set, none of which (as technical terms) appear in the four nikāyas. Measuring their stratum-distribution is the cleanest canon-vs-commentary demonstration in this entire question.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: That upacāra-samādhi, appanā, khaṇika-samādhi, and the nimitta-progression are commentary/Abhidhamma-stratum terms with ~0 canonical attestation, even though the STATES they name (hindrance-free pliant mind, momentary collectedness) are canonically described in other words.
avenues.inference_path: If the tier-vocabulary is 0 in canon but the underlying states are present under canonical descriptors (kallacitta = upacāra; the momentary settledness at the breakthrough = khaṇika-samādhi), the finding is precise: the commentary did not fabricate a requirement out of nothing; it NAMED and SYSTEMATIZED a genuinely canonical threshold state, then over-specified it into a jhāna-graded cittavīthi. So 'access concentration has 0 canon' is true of the LABEL but not of the PHENOMENON. That is the most defensible, non-partisan resolution of the lens, and it can be demonstrated purely by term-stratum distribution.

avenues.avenue: The faculties at the breakthrough — samādhindriya among the five, and whether concentration leads or balances at attainment
avenues.rationale: The five faculties (saddhā, viriya, sati, samādhi, paññā) must be present and balanced at the breakthrough; samādhi is ONE of five, not the dominant one, and the texts on faculty-balance (indriya-samatta) and sharp-vs-dull faculties bear on how much samādhi any given attainer needs.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Whether samādhi is ever singled out as the rate-limiting faculty for stream-entry, or is consistently one balanced member with paññā/saddhā often foregrounded for the first breakthrough (the saddhānusārī/dhammānusārī pair turn on faith and wisdom, not concentration).
avenues.inference_path: The saddhānusārī (faith-follower) and dhammānusārī (Dhamma/wisdom-follower) — the two who enter the stream-certainty — are typed by FAITH and WISDOM, not by concentration. If the canon's own typology of those-bound-for-stream-entry omits a concentration-type, that omission is evidence that samādhi is not the gating faculty for the first breakthrough. Concentration is necessary-but-balanced, not the bottleneck — consistent with 'mattaso kārī'. Enumerating the saddhānusārī/dhammānusārī passages (MN 70, SN 25, SN 55) and noting the absence of a 'samādhānusārī' is a sharp near-absence inference.

avenues.avenue: Orthogonal: the death-bed / extreme-pain attainments and noisy contexts — places absorption is physically impossible yet attainment occurs
avenues.rationale: Several attainers reach or deepen attainment while in severe illness or sharp pain (Dīghāvu the lay youth, the dying who are given the stream-entry factors), or in non-secluded settings. Deep jhāna (especially the formless/equanimity reaches) is classically incompatible with acute pain — pain is a jhāna-hindrance. Attainment amid pain is thus evidence about the maximum concentration that need be present.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Cases where the dhammacakkhu or higher fruit arises under bodily duress or in non-meditative settings, where sustained absorption is implausible.
avenues.inference_path: If breakthroughs occur under acute pain or in unsettled deathbed contexts where appanā-jhāna is phenomenologically excluded, then the concentration actually present at those breakthroughs cannot be deep absorption — it must be a pain-compatible composure (sati + threshold collectedness). This bounds the required concentration FROM ABOVE: whatever the floor is, it is low enough to coexist with severe vedanā, which deep jhāna does not. A powerful orthogonal constraint on the upper bound of the requirement.

avenues.avenue: Orthogonal: women, children, and rapid mass-attainment scenes — scale arguments against an individual deep-jhāna prerequisite
avenues.rationale: Discourses often end with large numbers (84,000 devas, the whole assembly) gaining the dust-free eye simultaneously during a single talk. Simultaneous mass stream-entry during a discourse is structurally incompatible with each attainer independently developing and emerging from jhāna in real time.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The scale and simultaneity of mass-attainment scenes and the complete absence of any per-person jhāna step within them.
avenues.inference_path: Mass simultaneous dhammacakkhu during a talk is a reductio against a deep-jhāna prerequisite for stream-entry: you cannot have thousands independently entering and emerging from appanā mid-sermon. Whatever concentration these attainers had, it was the listening-mind composure the talk itself induced (the anupubbikathā producing kallacitta in the audience). This converges hard with the kallacitta and instantaneity avenues: the canon's typical stream-entry is a threshold event on a talk-composed mind, en masse — leaving the deep-absorption requirement as a commentarial superstructure, however much it may apply to the higher fruits.

avenues.lens: LENS 13 — The graded samādhi-faculty (samādhindriya) and samādhi-power (samādhibala): whether the canon's own internal machinery for grading the five faculties maps a GRADED quantity of concentration onto the grades of noble attainment (sotāpanna → sakadāgāmī → anāgāmī → arahant). The pivot is SN 48.24 (Saṃkhitta / "in brief": completing-and-fulfilling all five faculties = arahant; less-than-fully = the ladder down through ekabīji, kolaṅkola, sattakkhattuparama, then dhammānusārī / saddhānusārī). If the faculties are graded by DEGREE OF DEVELOPMENT and one of those five faculties IS samādhi, then the canon already encodes a samādhi-floor per fruit — but it never converts that floor into a jhāna-count. The whole question is whether samādhindriya, when "fulfilled," means jhāna, and whether a PARTIAL samādhindriya (sufficient for the lower fruits) is the canon's own name for what the commentary later calls upacāra-samādhi. Reasoning maps where in SN 48 / SN 50 / Paṭisambhidāmagga / Vibhaṅga / Puggalapaññatti the grading is set, where samādhindriya is DEFINED as the four jhānas (the load-bearing equation), and where a softer definition (cittassa ekaggatā with no jhāna formula) appears — because a softer canonical definition of the faculty is the strongest in-canon support for a sub-jhānic floor.
avenues.avenue: The definition of samādhindriya itself — does the canon equate the faculty with the four jhānas, or with bare one-pointedness? This is the single most load-bearing equation for the whole question.
avenues.rationale: SN 48.10 (Vibhaṅga sutta of the indriya-saṃyutta) gives the textbook definition of samādhindriya. There are TWO canonical formulae in circulation: (a) the faculty = making release the object, gaining samādhi / one-pointedness (vossaggārammaṇaṃ karitvā labhati samādhiṃ labhati cittassa ekaggataṃ) — NO jhāna ladder; and (b) the faculty = the four jhānas explicitly (vivicceva kāmehi … catutthaṃ jhānaṃ). The canon contains both. Which formula a given sutta uses is the crux.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: If the dominant / earliest-stratum definition of samādhindriya is the 'vossaggārammaṇaṃ … cittassa ekaggataṃ' form (no jhāna), the canon's own faculty-machinery does NOT require jhāna to instantiate the faculty — only one-pointedness — which is direct textual room for a sub-jhānic floor for the lower fruits. If the dominant form is the four-jhāna form, the faculty IS jhāna by definition and the floor is jhānic.
avenues.inference_path: The CO-OCCURRENCE of both formulae, and their stratal distribution (which nikāya layer carries which), is itself the finding. A near-absence of the four-jhāna formula in the faculty-definitions, with the ekaggatā formula dominant, would argue the jhāna-equation is a later tightening. Conversely if the four-jhāna formula is the default and the ekaggatā formula rare/late, the faculty was jhānic from the start. Even the DUAL existence supports the 'graded measure' reading: the canon offers a strong and a weak instantiation of the same faculty, exactly what a graded measure needs.

avenues.avenue: SN 48.24 (Saṃkhitta) and its neighbours — the explicit conversion of faculty-completeness into the grades of noble person. The keystone locus.
avenues.rationale: SN 48.24 says: one who completes-and-fulfils the five faculties is an arahant; with them weaker (mudutara) one is, in descending order, antarāparinibbāyī … down through ekabīji, kolaṅkola, sattakkhattuparama, then dhammānusārī and saddhānusārī. This is the canon EXPLICITLY mapping degree-of-faculty onto grade-of-fruit. Because samādhi is one of the five, the degree of samādhi is one input to the grade.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The exact monotone mapping (more faculty = higher fruit) confirms the canon grades by quantity of development, not by a binary jhāna/no-jhāna switch. The presence of saddhānusārī / dhammānusārī as the FLOOR (those who have not yet even touched the fruit but are bound for it) shows the lowest rung is defined by faculty-development being merely under way, not complete.
avenues.inference_path: A monotone ladder keyed to faculty-strength is structurally a 'doer-to-a-measure' statement (AN 3.86's mattaso kārī) expressed in faculty language. If SN 48.24 grades by AGGREGATE faculty-strength without singling samādhi out for a special jhāna threshold, that is evidence the canon does NOT impose a discrete jhāna gate at any fruit-boundary — the floor is continuous. The absence of any 'and he must have attained the first jhāna to be a stream-enterer' clause anywhere in this graded series is a loud near-absence.

avenues.lens: LENS 13 — The graded samādhi-faculty (samādhindriya) and samādhi-power (samādhibala): whether the canon's own internal machinery for grading the five faculties maps a GRADED quantity of concentration onto the grades of noble attainment (sotāpanna → sakadāgāmī → anāgāmī → arahant). The pivot is SN 48.24 (Saṃkhitta / "in brief": completing-and-fulfilling all five faculties = arahant; less-than-fully = the ladder down through ekabīji, kolaṅkola, sattakkhattuparama, then dhammānusārī / saddhānusārī). If the faculties are graded by DEGREE OF DEVELOPMENT and one of those five faculties IS samādhi, then the canon already encodes a samādhi-floor per fruit — but it never converts that floor into a jhāna-count. The whole question is whether samādhindriya, when "fulfilled," means jhāna, and whether a PARTIAL samādhindriya (sufficient for the lower fruits) is the canon's own name for what the commentary later calls upacāra-samādhi. avenues.avenue: SN 48.12–18 — the parallel grading suttas that re-state the ladder, to confirm SN 48.24 is not a one-off and to capture variant fruit-lists.
avenues.rationale: SN 48.12–18 and the wider stretch repeat 'one who fulfils them fully is X, partially is Y' with variant target-lists, sometimes folding in the seven noble persons (ubhatobhāgavimutta, paññāvimutta, kāyasakkhī, diṭṭhippatta, saddhāvimutta, dhammānusārī, saddhānusārī).
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The seven-person list is the OTHER canonical grading axis, and crucially it is keyed to whether jhāna/formless attainments are present (kāyasakkhī and ubhatobhāgavimutta 'touch with the body' the peaceful formless liberations; paññāvimutta does NOT). This is the canon's clearest internal signal that some noble persons reach the fruit WITHOUT the higher absorptions.
avenues.inference_path: If paññāvimutta is defined as one who has destroyed the cankers WITHOUT having attained the (formless) liberations 'touched with the body', the canon explicitly admits arahants who lack the full jhānic/formless ladder. That is the strongest in-canon footing for the 'dry/insight' camp at the TOP fruit — and by descent argues the lower fruits cannot require MORE jhāna than arahantship's lower bound. The kāyasakkhī/paññāvimutta contrast is the faculty-question's smoking gun; it belongs in this lens because it is where the samādhi-faculty's presence vs absence sorts noble persons.

avenues.avenue: samādhibala — the power, in SN 50 and the bala-saṃyutta / AN, and whether the power has the same dual definition as the faculty.
avenues.rationale: The five powers are the five faculties under a different aspect (unshakable, akampiya). SN 50 mirrors SN 48 structurally. If samādhibala carries the four-jhāna definition while samādhindriya carries the ekaggatā definition (or vice-versa), the asymmetry is informative.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Whether 'power' implies a higher threshold than 'faculty' (power = unshakable, hence possibly = jhāna-level stability) while faculty = merely operative one-pointedness. A graded faculty→power progression would itself be a measure-gradient.
avenues.inference_path: If indriya and bala share an identical definition (the texts often cross-refer), then there is no separate 'power' threshold and the floor is set once at faculty level. If 'power' is reserved for the unshakable (jhānic) grade, the canon may be saying: faculty-level samādhi suffices for the lower path, power-level (jhāna) for the higher. That asymmetry would let one assign the jhāna requirement to the upper fruits (consistent with the non-returner = fulfiller-in-samādhi of AN 3.86) while leaving the lower fruits at faculty-level (sub-jhānic) — a precise reconciliation of the question's own framing.

avenues.avenue: The indriya-paropariyatta / indriya-paropariyatti-ñāṇa — the Buddha's knowledge of the higher-and-lower state of beings' faculties — as the canonical authority that faculties exist in degrees at all.
avenues.rationale: This jñāna (one of the Tathāgata's powers, MN 12; also Paṭisambhidāmagga) presupposes that faculties come sharp (tikkha) and dull (mudu). The four paṭipadā (sukhā/dukkhā × khippa/dandha-abhiññā, AN 4.162ff) grade the SPEED and EASE of attainment by faculty-strength.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: That the canon's anthropology of practice is intrinsically graded — beings differ in faculty-degree, and this difference governs how and how fast they attain. The four paṭipadā explicitly tie sharp faculties to quick direct-knowledge.
avenues.inference_path: If the speed/ease of attainment is set by faculty-degree (including samādhi) but the canon never says a MINIMUM samādhi-degree gates a given fruit, the gradient is about velocity not eligibility — supporting the idea that even weak samādhi can reach the lower fruits, just slowly (dandha). A co-occurrence of low samādhi-faculty with attainment-of-fruit anywhere (the dukkhā-paṭipadā / dandhābhiññā type still reaching the goal) would directly rebut a hard jhāna gate.

avenues.avenue: The four developments of samādhi and the qualitative kinds of samādhi the faculty can be — to test whether 'samādhi' in the faculty is necessarily jhānic samādhi.
avenues.rationale: AN 4.41 (samādhibhāvanā) lists four developments of samādhi, only the first of which is the four jhānas (diṭṭhadhammasukhavihāra); others serve knowledge-and-vision, mindfulness-clear-comprehension, and the destruction of the cankers via contemplating rise-and-fall of the aggregates. Critically, the canon has NO upacāra/khaṇika/appanā terminology — that triad is purely commentarial.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: That 'samādhi' is a multi-purpose category in the canon, and the development aimed at āsava-khaya is described as insight-contemplation, not deep absorption. The absence of khaṇika/upacāra/appanā as canonical terms is itself a finding.
avenues.inference_path: If the canon's own taxonomy of samādhi-development routes the cankers-destroying kind through impermanence-contemplation rather than through deeper jhāna, the faculty serving liberation need not be the jhāna-faculty. And the TOTAL canonical absence of upacāra-samādhi (a deliberate near-absence to confirm) is the empirical backbone of the claim that 'access concentration' is a commentarial fill of a canonical gap — which is exactly what the question asserts and wants pinned to the last item.

avenues.avenue: Where samādhindriya is dropped from / present in the stream-entry definition — the sotāpattiyaṅga / four limbs of stream-entry and whether samādhi appears among the qualifiers of a stream-enterer.
avenues.rationale: The four factors of a stream-enterer (aveccappasāda in Buddha/Dhamma/Saṅgha + ariyakanta-sīla) and the abandonment of the three lower fetters DEFINE sotāpatti — and samādhi/jhāna is conspicuously NOT in that list. The fetters abandoned at stream-entry are identity-view, doubt, clinging-to-rites; the samādhi-related fetters (rūpa/arūpa-rāga, uddhacca) go only at arahantship.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: That the canon's OWN definition of the lowest fruit makes no mention of an attainment-level of concentration, and the fetters whose removal would require deep samādhi are removed last.
avenues.inference_path: This is the largest structural near-absence in the whole question. If the defining criteria of stream-entry are faith + virtue + three-fetter-removal and contain no samādhi-attainment clause, the burden is on the jhāna-required camp to import a samādhi floor the definition omits. The fetter-model's placement of restlessness (uddhacca) and form/formless-lust at the TOP strongly implies that high samādhi is associated with the LATER fruits, leaving the lower fruits with a low (faculty-level) samādhi floor. Co-occurrence of sotāpatti criteria with NO jhāna mention, across every restatement, hardens this.

avenues.avenue: AN 3.86 / 3.87 / 3.88 (sikkhā) and AN 9.12 (saupādisesa) and Puggalapaññatti — the explicit 'doer-to-a-measure in samādhi' gradient that the question centres on, read THROUGH the faculty lens.
avenues.rationale: AN 3.86 grades the trainee: fulfiller in sīla, doer-to-a-measure (mattaso kārī) in samādhi and paññā = the lower fruits (sotāpanna types, sakadāgāmī); fulfiller in sīla AND samādhi, doer-to-a-measure in paññā = the non-returner types; fulfiller in all three = arahant. AN 9.12 enumerates the nine saupādisesa persons (the same ladder). This is the gradient explicitly tied to samādhi by NAME.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The exact placement of the samādhi-fulfilment boundary: the non-returner is the FIRST grade that fulfils samādhi; everything below is 'to a measure'. This is the canon quantifying the samādhi requirement ordinally without giving a jhāna number.
avenues.inference_path: This is the question's own keystone, and reading it through faculty language closes the loop: 'doer-to-a-measure in samādhi' = a partially-developed samādhindriya; 'fulfiller in samādhi' = a completed samādhindriya = the non-returner. Cross-referencing AN 3.86's mattaso kārī with SN 48.24's mudutara faculties shows the two gradients are the SAME gradient in two vocabularies. The fact that the non-returner is where samādhi is first 'fulfilled' is decisive: it means the canon places the FULL samādhi (plausibly = jhāna, since the non-returner sheds the form/formless fetters' lower coil) at the third fruit, leaving stream-entry and once-return below the samādhi-fulfilment line — i.e., reachable with measure-only (sub-fulfilled, sub-jhānic) samādhi. The whole jhāna debate is, on this reading, already answered ordinally by AN 3.86; the only thing missing is the cardinal number, which is exactly the gap the commentary fills.

avenues.avenue: The Paṭisambhidāmagga treatment of the faculties (indriya-kathā) — the canon's most analytic grading of the five faculties, including the modes (vasitā) and the levels at which each faculty becomes dominant.
avenues.rationale: Paṭisambhidāmagga's indriya-kathā defines each faculty by its characteristic act: saddhā = adhimokkha (resolution), viriya = paggaha (exertion), sati = upaṭṭhāna (establishment), samādhi = avikkhepa (non-distraction), paññā = dassana (seeing). It also analyses the faculties at multiple attainment levels.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: That samādhi-as-faculty is canonically (well, Khuddaka-canonically) characterised as non-distraction (avikkhepa), NOT as absorption. Non-distraction is a lower bar than jhāna.
avenues.inference_path: If the faculty's defining function is avikkhepa (non-scatter) rather than jhāna-absorption, then exercising the samādhindriya requires only undistracted attention — a sub-jhānic state — which directly supports a low floor for the faculty and hence for the fruits keyed to a merely-operative (not fulfilled) faculty. The Paṭis sits at the seam between canon and proto-commentary, so its definitions also DATE the drift toward the absorption-reading: if Paṭis still says avikkhepa and the later Aṭṭhakathā says upacāra/appanā, the gap is localised.

avenues.avenue: Vibhaṅga (Abhidhamma) indriya-vibhaṅga and jhāna-vibhaṅga — the Abhidhamma's formal definition of samādhindriya and whether IT imports the jhāna ladder.
avenues.rationale: The Vibhaṅga (Dhammasaṅgaṇi too) defines samādhi/samādhindriya by a string of synonyms — ṭhiti, saṇṭhiti, avaṭṭhiti, avisāhāra, avikkhepa, samatha, samādhindriya, sammā-samādhi — with cittassekaggatā. Notably it does NOT make the four jhānas the definition of the faculty; jhāna is treated separately in jhāna-vibhaṅga.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: That even the Abhidhamma, which is otherwise maximalist, defines the samādhi-faculty by stability/one-pointedness synonyms rather than by the jhāna formula. The appearance of 'appanā' (in 'appanā byappanā cetaso abhiniropanā') as a description of vitakka/applied-thought — NOT as a name for absorption-concentration — is a key terminological point: canonical 'appanā' ≠ commentarial appanā-samādhi.
avenues.inference_path: If the Abhidhamma faculty-definition is the stability-synonym string and not the jhāna formula, the absorption-reading of the faculty is NOT even Abhidhammic; it is specifically Aṭṭhakathā-stratum. That localises the introduction of the jhāna-gate to the commentary, matching the question's claim that upacāra-samādhi is 0-canon. The canonical sense of 'appanā' (fixing of applied-thought) being different from the commentarial 'appanā-samādhi' (absorption) is precisely the kind of term-drift whose detection settles the debate.
