avenues.avenue: Puggalapaññatti's persons-by-faculty and the explicit 'samādhismiṃ paripūrakārī' classification of the nine/seven persons.
avenues.rationale: Puggalapaññatti formalises the very mattaso-kārī/paripūrakārī classification of AN 3.86 into a person-typology and ties it to the noble-person ladder.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The Abhidhamma's own freezing of the ordinal samādhi-gradient into named person-types, confirming the gradient is a settled doctrinal structure, not a stray sutta phrase.
avenues.inference_path: Puggalapaññatti repeating AN 3.86 without adding a jhāna-count means the cardinalisation never happened in the canon at all, across THREE baskets (Sutta in AN, Abhidhamma in Vibhaṅga/Pugg). A three-basket near-absence of any jhāna-number on the lower fruits is about as strong as a corpus-internal argument from silence can get. Conversely if Pugg DOES specify jhāna for any fruit, that single hit would be load-bearing the other way.

avenues.lens: LENS 13 — The graded samādhi-faculty (samādhindriya) and samādhi-power (samādhibala): whether the canon's own internal machinery for grading the five faculties maps a GRADED quantity of concentration onto the grades of noble attainment (sotāpanna → sakadāgāmī → anāgāmī → arahant). The pivot is SN 48.24 (Saṃkhitta / "in brief": completing-and-fulfilling all five faculties = arahant; less-than-fully = the ladder down through ekabīji, kolaṅkola, sattakkhattuparama, then dhammānusārī / saddhānusārī). If the faculties are graded by DEGREE OF DEVELOPMENT and one of those five faculties IS samādhi, then the canon already encodes a samādhi-floor per fruit — but it never converts that floor into a jhāna-count. The whole question is whether samādhindriya, when "fulfilled," means jhāna, and whether a PARTIAL samādhindriya (sufficient for the lower fruits) is the canon's own name for what the commentary later calls upacāra-samādhi. Reasoning maps where in SN 48 / SN 50 / Paṭisambhidāmagga / Vibhaṅga / Puggalapaññatti the grading is set, where samādhindriya is DEFINED as the four jhānas (the load-bearing equation), and where a softer definition (cittassa ekaggatā with no jhāna formula) appears — because a softer canonical definition of the faculty is the strongest in-canon support for a sub-jhānic floor.
avenues.avenue: The commentarial fill — where the Aṭṭhakathā/Ṭīkā gloss samādhindriya/samādhibala with upacāra/appanā, to LOCATE and DATE the jhāna-gate insertion.
avenues.rationale: The commentary's vocabulary — upacāra (access), appanā (absorption), parikamma (preparatory), khaṇika (momentary), and especially sukkhavipassaka (dry-insight worker) / suddhavipassanāyānika — is the apparatus that converts the canon's ordinal gradient into a graded concentration architecture and simultaneously creates the dry-insight exemption.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Exactly where the commentary assigns each grade of samādhi to each fruit, and where it introduces the sukkhavipassaka who reaches arahantship on momentary/insight concentration alone — the commentary itself authorising a no-jhāna path.
avenues.inference_path: This is the inverse-inference: the commentary that supposedly REQUIRES jhāna is also the source of the sukkhavipassaka who does NOT need it. If the same commentarial stratum that invents upacāra-samādhi also names the dry-insight arahant, then the strong-jhāna-requirement reading is not even uniformly commentarial — it is one commentarial option among two. Finding sukkhavipassaka / vipassanā-samādhi in the commentary while finding zero canonical upacāra confirms the question's framing precisely AND shows both poles of the modern debate were already present at the commentarial layer. Co-occurrence of upacāra with the faculty-glosses dates the gate; co-occurrence of sukkhavipassaka with arahatta dates the exemption.

avenues.lens: LENS 13 — The graded samādhi-faculty (samādhindriya) and samādhi-power (samādhibala): whether the canon's own internal machinery for grading the five faculties maps a GRADED quantity of concentration onto the grades of noble attainment (sotāpanna → sakadāgāmī → anāgāmī → arahant). The pivot is SN 48.24 (Saṃkhitta / "in brief": completing-and-fulfilling all five faculties = arahant; less-than-fully = the ladder down through ekabīji, kolaṅkola, sattakkhattuparama, then dhammānusārī / saddhānusārī). If the faculties are graded by DEGREE OF DEVELOPMENT and one of those five faculties IS samādhi, then the canon already encodes a samādhi-floor per fruit — but it never converts that floor into a jhāna-count. The whole question is whether samādhindriya, when "fulfilled," means jhāna, and whether a PARTIAL samādhindriya (sufficient for the lower fruits) is the canon's own name for what the commentary later calls upacāra-samādhi. Reasoning maps where in SN 48 / SN 50 / Paṭisambhidāmagga / Vibhaṅga / Puggalapaññatti the grading is set, where samādhindriya is DEFINED as the four jhānas (the load-bearing equation), and where a softer definition (cittassa ekaggatā with no jhāna formula) appears — because a softer canonical definition of the faculty is the strongest in-canon support for a sub-jhānic floor.
avenues.avenue: Faculty-balancing and the dominance of one faculty over others — whether the canon/commentary lets paññā lead with samādhi merely 'kept up', which would license a low-samādhi high-paññā profile for some attainers.
avenues.rationale: The commentarial doctrine of balancing the faculties (saddhā with paññā, viriya with samādhi) and of one faculty being dominant (adhipati) implies that profiles differ: a paññā-dominant attainer may carry only as much samādhi as needed to balance, not maximal jhāna.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Whether the system tolerates (or even predicts) attainers whose samādhi is the junior partner — the structural niche for the dry-insight type and for the dukkhā-paṭipadā/dandhābhiññā types.
avenues.inference_path: If faculty-balancing only requires samādhi to be commensurate with paññā (not absolutely high), then a person with strong insight needs only moderate samādhi to stay balanced — and moderate (faculty-level, undistracted-but-not-absorbed) samādhi reaching the fruit is exactly the sub-jhānic floor. This is an orthogonal route to the same conclusion via the balancing doctrine rather than via direct fruit-definitions. The vemattatā (diversity) of faculties across persons is the canon admitting non-uniform samādhi among the awakened.

avenues.lens: LENS 13 — The graded samādhi-faculty (samādhindriya) and samādhi-power (samādhibala): whether the canon's own internal machinery for grading the five faculties maps a GRADED quantity of concentration onto the grades of noble attainment (sotāpanna → sakadāgāmī → anāgāmī → arahant). The pivot is SN 48.24 (Saṃkhitta / "in brief": completing-and-fulfilling all five faculties = arahant; less-than-fully = the ladder down through ekabīji, kolaṅkola, sattakkhattuparama, then dhammānusārī / saddhānusārī). If the faculties are graded by DEGREE OF DEVELOPMENT and one of those five faculties IS samādhi, then the canon already encodes a samādhi-floor per fruit — but it never converts that floor into a jhāna-count. The whole question is whether samādhindriya, when "fulfilled," means jhāna, and whether a PARTIAL samādhindriya (sufficient for the lower fruits) is the canon's own name for what the commentary later calls upacāra-samādhi. Reasoning maps where in SN 48 / SN 50 / Paṭisambhidāmagga / Vibhaṅga / Puggalapaññatti the grading is set, where samādhindriya is DEFINED as the four jhānas (the load-bearing equation), and where a softer definition (cittassa ekaggatā with no jhāna formula) appears — because a softer canonical definition of the faculty is the strongest in-canon support for a sub-jhānic floor.
avenues.avenue: Orthogonal probe — the satta bojjhaṅga ordering (samādhi as the sixth, near the top) and the gradual-training (anupubbasikkhā) placement of jhāna AFTER virtue but and the question of whether insight-suttas SKIP jhāna.
avenues.rationale: The seven awakening-factors place samādhi sixth (after tranquillity, before equanimity), implying a maturation order; the threefold/anupubba training places adhicitta (= jhāna, per the standard gloss) as the middle training. If some liberation-suttas describe awakening via perception-of-impermanence/not-self with no jhāna step, that is a structural skip.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Whether the architecture mandates passing through high samādhi before paññā matures, or whether parallel sutta-templates reach release through perception-work that bypasses the jhāna rung.
avenues.inference_path: The adhicitta-sikkhā is standardly glossed as the four jhānas — which would, taken strictly, put jhāna inside the training EVERY trainee undertakes, cutting AGAINST a dry path. So this avenue is the strongest counter-evidence and must be searched honestly: if adhicitta = jhāna is canonical and the threefold training is universal, the lower fruits' trainees are ALSO doing adhicitta, just 'to a measure'. That actually RECONCILES with AN 3.86: 'mattaso kārī in samādhi' = doing adhicitta-sikkhā partially. So the very factor that looks anti-dry-path resolves into the measure-gradient. Meanwhile any impermanence/not-self liberation-template that omits the jhāna formula (a near-absence within a liberation pericope) is a counterweight showing the redactors did not always insert jhāna on the path to release.

avenues.lens: LENS 14 — the order and yoking of calm (samatha) and insight (vipassanā): which leads, which trails, whether the four ways (AN 4.170) and the four-persons typology (AN 4.94) license a calm-light or insight-led route, and what each of the four configurations implies about the MINIMAL samatha (and hence the concentration-floor) the canon will tolerate for awakening. The hinge inference: if the canon names a legitimate way in which insight precedes calm, or names a path-attainer who "gains internal calm but not the higher wisdom" as a defective-and-correctable type rather than a non-attainer, then the floor for the lower fruits sits below jhāna; if every one of the four ways and every awakening narrative routes through developed samatha first, the floor is jhanic. Negative space (the near-absence of a fully calm-less attainer in the four-fold schemes) is as probative as any positive hit.
avenues.avenue: AN 4.170 (Yuganaddha Sutta) — the four ways to arahantship, the canonical charter for samatha/vipassanā ordering. Read the sutta and any commentary on it (Manorathapūraṇī to AN 4.170). The four: samatha-pubbangama (calm-first), vipassana-pubbangama (insight-first), yuganaddha (yoked), and the dhamma-uddhacca/cittassa uddhacca case (mind seized by agitation about dhammas, then settles). This is the single most load-bearing locus: it explicitly names insight-leading-calm as a path to the GOAL, not merely a preliminary.
avenues.rationale: AN 4.170 is the one place the canon enumerates the order-relations of samatha and vipassanā as alternative roads to the same terminus (arahantship). If insight-first is a named legitimate road, the canon concedes that calm need not be brought to its peak before insight begins its work — which lowers the implied entry-floor for the path's earlier fruits a fortiori, since the lower fruits are easier than the arahantship this sutta is about.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The exact wording of vipassanā-pubbaṅgama and of the dhammuddhacca case — whether 'develops calm preceded by insight' is the magga to full liberation, establishing that a developed-samatha-first sequence is NOT the only canonical route. The commentary's gloss (does Mp read the calm in vipassanā-pubbangama as jhāna proper or as something lighter?) shows where the tradition itself placed the floor.
avenues.inference_path: A hit confirming insight-led calm as a road to arahantship licenses the inference that the path can begin and advance with samatha trailing rather than maxed-out; since stream-entry and once-return are the EARLY fruits of that same path, a sub-jhanic floor for them follows by the more-includes-the-less argument. Conversely, if even the vipassanā-first way is glossed by the commentary as eventually requiring jhāna before the path-moment, that pins the floor higher and shows the dry-insight reading to be a commentarial gap-fill, not canon.

avenues.lens: LENS 14 — the order and yoking of calm (samatha) and insight (vipassanā): which leads, which trails, whether the four ways (AN 4.170) and the four-persons typology (AN 4.94) license a calm-light or insight-led route, and what each of the four configurations implies about the MINIMAL samatha (and hence the concentration-floor) the canon will tolerate for awakening. The hinge inference: if the canon names a legitimate way in which insight precedes calm, or names a path-attainer who "gains internal calm but not the higher wisdom" as a defective-and-correctable type rather than a non-attainer, then the floor for the lower fruits sits below jhāna; if every one of the four ways and every awakening narrative routes through developed samatha first, the floor is jhanic. Negative space (the near-absence of a fully calm-less attainer in the four-fold schemes) is as probative as any positive hit.
avenues.avenue: AN 4.94 (and its neighbors AN 4.92/4.93) — the four persons by the calm/insight quality they possess or lack, and the EXPLICIT instruction that the defective ones go to those who have the missing half to learn it. The diagnostic four: (1) gains internal calm of mind but not the higher wisdom of insight into dhammas (ceto-samatha but not adhipanna-dhamma-vipassana), (2) gains insight-wisdom but not internal calm, (3) gains neither, (4) gains both. Crucial: the calm-lacking insight-haver is told to approach a calm-haver and ask how to steady, compose, unify, concentrate the mind — proving the canon recognizes a real practitioner who has wisdom-work underway with calm still deficient.
avenues.rationale: AN 4.94 is the cleanest typology of partial practitioners. The very existence of person-type (2) — wisdom present, calm absent, told to go learn calm — is direct canonical evidence that insight can be underway while samatha is still deficient, i.e. the two are dissociable in time and the practitioner is not thereby disqualified. The instruction is remedial, not exclusionary.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The precise verbs the calm-deficient one is told to cultivate (santhapeti, sannisadeti, ekodi-karoti, samadahati) — these define what 'enough calm' operationally is. If the target is described in jhāna terms, the remedy is jhanic; if it is described merely as steadying/unifying without the jhāna-factor formula, the canon's own remedy for the calm-deficient sits below jhāna.
avenues.inference_path: If the canon admits a person with insight-wisdom but deficient calm as a legitimate (if incomplete) practitioner, then calm is not a strict prerequisite-in-sequence for insight to have begun — it is a co-factor to be balanced. That co-factor framing supports a floor for the lower fruits that is 'sufficient steadiness for insight to see clearly,' not 'attained jhāna.' A near-absence — if the calm-deficient type were NEVER said to attain anything — would cut the other way; the presence/absence of any attainment verb attached to type (2) is the tell.

avenues.lens: LENS 14 — the order and yoking of calm (samatha) and insight (vipassanā): which leads, which trails, whether the four ways (AN 4.170) and the four-persons typology (AN 4.94) license a calm-light or insight-led route, and what each of the four configurations implies about the MINIMAL samatha (and hence the concentration-floor) the canon will tolerate for awakening. The hinge inference: if the canon names a legitimate way in which insight precedes calm, or names a path-attainer who "gains internal calm but not the higher wisdom" as a defective-and-correctable type rather than a non-attainer, then the floor for the lower fruits sits below jhāna; if every one of the four ways and every awakening narrative routes through developed samatha first, the floor is jhanic. Negative space (the near-absence of a fully calm-less attainer in the four-fold schemes) is as probative as any positive hit.
avenues.avenue: The samatha–vipassanā DYAD as a fixed compound and its standard predicates — 'these two things conduce to clear knowing' (AN 2.31 / the dve dhamma vijjabhagiya pair), samatha→citta-bhavana→raga pahana, vipassana→panna-bhavana→avijja pahana. This separates what each member DOES, and what each is FOR. If samatha's job is rooting out lust (a fetter relevant to once-return/non-return) and vipassana's is rooting out ignorance, the division-of-labor itself bears on which fruit needs which.
avenues.rationale: AN 2.31 explicitly assigns samatha → mind-development → abandoning of lust, and vipassanā → wisdom-development → abandoning of ignorance, and pairs the two outcomes as ceto-vimutti and paññā-vimutti. Mapping the fetters abandoned at each fruit onto this division tells you HOW MUCH calm a given fruit structurally needs.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Whether the canon ties samatha specifically to lust (kama-raga is a fetter cut only at non-return) and vipassana to ignorance (cut at arahantship). If lust is the samatha-target and lust survives stream-entry and once-return, then deep samatha is structurally aimed at the LATER fruits, not the earlier ones.
avenues.inference_path: This is the strongest indirect argument for a low floor at the lower fruits. The fetters destroyed at stream-entry — identity-view, doubt, clinging-to-rites — are paññā/diṭṭhi defilements, not raga defilements. If samatha's canonical job is the abandoning of raga (a non-return-level fetter) and the early fruits cut only view-and-doubt fetters, then the early fruits are insight-loaded and calm-light by the canon's own division of labor — and the 'fulfiller in concentration = non-returner' grading of AN 3.85ff falls out as a consequence, not a coincidence. A co-occurrence of 'samatha' with 'kama-raga' anywhere would corroborate; its absence around the stream-entry fetters would too.

avenues.lens: LENS 14 — the order and yoking of calm (samatha) and insight (vipassanā): which leads, which trails, whether the four ways (AN 4.170) and the four-persons typology (AN 4.94) license a calm-light or insight-led route, and what each of the four configurations implies about the MINIMAL samatha (and hence the concentration-floor) the canon will tolerate for awakening. The hinge inference: if the canon names a legitimate way in which insight precedes calm, or names a path-attainer who "gains internal calm but not the higher wisdom" as a defective-and-correctable type rather than a non-attainer, then the floor for the lower fruits sits below jhāna; if every one of the four ways and every awakening narrative routes through developed samatha first, the floor is jhanic. Negative space (the near-absence of a fully calm-less attainer in the four-fold schemes) is as probative as any positive hit.
avenues.avenue: The paññā-vimutta / ubhatobhāga-vimutta distinction and the saddha/dhamma-anusari, kayasakkhi, ditthippatta, saddhavimutta seven-noble-persons typology (MN 70 Kitagiri, SN 48.x faculties, Puggalapannatti). The paññā-liberated arahant who does NOT touch the eight peaceful formless liberations with the body is the canon's own proof that the highest fruit can be reached without the deepest samatha — which forces the lower fruits below that ceiling.
avenues.rationale: MN 70 defines the paññā-vimutta as one who does NOT dwell having touched with the body the peaceful formless liberations transcending form, yet whose taints are destroyed by seeing with wisdom. This is the canon explicitly decoupling full liberation from the formless (deep-samatha) attainments. The seven-persons ladder places saddhanusari/dhammanusari (stream-entry-bound) BELOW even kayasakkhi, suggesting the earliest attainers need the least samatha.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The exact negative clause ('na kayena phusitva viharati' on the formless) attached to a tainted-destroyed arahant proves the deepest calm is dispensable at the top — and the placement of the faith/dhamma-followers (stream-entry candidates) at the foot of the ladder shows where minimal samatha lives.
avenues.inference_path: If the highest fruit (arahantship) is attainable in a paññā-vimutta mode lacking the formless jhānas, then by strict monotonicity the lower fruits cannot require MORE samatha than that ceiling — so the floor for stream-entry/once-return sits at or below 'no formless attainments,' and plausibly below the four rūpa-jhānas too, since the dhammānusārī is lower still. The kāyasakkhī (body-witness, samatha-strong) sitting ABOVE the dhammānusārī on the same ladder is a co-occurrence that ranks calm-heavy types as not the earliest — reinforcing an insight-led entry.

avenues.lens: LENS 14 — the order and yoking of calm (samatha) and insight (vipassanā): which leads, which trails, whether the four ways (AN 4.170) and the four-persons typology (AN 4.94) license a calm-light or insight-led route, and what each of the four configurations implies about the MINIMAL samatha (and hence the concentration-floor) the canon will tolerate for awakening. The hinge inference: if the canon names a legitimate way in which insight precedes calm, or names a path-attainer who "gains internal calm but not the higher wisdom" as a defective-and-correctable type rather than a non-attainer, then the floor for the lower fruits sits below jhāna; if every one of the four ways and every awakening narrative routes through developed samatha first, the floor is jhanic. Negative space (the near-absence of a fully calm-less attainer in the four-fold schemes) is as probative as any positive hit.
avenues.avenue: The 'jhāna-less' or 'concentration-light' stream-enterer evidence: the standard stream-entry pericope (four limbs of stream-entry = aveccappasada in Buddha/Dhamma/Sangha + ariya-kanta sila) and whether jhāna or samadhi appears in the entry requirements at all. Also the sotapatti-anga (SN 55.x) and the sotapanna who is praised for virtue+faith+wisdom but NOT for jhāna.
avenues.rationale: If the canon's own definition of what a stream-enterer HAS (the four sotāpatti factors) contains faith, virtue, and wisdom but does NOT name jhāna or even samādhi, that omission is a powerful negative-space datum: the canon did not include developed concentration in the stream-enterer's described possessions. The AN 3.85ff 'mattaso kārī' (doer to a measure in concentration) applies precisely to the stream-enterer/once-returner — the canon GRADES him as a partial concentration-doer.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Whether samādhi/jhāna is present or absent in the four sotāpatti-aṅgas, and the exact scope of 'samadhismim mattaso kari' (which trainees it covers, and whether the once-returner is also a mere measure-doer while the non-returner is the fulfiller).
avenues.inference_path: The 'doer to a measure in concentration' grading is the canon QUANTIFYING the floor without a number — it says the early trainee's concentration is partial, sufficient, on-the-way, not fulfilled. Coupled with the absence of jhāna from the four sotāpatti factors, the near-absence is itself the finding: the canon declines to require jhāna of the stream-enterer. A hit where 'mattaso kari' is glossed by commentary as 'has not jhāna / has only access' would show the commentary reading the gap exactly as the dry-insight school does.

avenues.lens: LENS 14 — the order and yoking of calm (samatha) and insight (vipassanā): which leads, which trails, whether the four ways (AN 4.170) and the four-persons typology (AN 4.94) license a calm-light or insight-led route, and what each of the four configurations implies about the MINIMAL samatha (and hence the concentration-floor) the canon will tolerate for awakening. The hinge inference: if the canon names a legitimate way in which insight precedes calm, or names a path-attainer who "gains internal calm but not the higher wisdom" as a defective-and-correctable type rather than a non-attainer, then the floor for the lower fruits sits below jhāna; if every one of the four ways and every awakening narrative routes through developed samatha first, the floor is jhanic. Negative space (the near-absence of a fully calm-less attainer in the four-fold schemes) is as probative as any positive hit.
avenues.avenue: The danger/over-balance texts: AN on the imbalance of effort and serenity, the lute-string simile (SN 21.x / AN 6.55 Sona), and the texts warning that too much samatha breeds idleness and too much vipassana breeds agitation — i.e. the explicit canonical doctrine that the two must be BALANCED, which presupposes either can run ahead of the other. Also the 'when the mind is slack, this is the time for samatha; when restless, also samatha' (AN on the timely application of the awakening factors, SN 46.53 Aggi).
avenues.rationale: If the canon teaches that calm and insight (or effort and concentration) can be out of balance in either direction and must be tuned, it presupposes that a practitioner can be insight-heavy/calm-light and still be ON the path (needing only rebalancing). The balance doctrine is incompatible with a strict 'jhāna-first then insight' gate.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Direct canonical statements that excess samatha → kosajja (idleness) and excess vipassanā/viriya → uddhacca (restlessness), with the remedy being rebalancing rather than disqualification — establishing both members as dynamically adjustable rather than strictly sequenced.
avenues.inference_path: A balance-model of the two faculties implies a RANGE of acceptable samatha levels at any point on the path, with a floor (enough not to be restless) rather than a fixed jhanic threshold. That range-with-a-floor is exactly the 'sufficient steadiness for insight' reading. Even an orthogonal hit — the faculty-balancing of the five indriyas where saddhā balances paññā and viriya balances samādhi — corroborates that samādhi is one tunable faculty among five, not a gated prerequisite.

avenues.lens: LENS 14 — the order and yoking of calm (samatha) and insight (vipassanā): which leads, which trails, whether the four ways (AN 4.170) and the four-persons typology (AN 4.94) license a calm-light or insight-led route, and what each of the four configurations implies about the MINIMAL samatha (and hence the concentration-floor) the canon will tolerate for awakening. The hinge inference: if the canon names a legitimate way in which insight precedes calm, or names a path-attainer who "gains internal calm but not the higher wisdom" as a defective-and-correctable type rather than a non-attainer, then the floor for the lower fruits sits below jhāna; if every one of the four ways and every awakening narrative routes through developed samatha first, the floor is jhanic. Negative space (the near-absence of a fully calm-less attainer in the four-fold schemes) is as probative as any positive hit.
avenues.avenue: Commentarial gap-fill detection: where do the Aṭṭhakathā/Ṭīkā IMPORT upacāra-samādhi, khaṇika-samādhi (momentary concentration), and the suddha-vipassanā-yānika / vipassanā-yānika labels onto these canonical four-way and four-person texts? Locating the commentary's added vocabulary on exactly the AN 4.170/4.94 base texts shows the seam between what the canon said and what the tradition supplied.
avenues.rationale: The whole question turns on a commentarial supplement (upacāra-samādhi, sukkha-vipassaka 'dry-insight worker') filling a canonical silence. Pinning where the commentary attaches these terms — especially onto the four ways and four persons — reveals whether the tradition itself read these texts as licensing a calm-light path (sukkha-vipassaka) and what it thought the minimum was (access vs momentary concentration).
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Whether the commentary, glossing the vipassanā-pubbaṅgama way or the calm-deficient person, names a sukkha-vipassaka / vipassanā-yānika who proceeds on momentary or access concentration without jhāna — i.e. the tradition's own answer to the floor, and its admission that the canon left it open.
avenues.inference_path: If the commentary supplies sukkha-vipassaka / khaṇika-samādhi precisely at AN 4.170's insight-first way, that is decisive evidence that (a) the canon did not specify the floor, and (b) the tradition read the insight-led way as a genuinely calm-light route — handing the modern dry-insight camp a commentarial pedigree while confirming the canon's own silence. Conversely if the commentary insists even the insight-first practitioner must have jhāna 'as basis' (pādaka-jjhāna), that shows the tradition reading the floor as jhanic despite the canonical opening.

avenues.lens: LENS 14 — the order and yoking of calm (samatha) and insight (vipassanā): which leads, which trails, whether the four ways (AN 4.170) and the four-persons typology (AN 4.94) license a calm-light or insight-led route, and what each of the four configurations implies about the MINIMAL samatha (and hence the concentration-floor) the canon will tolerate for awakening. The hinge inference: if the canon names a legitimate way in which insight precedes calm, or names a path-attainer who "gains internal calm but not the higher wisdom" as a defective-and-correctable type rather than a non-attainer, then the floor for the lower fruits sits below jhāna; if every one of the four ways and every awakening narrative routes through developed samatha first, the floor is jhanic. Negative space (the near-absence of a fully calm-less attainer in the four-fold schemes) is as probative as any positive hit.
avenues.avenue: The 'jhāna as the path-factor sammā-samādhi' constraint — the standard definition of right concentration as the four jhānas (DN 22 / SN 45.8 Magga-vibhanga). If sammā-samādhi (a limb of the eightfold path that the stream-enterer is said to possess) is DEFINED as the four jhānas, that is the strongest pro-jhāna-floor datum and must be weighed against everything above. Also the alternative SN 45.x definition of sammā-samādhi by the noble-disciple-with-supports formula.
avenues.rationale: This is the central counter-evidence: if right concentration = the four jhānas, and the eightfold path (incl. right concentration) is what the stream-enterer treads / possesses, then jhāna looks required. The question is whether the canon ever says the stream-enterer POSSESSES fulfilled sammā-samādhi or only treads a path that culminates in it. The 'mattaso kārī' grading suggests the latter — the trainee is developing, not completed, in the samādhi limb.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The exact relation between the jhāna-definition of sammā-samādhi and the trainee's actual attainment — whether the path-limb is something one IS perfected in at stream-entry or something one is 'a doer to a measure' of, with full possession reserved for the higher fruits.
avenues.inference_path: Reconciling the jhāna-definition of sammā-samādhi with 'mattaso kārī' is the crux. If the canon grades the early trainee as only a partial doer of the very limb that is defined as jhāna, then either (a) the early trainee has incomplete/nascent jhāna (a moderate floor), or (b) the jhāna-definition states the FULL development of the limb, which the early fruits do not yet require possessing — making the definition a description of the path's completion, not its entry-ticket. Either reading lowers the entry-floor below 'mastered four jhānas'; the difference is how far below.

avenues.lens: LENS 14 — the order and yoking of calm (samatha) and insight (vipassanā): which leads, which trails, whether the four ways (AN 4.170) and the four-persons typology (AN 4.94) license a calm-light or insight-led route, and what each of the four configurations implies about the MINIMAL samatha (and hence the concentration-floor) the canon will tolerate for awakening. The hinge inference: if the canon names a legitimate way in which insight precedes calm, or names a path-attainer who "gains internal calm but not the higher wisdom" as a defective-and-correctable type rather than a non-attainer, then the floor for the lower fruits sits below jhāna; if every one of the four ways and every awakening narrative routes through developed samatha first, the floor is jhanic. Negative space (the near-absence of a fully calm-less attainer in the four-fold schemes) is as probative as any positive hit.
avenues.avenue: Cross-fruit monotonicity via the gradual-training and anupubbasikkha texts, plus the 'fulfilled in virtue, a doer-to-a-measure in concentration and wisdom = stream-enterer/once-returner; fulfilled in virtue and concentration, doer-to-a-measure in wisdom = non-returner; fulfilled in all three = arahant' schema (AN 3.85–87, AN 9.12 Saupādisesa, Pp). This is the single most direct GRADIENT statement of the concentration-floor — read it and its commentary exhaustively.
avenues.rationale: AN 3.85–87 + AN 9.12 are the texts the question itself cites as giving the gradient without the measure. They explicitly say the stream-enterer and once-returner are FULFILLED in virtue but only DOERS-TO-A-MEASURE in concentration, while the non-returner is the FIRST who is fulfilled in concentration. This is the canonical floor-statement; everything else is corroboration or counter-evidence to it.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The exact persons mapped to 'doer to a measure in concentration' (both stream-enterer and once-returner) vs 'fulfilled in concentration' (non-returner up) — confirming the once-returner too is below concentration-fulfilment, and that the leap in samādhi happens at the non-return transition, exactly where kāma-rāga (the samatha-target fetter) is cut.
avenues.inference_path: This is the keystone. 'Fulfiller in concentration = non-returner' + 'samatha's job = abandoning rāga' + 'kāma-rāga cut at non-return' form a triangle that all points the same way: deep samatha is structurally indexed to the non-return transition, NOT to the two lower fruits, which are graded as partial-concentration. The lower-fruit floor is therefore 'a measure' — present, real, but explicitly less-than-fulfilled, i.e. below the jhanic ceiling the non-returner reaches. The commentary's gloss on 'mattaso' is where to look for whether the tradition read 'a measure' as 'access concentration' (sub-jhanic) or 'first jhana.'

avenues.lens: LENS 14 — the order and yoking of calm (samatha) and insight (vipassanā): which leads, which trails, whether the four ways (AN 4.170) and the four-persons typology (AN 4.94) license a calm-light or insight-led route, and what each of the four configurations implies about the MINIMAL samatha (and hence the concentration-floor) the canon will tolerate for awakening. The hinge inference: if the canon names a legitimate way in which insight precedes calm, or names a path-attainer who "gains internal calm but not the higher wisdom" as a defective-and-correctable type rather than a non-attainer, then the floor for the lower fruits sits below jhāna; if every one of the four ways and every awakening narrative routes through developed samatha first, the floor is jhanic. Negative space (the near-absence of a fully calm-less attainer in the four-fold schemes) is as probative as any positive hit.
avenues.avenue: Orthogonal/negative probe: search the awakening-on-hearing and sudden-realization narratives (the many 'while the discourse was being spoken, the dustless stainless dhamma-eye arose' — dhammacakkhu — and Bāhiya, Upatissa/Sāriputta hearing the ye dhammā verse) for ANY mention of antecedent jhāna or concentration practice. The dhamma-eye arising mid-talk is the canon's most calm-light awakening scene.
avenues.rationale: If the dhamma-eye (stream-entry) repeatedly arises in lay listeners DURING a graduated talk, with no narrated jhāna run-up — only a 'ready, malleable, unhindered, elated, confident mind' (kalla-mudu-vinivarana-udagga-pasanna citta) prepared by the dana-sila-sagga talk — then the canon's own portrayal of how stream-entry happens does not route through jhāna. The mental state named (vinivarana = hindrances-removed) is a calm state, but a momentary/listening one, not an attained jhāna.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The standard pre-dhamma-eye mind-state formula (kalla, mudu, vinīvaraṇa, udagga, pasanna) — this IS the canon's description of the minimal calm sufficient for stream-entry: hindrances temporarily down, mind receptive. Whether that state is ever equated with jhāna or kept distinct is the datum.
avenues.inference_path: A pile of stream-entry-on-hearing scenes with the kalla-mudu-vinivarana formula and no jhāna run-up is overwhelming negative-space evidence that the canon's operative floor for stream-entry is 'hindrances momentarily suppressed, mind receptive' — a sub-jhanic, situational calm. This is the empirical counterpart to AN 3.85's 'doer to a measure': the narratives SHOW the measure being small. 'Vinivarana' (hindrance-free) being the named calm, rather than 'jhana,' tells you the floor sits at hindrance-suppression, which is precisely how the commentary later defines upacara-samadhi — so the canonical scenes prefigure the commentarial access-concentration without using the word, which is the cleanest possible demonstration that the commentary filled a real gap with a real (sub-jhanic) phenomenon.

avenues.lens: LENS 14 — the order and yoking of calm (samatha) and insight (vipassanā): which leads, which trails, whether the four ways (AN 4.170) and the four-persons typology (AN 4.94) license a calm-light or insight-led route, and what each of the four configurations implies about the MINIMAL samatha (and hence the concentration-floor) the canon will tolerate for awakening. The hinge inference: if the canon names a legitimate way in which insight precedes calm, or names a path-attainer who "gains internal calm but not the higher wisdom" as a defective-and-correctable type rather than a non-attainer, then the floor for the lower fruits sits below jhāna; if every one of the four ways and every awakening narrative routes through developed samatha first, the floor is jhanic. Negative space (the near-absence of a fully calm-less attainer in the four-fold schemes) is as probative as any positive hit.
avenues.avenue: The yoked (yuganaddha) configuration in depth and the 'samatha and vipassana developed in tandem / harnessed' imagery — the pair-yoked-oxen metaphor and whether the canon ever describes them as developed strictly-in-sequence vs strictly-in-tandem. Also Paṭisambhidāmagga's Yuganaddha-kathā, which is the earliest systematic treatment and where the technical machinery for 'how can they be yoked if they have different objects' is worked out.
avenues.rationale: The yuganaddha case tests whether the canon can even conceive calm and insight as simultaneous-on-the-same-moment, or whether 'yoked' means rapidly-alternating. Paṭis Yuganaddha-kathā lists the modes (sixteen) in which they are yoked, and is the tradition's attempt to specify the mechanics. How it resolves the same-object problem tells you whether minimal-samatha-with-insight is mechanically coherent in the system.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Whether 'yoked' is glossed as same-moment (requiring the samādhi to be present continuously, hence a higher floor) or as balanced-development/alternation (permitting momentary concentration between insight-moments, hence a lower floor). The Paṭis sixteen-modes list is the technical answer.
avenues.inference_path: If even the YOKED way is achievable with momentary/alternating concentration rather than sustained jhāna, then none of the four ways forces a jhanic floor — and the floor question collapses to 'enough momentary steadiness for insight to land,' the same sub-jhanic answer reached by the other avenues. If yuganaddha is read as requiring continuous absorption, it carves out one way that DOES need jhāna, leaving the other three (esp. vipassanā-pubbaṅgama) as the calm-light roads — still preserving a non-jhanic route to the lower fruits.

avenues.lens: LENS 14 — the order and yoking of calm (samatha) and insight (vipassanā): which leads, which trails, whether the four ways (AN 4.170) and the four-persons typology (AN 4.94) license a calm-light or insight-led route, and what each of the four configurations implies about the MINIMAL samatha (and hence the concentration-floor) the canon will tolerate for awakening. The hinge inference: if the canon names a legitimate way in which insight precedes calm, or names a path-attainer who "gains internal calm but not the higher wisdom" as a defective-and-correctable type rather than a non-attainer, then the floor for the lower fruits sits below jhāna; if every one of the four ways and every awakening narrative routes through developed samatha first, the floor is jhanic. Negative space (the near-absence of a fully calm-less attainer in the four-fold schemes) is as probative as any positive hit.
avenues.avenue: Faculty/power architecture: the five indriyas/balas where samādhi is one of five, paired with paññā, and saddhā/viriya/sati as the others — and the SN 48 texts on how the faculties mature and which faculty's maturity defines which noble person (esp. SN 48.24 / the indriya-based account of the seven persons, where the DEGREE of faculty-completion sorts arahant > ... > dhammānusārī). Whether samādhi-faculty maturity or paññā-faculty maturity sorts the lower attainers.
avenues.rationale: SN 48.24 sorts the noble persons by the completeness/degree of the five faculties: full completion = arahant, descending degrees = the lower persons, with saddhānusārī/dhammānusārī at the bottom. If the lower persons are characterized by LESS faculty-completion across the board (including samādhi), the canon is again grading the early attainers as concentration-incomplete — corroborating the 'doer to a measure' line from the faculty side.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Whether the lower noble persons are defined by deficient samādhi-faculty specifically, or by deficiency spread across faculties, or by which faculty leads (saddhā for the faith-follower, paññā for the dhamma-follower) — revealing that the entry-tier is sorted by faith/wisdom dominance, not by concentration attainment.
avenues.inference_path: If the saddhānusārī leads with faith and the dhammānusārī with wisdom, and neither is sorted by samādhi attainment, the canon's faculty-architecture entry-tier is faith/wisdom-led, not concentration-led — independent corroboration that the lower-fruit floor is set by sufficient-faith-or-wisdom plus a measure of concentration, not by jhanic samādhi. The kāyasakkhī (samādhi-led, formless-touching) appearing only in the MIDDLE of the ladder, not at entry, again shows deep calm is not the entry currency.

avenues.lens: LENS 14 — the order and yoking of calm (samatha) and insight (vipassanā): which leads, which trails, whether the four ways (AN 4.170) and the four-persons typology (AN 4.94) license a calm-light or insight-led route, and what each of the four configurations implies about the MINIMAL samatha (and hence the concentration-floor) the canon will tolerate for awakening. The hinge inference: if the canon names a legitimate way in which insight precedes calm, or names a path-attainer who "gains internal calm but not the higher wisdom" as a defective-and-correctable type rather than a non-attainer, then the floor for the lower fruits sits below jhāna; if every one of the four ways and every awakening narrative routes through developed samatha first, the floor is jhanic. Negative space (the near-absence of a fully calm-less attainer in the four-fold schemes) is as probative as any positive hit.
avenues.avenue: Susīma Sutta (SN 12.70) — the paññā-vimutta arahants who declare final knowledge yet deny the formless attainments and even deny possessing the higher powers, with the Buddha's verdict 'first the knowledge of the stability of the dhamma (dhamma-tthiti-nana), afterward the knowledge of nibbana.' The single most-debated text in the whole jhāna-requirement controversy.
avenues.rationale: SN 12.70 is THE locus classicus for the dry-insight / paññā-vimutta-without-jhāna reading: arahants who explicitly disclaim the formless attainments and psychic powers, validated by the Buddha. Its commentary (Spk) is where the tradition either embraces (sukkha-vipassaka) or domesticates (insists they had jhāna they didn't mention) the calm-light arahant.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The arahants' own disclaimer of the formless and the Buddha's 'dhamma-stability-knowledge first, nibbāna-knowledge after' sequence — and the commentary's gloss: does Spk call these paññā-vimuttā sukkha-vipassakā (dry-insight, no jhāna) or read in unstated jhāna?
avenues.inference_path: If the Buddha ratifies arahants who disclaim the formless, the top fruit is reachable without the deepest samatha; by monotonicity the lower fruits need no more, so the floor for stream-entry/once-return sits well below the formless and, on the sukkha-vipassaka reading of Spk, below the four jhānas entirely. The 'dhamma-tthiti-nana before nibbana-nana' phrase is the insight-led sequence stated at the moment of the highest fruit — the strongest possible corroboration that insight can run ahead of, and be completed before, the deepest calm-knowledge. If instead Spk insists on hidden jhāna, that records the tradition refusing the low floor even where the canon opens it — itself a finding about the canon-vs-commentary seam.

avenues.lens: LENS 14 — the order and yoking of calm (samatha) and insight (vipassanā): which leads, which trails, whether the four ways (AN 4.170) and the four-persons typology (AN 4.94) license a calm-light or insight-led route, and what each of the four configurations implies about the MINIMAL samatha (and hence the concentration-floor) the canon will tolerate for awakening. The hinge inference: if the canon names a legitimate way in which insight precedes calm, or names a path-attainer who "gains internal calm but not the higher wisdom" as a defective-and-correctable type rather than a non-attainer, then the floor for the lower fruits sits below jhāna; if every one of the four ways and every awakening narrative routes through developed samatha first, the floor is jhanic. Negative space (the near-absence of a fully calm-less attainer in the four-fold schemes) is as probative as any positive hit.
avenues.avenue: Negative-control / disconfirmation probe: hunt for any canonical statement that EXPLICITLY makes jhāna a prerequisite for stream-entry or once-return specifically, or that says insight is impossible without jhāna. The 'natthi jhanam apannassa, natthi panna ajhayato' (Dhp 372) verse and any 'one cannot see things as they are without concentration / samahito yathabhutam pajanati' (SN 22.5, SN 56.1) formula. This is the strongest pro-floor evidence and must be enumerated to be weighed.
avenues.rationale: SN 22.5 / SN 56.1 'samāhito yathābhūtaṃ pajānāti' (the concentrated one knows as it really is) and the upanisā chain (SN 12.23, where samādhi is the proximate cause of yathābhūta-ñāṇadassana) are the canon's pro-concentration backbone: insight is said to REQUIRE a concentrated mind. Dhp 372 makes jhāna and paññā mutually dependent. These must be set against the low-floor evidence.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Whether 'samāhito' (concentrated) in these formulas is defined as jhāna or left at the bare 'composed/collected' level — the same ambiguity as sammā-samādhi. The upanisā chain's 'samādhi' link, if unglossed as jhāna in canon but glossed as access-or-jhāna in commentary, again exposes the seam.
avenues.inference_path: If the pro-concentration formulas use 'samāhita' (collected) rather than 'jhāna-attained,' the canon asserts insight needs COMPOSURE, not necessarily ABSORPTION — leaving room for the sub-jhanic floor. The whole debate reduces to whether 'samāhita' = jhāna; the canon's refusal to substitute 'jhāyī' for 'samāhita' in the insight-enabling formulas (a near-absence) is evidence that composure short of jhāna was intended. If, conversely, any text directly conditions stream-entry on jhāna-attainment, that single hit would raise the floor decisively — so its presence or (more likely) absence is itself the verdict. The upanisā chain placing samādhi BEFORE yathābhūta-ñāṇa supports samatha-first as ONE order, which must then be reconciled with AN 4.170's insight-first option — the reconciliation being that 'samādhi' there means the momentary composure insight rides on, not pre-attained jhāna.

avenues.lens: LENS 15 — the negative space. The argument is structural: the canon repeatedly specifies what is sufficient/necessary at each stage of awakening (the fettering items dropped at stream-entry, the limbs of stream-entry, the gradual-training culmination), and it grades concentration on a continuum (mattaso kari → paripuri-kari). If a concentration FLOOR existed for the lower fruits, the formulas that exhaustively itemize "enough for stream-entry" are exactly where it would be stated — and its patterned absence there, set against its presence in the non-returner profile, is the finding. The method is: (1) map every locus that enumerates sufficiency/necessity for sotapatti and sakadagami and check whether any jhana/samadhi attainment is ever listed as a member of that set; (2) read the mattaso-kari gradient itself for whether the "measure" is ever quantified; (3) probe the commentary's upacara-samadhi as a gap-filler with zero canonical attestation; (4) triangulate via orthogonal subjects (the dry-insight arahant, the saddhanusari/dhammanusari fast-entrants, the deathbed/lay stream-enterers who plainly lacked jhana) where the ABSENCE of a concentration prerequisite is forced by the case rather than merely unstated. A near-absence in the sufficiency formulas, combined with positive presence of samadhi only at the non-returner tier, converts the silence into a graded claim: the canon places the concentration floor for full liberation high (non-returner = samadhi-fulfiller) but never erects one for the lower fruits, leaving the lower-fruit concentration requirement formally open — which is itself the strongest canonical evidence against a stated jhana-minimum for stream-entry/once-return.
avenues.avenue: The mattaso-kari / paripuri-kari gradient itself (the three-trainings 'doer to a measure' vs 'fulfiller') — read for whether the MEASURE is ever quantified or floored
avenues.rationale: AN 3.86/3.87, AN 9.12 (Saupadisesa), and Puggalapannatti grade the trainee as a partial-doer in concentration and reserve fulfillment of concentration for the non-returner. This is the single passage that explicitly attaches a concentration QUANTITY to a fruit-tier. If the canon ever floors the 'measure', it is here; if it only ever gives 'some' without a numeric or jhanic minimum, the gradient is qualitative, not quantitative.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: A hit pairing mattaso/padesa with samadhi and a fruit-name shows the canon's own grading vocabulary; the SHAPE of the predicate (relative 'some/partial' vs any absolute floor) is the evidence.
avenues.inference_path: If 'mattaso kari in samadhi' is the WORST concentration grade that still secures stream-entry/once-return and it is described only relatively (a portion, not a named attainment), then the canon affirmatively places NO jhanic floor on the lower fruits while reserving full samadhi for the non-returner. The gradient existing but never being quantified is the positive form of the negative-space finding.

The argument is structural: the canon repeatedly specifies what is sufficient/necessary at each stage of awakening (the fettering items dropped at stream-entry, the limbs of stream-entry, the gradual-training culmination), and it grades concentration on a continuum (mattaso kari → paripuri-kari). If a concentration FLOOR existed for the lower fruits, the formulas that exhaustively itemize "enough for stream-entry" are exactly where it would be stated — and its patterned absence there, set against its presence in the non-returner profile, is the finding. The method is: (1) map every locus that enumerates sufficiency/necessity for sotapatti and sakadagami and check whether any jhana/samadhi attainment is ever listed as a member of that set; (2) read the mattaso-kari gradient itself for whether the "measure" is ever quantified; (3) probe the commentary's upacara-samadhi as a gap-filler with zero canonical attestation; (4) triangulate via orthogonal subjects (the dry-insight arahant, the saddhanusari/dhammanusari fast-entrants, the deathbed/lay stream-enterers who plainly lacked jhana) where the ABSENCE of a concentration prerequisite is forced by the case rather than merely unstated. A near-absence in the sufficiency formulas, combined with positive presence of samadhi only at the non-returner tier, converts the silence into a graded claim: the canon places the concentration floor for full liberation high (non-returner = samadhi-fulfiller) but never erects one for the lower fruits, leaving the lower-fruit concentration requirement formally open — which is itself the strongest canonical evidence against a stated jhana-minimum for stream-entry/once-return.
avenues.avenue: The limbs/factors of stream-entry (sotapattiyanga) — the canon's own checklist of what makes a stream-enterer; test whether samadhi/jhana is ever a member
avenues.rationale: There are two canonical 'limbs of stream-entry' lists: the four FACTORS-FOR-becoming (association with good people, hearing true Dhamma, wise attention, practice in accordance) and the four POSSESSIONS of the stream-enterer (verified confidence in Buddha/Dhamma/Sangha + virtues dear to the noble ones). Neither set names a jhana or a samadhi attainment. This is the most direct sufficiency formula.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The full enumerated membership of each sotapattiyanga list. Crucially, what is NOT in it.
avenues.inference_path: If the canon's own exhaustive 'what a stream-enterer has / what makes one' lists virtue + verified faith + (in the becoming-list) wise attention and Dhamma-practice, but never a jhana, then concentration is conspicuously absent from the floor it most plausibly would occupy. The patterned absence here is the keystone: the canon CAN itemize prerequisites and chose to exclude attainment-concentration.

The argument is structural: the canon repeatedly specifies what is sufficient/necessary at each stage of awakening (the fettering items dropped at stream-entry, the limbs of stream-entry, the gradual-training culmination), and it grades concentration on a continuum (mattaso kari → paripuri-kari). If a concentration FLOOR existed for the lower fruits, the formulas that exhaustively itemize "enough for stream-entry" are exactly where it would be stated — and its patterned absence there, set against its presence in the non-returner profile, is the finding. The method is: (1) map every locus that enumerates sufficiency/necessity for sotapatti and sakadagami and check whether any jhana/samadhi attainment is ever listed as a member of that set; (2) read the mattaso-kari gradient itself for whether the "measure" is ever quantified; (3) probe the commentary's upacara-samadhi as a gap-filler with zero canonical attestation; (4) triangulate via orthogonal subjects (the dry-insight arahant, the saddhanusari/dhammanusari fast-entrants, the deathbed/lay stream-enterers who plainly lacked jhana) where the ABSENCE of a concentration prerequisite is forced by the case rather than merely unstated. A near-absence in the sufficiency formulas, combined with positive presence of samadhi only at the non-returner tier, converts the silence into a graded claim: the canon places the concentration floor for full liberation high (non-returner = samadhi-fulfiller) but never erects one for the lower fruits, leaving the lower-fruit concentration requirement formally open — which is itself the strongest canonical evidence against a stated jhana-minimum for stream-entry/once-return.
avenues.avenue: The faith-/wisdom-led fast entrants (saddhanusari, dhammanusari) and the seven-persons (ariyapuggala) taxonomy — does any tier require jhana to enter the path?
avenues.rationale: Kitagiri Sutta (MN 70) lays out seven noble persons. The kayasakkhi and ubhatobhagavimutta are explicitly defined by touching the formless/peaceful liberations with the body; the pannavimutta and the two path-entrants (saddhanusari/dhammanusari) are defined WITHOUT that clause. This is the canon explicitly distinguishing those who do and do not have the deep absorptions among the awakened/path-entrant set.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The defining predicate of each of the seven persons — who is/ isn't defined by 'having touched with the body those peaceful formless liberations'.
avenues.inference_path: If the canon explicitly DENIES the formless attainments to the pannavimutta and to the two faculty-led path-enterers while still counting them as on/in the path, then jhana-attainment is canonically NON-necessary for path entry. The contrast class (those defined by the attainments) makes the omission deliberate, not accidental — strong positive evidence for a non-jhanic lower-fruit path.

The argument is structural: the canon repeatedly specifies what is sufficient/necessary at each stage of awakening (the fettering items dropped at stream-entry, the limbs of stream-entry, the gradual-training culmination), and it grades concentration on a continuum (mattaso kari → paripuri-kari). The method is: (1) map every locus that enumerates sufficiency/necessity for sotapatti and sakadagami and check whether any jhana/samadhi attainment is ever listed as a member of that set; (2) read the mattaso-kari gradient itself for whether the "measure" is ever quantified; (3) probe the commentary's upacara-samadhi as a gap-filler with zero canonical attestation; (4) triangulate via orthogonal subjects (the dry-insight arahant, the saddhanusari/dhammanusari fast-entrants, the deathbed/lay stream-enterers who plainly lacked jhana) where the ABSENCE of a concentration prerequisite is forced by the case rather than merely unstated. A near-absence in the sufficiency formulas, combined with positive presence of samadhi only at the non-returner tier, converts the silence into a graded claim: the canon places the concentration floor for full liberation high (non-returner = samadhi-fulfiller) but never erects one for the lower fruits, leaving the lower-fruit concentration requirement formally open — which is itself the strongest canonical evidence against a stated jhana-minimum for stream-entry/once-return.
avenues.avenue: MN 70 Kitagiri 'gradual' clause and the explicit denial that final knowledge is reached all-at-once — read for whether the gradualism ever inserts a samadhi gate
avenues.rationale: MN 70's gradual sequence (faith → approach → listen → hear → retain → examine meaning → acceptance/desire → effort → weighing → striving → realize the truth with the body) is a culmination formula. It is the canon answering 'by what graded steps?' If a concentration floor existed in the gradual path it would sit in this chain.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Each member of the gradual chain in sequence; whether jhana/samadhi appears as a named rung.
avenues.inference_path: If the gradual-to-realization chain runs through faith, hearing, examination, and striving to 'realizing the supreme truth with the body' without naming a jhana attainment as a rung, then even the canon's CULMINATION formula omits a concentration floor. Absence inside an explicitly graded culmination is far stronger than absence in a one-off statement.

avenues.lens: LENS 15 — the negative space. The argument is structural: the canon repeatedly specifies what is sufficient/necessary at each stage of awakening (the fettering items dropped at stream-entry, the limbs of stream-entry, the gradual-training culmination), and it grades concentration on a continuum (mattaso kari → paripuri-kari). If a concentration FLOOR existed for the lower fruits, the formulas that exhaustively itemize "enough for stream-entry" are exactly where it would be stated — and its patterned absence there, set against its presence in the non-returner profile, is the finding. The method is: (1) map every locus that enumerates sufficiency/necessity for sotapatti and sakadagami and check whether any jhana/samadhi attainment is ever listed as a member of that set; (2) read the mattaso-kari gradient itself for whether the "measure" is ever quantified; (3) probe the commentary's upacara-samadhi as a gap-filler with zero canonical attestation; (4) triangulate via orthogonal subjects (the dry-insight arahant, the saddhanusari/dhammanusari fast-entrants, the deathbed/lay stream-enterers who plainly lacked jhana) where the ABSENCE of a concentration prerequisite is forced by the case rather than merely unstated. A near-absence in the sufficiency formulas, combined with positive presence of samadhi only at the non-returner tier, converts the silence into a graded claim: the canon places the concentration floor for full liberation high (non-returner = samadhi-fulfiller) but never erects one for the lower fruits, leaving the lower-fruit concentration requirement formally open — which is itself the strongest canonical evidence against a stated jhana-minimum for stream-entry/once-return.
avenues.avenue: The standard gradual-training (anupubbasikkha / samannaphala) ladder — does it make jhana a GATE to the fruits, or run parallel to them?
avenues.rationale: DN 2 (Samannaphala) and the MN gradual-training suttas place the four jhanas BEFORE the knowledges (insight-knowledge, asava-destruction). This is the prime exhibit for the 'jhana-required' camp. But the framing matters: the jhanas are presented as the fruits of recluseship achievable, and the destruction of asavas is the climax — the question is whether the canon ever attaches the LOWER fruits (sotapatti/sakadagami) to a jhanic gate, or whether the jhana ladder is the full-arahant route only.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The placement of the jhana formula relative to the knowledge formulas, and which FRUIT the post-jhana knowledges deliver (typically full liberation, asavakkhaya), not stream-entry.
avenues.inference_path: If the gradual-training jhana ladder culminates in asava-destruction (arahantship) and never anchors stream-entry or once-return to a completed jhana, then even the strongest jhana-required text is silent on the LOWER fruits. The negative-space claim survives the strongest counter-text: the jhanas gate the TOP, the canon nowhere gates the bottom rungs.

avenues.avenue: Stream-entry/awakening events that demonstrably lack jhana — the lay, deathbed, and on-the-spot dhamma-eye openings
avenues.rationale: The recurring conversion pericope (the graduated talk → the dhamma-eye arises on the spot while seated) shows stream-entry occurring during a discourse, with no jhana practice interposed. The mental state described (kalla/mudu/vinivarana — pliant, freed-of-hindrances) is a readiness state, not a named jhana. This is the empirical/case-based negative space.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The actual described antecedent of the dhamma-eye: a graduated talk and a hindrance-freed receptive mind, NOT an absorption attainment.
avenues.inference_path: If many stream-entries occur mid-discourse with the antecedent named as a 'hindrance-freed, pliant' mind and never as 'having emerged from the first jhana', then the canon's own narrated mechanism of stream-entry does not pass through jhana. 'Free of hindrances' (vinivarana) is the floor it DOES name — which maps onto the commentary's later upacara, but the canon stops at 'hindrance-free receptivity', not a counted absorption. The gap between vinivarana-citta and upacara-samadhi is the commentarial overreach to be measured.

avenues.avenue: The commentarial upacara-samadhi (access concentration) and parikamma/appana terminology — confirm zero/near-zero canonical attestation and locate where the commentary inserts it
avenues.rationale: The whole modern debate turns on a category (access concentration) that the commentaries (esp. Visuddhimagga) use to fill the canon's silence — a sub-jhanic but stabilized state that supposedly suffices for insight and the lower fruits. Establishing that upacara/appana/khanika-samadhi are commentarial coinages (Vism, Atthakatha, Tika) with no mula attestation is the affirmative half of the negative-space finding.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Distribution of upacara/appana/khanika across strata: dense in commentary/Tika, absent in mula. The Vism passage that schematizes parikamma→upacara→appana.
avenues.inference_path: If upacara/appana/khanika-samadhi appear ONLY in commentary and Tika and never in the four nikayas/Vinaya, then the 'concentration floor for the lower fruits' that practitioners cite is a post-canonical construct. The canon's silence was filled by Buddhaghosa's tier-scheme; the very existence of a coined intermediate category is evidence the canon left the measure unspecified. (Khanika-samadhi in particular is the dry-insight escape hatch — its commentarial-only status is decisive.)

avenues.avenue: The dry-insight / bare-insight arahant (sukkhavipassaka) and the 'liberated-by-wisdom without the formless attainments' definitions
avenues.rationale: AN/MN define the pannavimutta arahant as one who is liberated by wisdom but does NOT dwell having touched the peaceful formless liberations with the body. If even an ARAHANT can lack the formless attainments, the lower fruits a fortiori cannot require them. The sukkhavipassaka label is commentarial but rests on this canonical pannavimutta definition.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The canonical pannavimutta definition's explicit negation of the formless attainments; the commentarial sukkhavipassaka/vipassanayanika gloss built atop it.
avenues.inference_path: If the highest fruit (arahantship) is canonically attainable in a form that explicitly lacks the formless attainments, then no lower fruit can require them, and the burden shifts entirely to whether the FOUR JHANAS (not the formless) are required. This narrows the live question and shows the canon already tolerates the deepest liberation with a reduced samadhi profile — undercutting any high floor for the lower fruits.

avenues.avenue: Right-concentration definitions — is samma-samadhi ALWAYS defined as the four jhanas, or is there a jhana-free definition of the path-factor?
avenues.rationale: Magga-vibhanga (SN 45.8 etc.) defines samma-samadhi as the four jhanas — a pillar for the jhana-required camp. BUT MN 117 (Mahacattarisaka) defines NOBLE right concentration as one-pointedness equipped with the other seven path-factors (ariyo sammasamadhi saupaniso saparikkharo), a relational/functional definition NOT reducible to a jhana count. And Abhidhamma/Dhammasangani define samadhi as cittekaggata present in every wholesome citta.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The competing definitions of samma-samadhi: jhana-list (SN 45.8) vs noble-supported one-pointedness (MN 117) vs Abhidhamma per-citta ekaggata.
avenues.inference_path: If 'right concentration' has (a) a jhana-list definition AND (b) a definition as path-supported one-pointedness AND (c) an Abhidhammic definition as ekaggata in every skillful mind-moment, then the canon itself does not hold a single quantified concentration standard. The MN 117 'noble' definition (samadhi defined by its companions, not its depth) supplies a canonical basis for a momentary/functional concentration sufficient for path — i.e., the canon's own pluralism is the floor's absence.

avenues.avenue: Samatha-vipassana yoking and the four modes of arahant-attainment (AN 4.170 Yuganaddha) — does the canon make samatha-first mandatory?
avenues.rationale: AN 4.170 lists four routes to arahantship: samatha-then-vipassana, vipassana-then-samatha, the two yoked, and (fourth) the mind seized by dhamma-excitement that then settles. The mere existence of a vipassana-FIRST route, and a route where samatha arrives only after insight, shows the canon does not mandate completed samatha as a precondition.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The four-fold enumeration; specifically the vipassana-pubbangama and dhammuddhacca routes that do not front-load deep samadhi.
avenues.inference_path: If the canon explicitly sanctions developing insight BEFORE/without leading samatha and still reaching the path, then a completed-jhana floor is not a canonical universal even for the TOP fruit, and the lower fruits inherit that freedom. The fourth (dhammuddhacca) route is especially telling: it describes reaching the path from an agitated, not deeply-concentrated, starting state.

avenues.avenue: The fetters dropped at each fruit (samyojana) — the canon's most precise sufficiency ledger; test whether any concentration achievement is ever the operative cause
avenues.rationale: Stream-entry = three fetters gone; once-return = those three + attenuation of greed/hate/delusion; non-returner = five lower fetters gone (incl. sensual desire + ill-will); arahant = all ten. This is the canon's exact accounting of each fruit. It is defined by DEFILEMENTS REMOVED, never by ATTAINMENTS ACHIEVED.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Each fruit's fetter-ledger; that the differentiator is which defilements are abandoned, with no samadhi-attainment listed as the achievement that defines a fruit.
avenues.inference_path: If the canon defines fruits subtractively (fetters destroyed) rather than additively (states attained), then a concentration floor is categorically the wrong KIND of criterion — the canon's grading system has no slot for 'achieved jhana X'. The one place samadhi could enter additively is the non-returner (whose fetters gone include sensual desire + ill-will, the very hindrances samatha suppresses) — and indeed AN 9.12/3.86 make the non-returner the samadhi-fulfiller. So the fetter-ledger PREDICTS samadhi matters most exactly at the non-returner tier and not below: positive structural confirmation of where the floor sits.

avenues.avenue: Why the non-returner is the concentration-fulfiller — the link between abandoning sensual-desire+ill-will (5 lower fetters) and completing samadhi
avenues.rationale: AN 9.12 / AN 3.86-87 / Pp grade the non-returner as the fulfiller in concentration. The non-returner is exactly the one who has eradicated sensual desire and ill-will — the two hindrances whose suppression is the work of jhana. So the canon attaches full samadhi to the tier where it would mechanistically be required.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The co-occurrence of 'samadhi-fulfiller' with the non-returner and with the abandonment of the sensual/aversive fetters.
avenues.inference_path: This is the POSITIVE pole that gives the negative-space argument its shape. The canon DOES place a concentration completion — at the non-returner. By specifying the floor precisely there and grading the lower two fruits only as mattaso-kari, the canon is not silent out of oversight; it is making a graded claim. The presence of the high-tier floor proves the absence of a low-tier floor is meaningful, not a gap.

avenues.avenue: Concentration as one of the five faculties/powers and the asynchronous-maturation logic — does the canon let panna/saddha mature ahead of samadhi for the lower fruits?
avenues.rationale: SN 48 (Indriya-samyutta) treats the five faculties as developing to differing strengths and explicitly correlates DEGREE of faculty-maturation with WHICH fruit one attains (sharper faculties → higher fruit / arahant; weaker → stream-enterer). Samadhi is just one of five, and the canon allows uneven maturation.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The faculty-strength-to-fruit correlation; whether samadhindriya is ever singled out as the rate-limiter for the lower fruits, or whether the bundle matters.
avenues.inference_path: If fruit-attainment tracks the WHOLE faculty bundle's maturation and the canon explicitly permits uneven faculties (a stream-enterer can have weaker faculties overall), then a strong samadhi-faculty is not isolable as a floor for the lower fruits. The faculties' gradability mirrors the mattaso-kari gradient — concentration is dialed, not gated. A near-absence of any 'samadhindriya must reach level X for sotapatti' statement confirms no isolated floor.

avenues.avenue: Hindrance-abandonment (nivarana-pahana) as the canon's actual stated concentration-readiness threshold — distinct from named jhana
avenues.rationale: Across the suttas the readiness-for-insight state is described as the mind freed of the five hindrances and thereby 'pliant, workable, steady' — and THEN insight/knowledge follows. This 'hindrance-free workable mind' is the canon's actual named concentration threshold, and it is weaker/different from 'has attained and emerged from the first jhana'.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The recurring 'hindrances abandoned → mind becomes concentrable/workable → knowledge' sequence, and the vocabulary (mudu, kammaniya, samahita) used WITHOUT a jhana count.
avenues.inference_path: If the canon's repeatedly-named readiness state is 'free of the five hindrances and workable' rather than 'in/emerged-from jhana N', then the canonical floor — to the extent one exists — is hindrance-suppression, not absorption. This is exactly what the commentary later renamed upacara-samadhi. So the negative-space finding becomes precise: the canon DOES imply a soft floor (hindrance-freedom) but NOT a jhanic one; the commentary's contribution was to give that soft floor a technical name and a fixed position, not to find a canonical jhana-gate.

avenues.avenue: The vipassana / insight-knowledge sequence (anicca-dukkha-anatta contemplation) and whether it is ever predicated on a completed jhana
avenues.rationale: The liberation-by-seeing pericopes (anicca → nibbida → viraga → vimutti) describe the insight engine. The question is whether the canon ever inserts 'having first attained jhana' as a precondition to this seeing, or whether 'with right wisdom one sees' stands alone (sometimes appended 'samahito yathabhutam pajanati' — concentrated, one knows as it is).
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Whether the insight chain is gated by a named jhana, by generic 'samahito', or by nothing concentration-specific at all.
avenues.inference_path: If the insight chain is sometimes appended with bare 'samahito' (concentrated) and never with 'having attained the first/fourth jhana', then the canon ties insight to a generic, ungraded concentration ('samahita citta') — consistent with hindrance-freedom rather than absorption. The use of the unspecified 'samahito' (rather than a jhana ordinal) at the insight gate is itself evidence the floor is left unquantified.

avenues.lens: LENS 15 — the negative space. The argument is structural: the canon repeatedly specifies what is sufficient/necessary at each stage of awakening (the fettering items dropped at stream-entry, the limbs of stream-entry, the gradual-training culmination), and it grades concentration on a continuum (mattaso kari → paripuri-kari). If a concentration FLOOR existed for the lower fruits, the formulas that exhaustively itemize "enough for stream-entry" are exactly where it would be stated — and its patterned absence there, set against its presence in the non-returner profile, is the finding. The method is: (1) map every locus that enumerates sufficiency/necessity for sotapatti and sakadagami and check whether any jhana/samadhi attainment is ever listed as a member of that set; (2) read the mattaso-kari gradient itself for whether the "measure" is ever quantified; (3) probe the commentary's upacara-samadhi as a gap-filler with zero canonical attestation; (4) triangulate via orthogonal subjects (the dry-insight arahant, the saddhanusari/dhammanusari fast-entrants, the deathbed/lay stream-enterers who plainly lacked jhana) where the ABSENCE of a concentration prerequisite is forced by the case rather than merely unstated. A near-absence in the sufficiency formulas, combined with positive presence of samadhi only at the non-returner tier, converts the silence into a graded claim: the canon places the concentration floor for full liberation high (non-returner = samadhi-fulfiller) but never erects one for the lower fruits, leaving the lower-fruit concentration requirement formally open — which is itself the strongest canonical evidence against a stated jhana-minimum for stream-entry/once-return.
avenues.avenue: Lay and minimally-trained stream-enterers / the multitude of stream-enterers — population evidence against a jhana floor
avenues.rationale: The canon repeatedly reports large numbers of householders and deities attaining stream-entry/once-return while continuing lay life (sense enjoyment, family). A jhana floor for the lower fruits is in tension with mass lay attainment, since sustained jhana is hard for those still 'enjoying sensuality'.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Stream-enterers/once-returners described as still lay, sensual, householders — with their attainment unqualified by any jhana credential.
avenues.inference_path: If householders who still enjoy sensuality are routinely declared stream-enterers and once-returners, then the lower fruits are compatible with a life that does not sustain jhana — empirical/population evidence that no jhana floor operates below the non-returner (the non-returner being precisely the one who has dropped sensual desire). The lay-attainment pattern positively confirms the floor's tier-placement.

avenues.avenue: The 'this much is enough' / sufficiency idioms and the closing-of-the-path statements
avenues.rationale: Suttas with 'this much suffices for X' or 'one is incapable of growth unless Y' would name a concentration prerequisite if one were stipulated. The 'incapable of' (abhabba) formulas are negative-sufficiency statements — what blocks attainment.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Any 'enough for / incapable without' formula and whether concentration ever figures as the named sufficient/necessary condition.
avenues.inference_path: If 'enough for' and 'incapable without' formulas cite virtue, faith, non-remorse, or hindrance-abandonment but never 'attainment of jhana', then the canon's explicit sufficiency/necessity statements omit a concentration floor at the point they are most apt to state one. Absence in the very idiom of sufficiency is the cleanest negative-space datum.

avenues.avenue: The samadhi→yathabhutañana causal pericope (upanisa / proximate-cause chain) — does the canon make samadhi the necessary upanisa for the knowledge-and-vision that yields the fruits?
avenues.rationale: SN 12.23 (Upanisa) and AN 11.x lay out a forward chain: virtue → non-remorse → gladness → rapture → tranquility → happiness → SAMADHI → knowledge-and-vision-of-things-as-they-are → disenchantment → dispassion → liberation. Here samadhi IS a named proximate cause of the liberating knowledge.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Samadhi's position as the upanisa of yathabhuta-ñanadassana; whether 'samadhi' here is a jhana ordinal or an unqualified samadhi born of the tranquility-happiness sequence.
avenues.inference_path: This is the strongest STRUCTURAL pro-concentration text, so it must be read carefully. If the upanisa-chain names 'samadhi' generically (born of passaddhi-sukha) and NOT 'the fourth jhana', then even the canon's explicit causal-necessity statement for liberating knowledge requires only an unquantified samadhi, reachable via the gladness→tranquility route — i.e., the floor is real but unnamed/unmeasured, and is happiness-born settledness rather than absorption. The chain confirms concentration is NECESSARY-in-kind for the knowledge but the canon still declines to QUANTIFY it — precisely the negative-space thesis.
