avenues.avenue: Abhidhamma path-moment (magga-citta) and javana analysis — does the systematic literature require a jhana before the path-moment, and at what jhana-grade is the path-citta itself?
avenues.rationale: Abhidhamma (Dhammasangani, Vibhanga, Patthana, and esp. the Abhidhammattha-sangaha tradition) treats every supramundane path-moment as jhanic (lokuttara-jhana) and discusses whether the dry-insight worker's path-citta is reckoned at first-jhana intensity. This is the systematic literature's answer to the floor question.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Whether the path-citta is always classed as (at least first) jhana; the gotrabhu→magga vithi; the dry-insight path-citta's jhana-grade.
avenues.inference_path: If the Abhidhamma makes the path-moment ITSELF jhanic (lokuttara-jhana) even for the dry-insight worker — i.e., the absorption coincides with the path rather than preceding it as a prerequisite — then the systematic tradition relocates the 'jhana' from a PRECONDITION to a CONCOMITANT of awakening. That dissolves the floor question: you don't need prior jhana, the path-instant supplies first-jhana intensity. A hit here shows the commentary/Abhidhamma's actual reconciliation of the canon's silence, distinct from the upacara story. Its restriction to the systematic strata (absent in mula) is more negative-space evidence.

avenues.avenue: Momentary concentration (khanika-samadhi) and the vipassana-vehicle's concentration in the Visuddhimagga / commentaries — the precise commentarial device for a dry path
avenues.rationale: The commentaries (Vism III, and the vipassana-yanika discussions) coin 'momentary concentration' as the concentration of the pure-insight worker who never develops jhana — the unity of mind sustained moment-to-moment on changing insight-objects. This is the exact mechanism the modern dry-insight defenders cite.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The commentarial passages defining khanika-samadhi and the two vehicles (samatha-yanika / vipassana-yanika); their stratum (Vism/Atthakatha/Tika only).
avenues.inference_path: If khanika-samadhi and the explicit 'pure-insight vehicle' appear only in commentary, then the canon left the dry path neither asserted NOR forbidden, and the commentary built BOTH the upacara floor (for samatha-leaning practice) and the khanika alternative (for insight-leaning practice) to fill a single canonical silence. That the same tradition that invented upacara also sanctioned a sub-upacara momentary concentration shows the post-canonical literature itself reads the canon as not floor-ing the lower fruits at jhana.

avenues.avenue: Counter-texts that seem to REQUIRE jhana — the 'no jhana, no wisdom' verse and the jhayatha exhortations (steelman the opposing camp)
avenues.rationale: Dhp 372 ('no jhana for one without wisdom, no wisdom for one without jhana') and the recurring 'meditate, bhikkhus!' (jhayatha) exhortations are the canon's pro-jhana voice. A complete negative-space audit must locate the strongest FOR evidence to weigh against the absence.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The exact wording and context of the jhana-exhortations; whether 'jhana' there is technical absorption or the broader 'meditate/contemplate' sense.
avenues.inference_path: If the strongest pro-jhana texts (a) are verse/exhortation rather than fruit-criterion statements and (b) use 'jhana/jhayati' in contexts compatible with the broad 'cultivate meditative contemplation' sense, then even the FOR evidence never states a jhana FLOOR for a fruit — it praises meditation in general. The opposing camp's best texts being exhortative rather than criterial reinforces that the canon nowhere makes jhana a stated minimum for the lower fruits. Conceding and bounding the counter-evidence is what makes the absence finding robust.

avenues.avenue: The Susima Sutta and the pannavimutta-without-jhana dialogue (SN 12.70) — the canon's own test case for awakening without the attainments
avenues.rationale: SN 12.70: a group of arahants tell Susima they are 'liberated by wisdom' (pannavimutta) and explicitly say they do NOT have the formless attainments / are not meditators-of-that-kind, yet are arahants. This is the single most-cited canonical proof-text for awakening with a reduced samadhi profile.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The arahants' explicit self-description as pannavimutta lacking the santa-vimokha; the dhammatthitiñana-before-nibbanañana ordering.
avenues.inference_path: If full arahants in the canon explicitly declare they lack the deep attainments, the lower fruits cannot have a higher concentration requirement than arahantship. This single locus collapses the formless-floor entirely and shifts the whole dispute onto whether the FOUR rupa-jhanas (not formless) are required — and the canon's failure to attach even those to the lower fruits then stands unrebutted. The commentary's strained gloss of Susima (claiming they meant something narrower) is itself evidence of the canon resisting a high floor.

avenues.avenue: Right-effort / energy-concentration balance and the 'over-/under-concentrated' tuning suttas — concentration as adjustable, not a pass/fail gate
avenues.rationale: SN/AN tuning suttas (the lute-string simile, the goldsmith simile, the three nimittas of samadhi/paggaha/upekkha) treat concentration as something to be balanced and tuned across a range, not a threshold to be crossed. A continuum framing argues against a discrete floor.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Concentration discussed as a tunable balance (too tight / too loose) with three companion nimittas; no discrete 'minimum to qualify' framing.
avenues.inference_path: If the canon's own meta-instruction treats concentration as a dial balanced against energy and equanimity (apply concentration-sign when agitated, energy-sign when slack), then concentration is conceived as a regulated continuum, structurally incompatible with a stated pass/fail floor. The tuning model mirrors the mattaso-kari gradient: depth is dialed to need, not gated to a fruit.

avenues.avenue: Cross-stratum distribution audit of the floor-words themselves — establish the canon-vs-commentary boundary empirically by where the relevant samadhi-tier vocabulary lives
avenues.rationale: A deliberate breadth-sweep counting where each concentration-tier term occurs across mula / Abhidhamma / Atthakatha / Tika gives the empirical backbone: jhana/samadhi/samapatti present canon-wide; upacara/appana/parikamma/khanika clustering in commentary. The stratum-distribution IS the argument.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Relative frequency/presence of canon-attested vs commentary-only concentration vocabulary by stratum.
avenues.inference_path: If the tier-words that DEFINE a sub-jhanic floor (upacara, parikamma, appana, khanika) are absent from mula and dense in commentary while the canon-wide words (jhana, samadhi, samapatti) never co-occur with a lower-fruit-minimum predicate, then the entire apparatus that supplies a concentration floor is a commentarial layer over a canon that graded concentration without flooring it. This audit converts the negative space into a measured, defensible distribution claim — the deliverable's spine.

avenues.lens: Lens 16 — jhāna as pleasant-abiding (diṭṭha-dhamma-sukha-vihāra) vs soteriological requisite. The hypothesis: wherever the canon frames jhāna as an *abiding* (vihāra) — what the Tathāgata/arahant dwells in, a present-life comfort, a "pleasant abiding here and now" — rather than as a *cause* of fetter-cutting or stream-entry, the path-concentration floor is decoupled from full jhāna. Every locus where jhāna is praised-yet-not-causally-wired to liberation, every place it appears as the fruit/dwelling of one already accomplished, and every place the path-to-awakening sequence is stated WITHOUT jhāna as its concentration term, lowers the inferred floor. Conversely, every place jhāna sits inside the causal chain to āsavakkhaya raises it. The decisive evidence is distributional and contrastive: which verb governs jhāna (viharati 'dwells' vs a causal/proximate-cause idiom), and whether the lower fruits ever co-occur with the jhāna formula.
avenues.avenue: The 'pleasant abiding here and now' formula itself — the core diagnostic phrase
avenues.rationale: The single most load-bearing phrase. The four jhānas are repeatedly glossed as 'abhicetasika diṭṭhadhammasukhavihāra' — heightened mental states that are a pleasant abiding in the here-and-now — NOT as the proximate cause of āsavakkhaya. Where this gloss governs jhāna, jhāna is framed as comfort/dwelling, not as the fetter-cutting engine. The verb viharati ('dwells in') vs a causal idiom is the contrastive tell.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: A hit confirms the canon's own characterization of jhāna as an abiding/comfort; counting how often the four-jhāna formula is tagged 'ditthadhammasukhavihara' vs tagged as a step toward ñāṇa/āsavakkhaya gives a distributional ratio for abiding-framing vs cause-framing.
avenues.inference_path: If the dominant canonical gloss on the jhāna tetrad is 'pleasant abiding here and now' rather than 'and with this concentrated mind he directs it to the knowledge of the destruction of the taints', then jhāna's primary canonical role is dwelling, and the soteriological work is done by the subsequent insight move — lowering the concentration floor required AT the moment of fetter-cutting. Even a near-absence of the causal idiom in the same breath as the abiding idiom is evidence: the canon keeps them in separate registers.

avenues.avenue: The four-fold typology of jhāna-development (AN 4.41 Samadhibhavana) — the explicit canonical partition of what concentration is FOR
avenues.rationale: AN 4.41 is the canon's own four-way sort of concentration-development by PURPOSE: (1) ditthadhammasukhavihara, (2) ñāṇadassana, (3) sati-sampajañña, (4) āsavakkhaya. Critically, the FIRST purpose is the four jhānas and is explicitly the pleasant-abiding one; the LAST (taint-destruction) is given a DIFFERENT practice (contemplating rise/fall of the five aggregates), not the jhānas. This is the strongest single text for the lens.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Confirms the canon partitions concentration so that the jhāna-tetrad maps to 'pleasant abiding' while taint-destruction is assigned to aggregate-contemplation — i.e. the canon itself separates the abiding-function from the liberating-function.
avenues.inference_path: If the very text that sorts samādhi by purpose assigns jhāna to abiding and assigns taint-destruction to a non-jhānic insight practice, then the canon's own taxonomy decouples the concentration floor for liberation from jhāna. This is near-decisive for a lowered floor: the canon does not need full jhāna to be present in the taint-destruction lane it itself defines.

avenues.lens: Lens 16 — jhāna as pleasant-abiding (diṭṭha-dhamma-sukha-vihāra) vs soteriological requisite. The hypothesis: wherever the canon frames jhāna as an *abiding* (vihāra) — what the Tathāgata/arahant dwells in, a present-life comfort, a "pleasant abiding here and now" — rather than as a *cause* of fetter-cutting or stream-entry, the path-concentration floor is decoupled from full jhāna. Every locus where jhāna is praised-yet-not-causally-wired to liberation, every place it appears as the fruit/dwelling of one already accomplished, and every place the path-to-awakening sequence is stated WITHOUT jhāna as its concentration term, lowers the inferred floor. Conversely, every place jhāna sits inside the causal chain to āsavakkhaya raises it. The decisive evidence is distributional and contrastive: which verb governs jhāna (viharati 'dwells' vs a causal/proximate-cause idiom), and whether the lower fruits ever co-occur with the jhāna formula.
avenues.avenue: Jhāna as the Tathagata's / arahant's dwelling — abiding of the accomplished, not road to accomplishment
avenues.rationale: The canon names a triad of 'dwellings' the Tathāgata uses — dibba (divine, = jhāna/brahmavihāra), brahma, ariya — explicitly as what he ABIDES in now, often after awakening. If the jhāna-tetrad clusters with 'the dwelling the Tathāgata dwells in' it is framed as the fruit-state, not the path-cause.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Shows jhāna catalogued among the post-awakening dwellings (dibba-vihāra) — a state of the already-liberated rather than a precondition for liberating.
avenues.inference_path: If jhāna appears as 'the dwelling I now dwell in' spoken by an arahant/Tathāgata, its role is enjoyment of attainment, not production of it. A corpus where jhāna predominantly co-occurs with the accomplished-dweller frame, and only secondarily with the path-sequence frame, supports an abiding-over-cause reading and a lower floor for the lower fruits.

avenues.lens: Lens 16 — jhāna as pleasant-abiding (diṭṭha-dhamma-sukha-vihāra) vs soteriological requisite. avenues.avenue: The contrastive control: jhāna INSIDE the causal chain to taint-destruction (the gradual-training pericope) — the rival framing whose frequency must be weighed against the abiding framing
avenues.rationale: To measure the abiding-framing you must count the rival cause-framing. The gradual-training formula (DN 2 etc.) does causally wire jhāna to āsavakkhaya: '...with mind thus concentrated, purified... he directs it to knowledge of the destruction of the taints.' This is the jhāna-as-requisite evidence. Its distribution (which suttas, which nikāyas, mūla vs commentary) is the denominator.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Maps every place jhāna is explicitly the proximate platform for the taint-destruction move — the strongest pro-requisite evidence; its count and clustering set the baseline against which the abiding-framing is judged.
avenues.inference_path: If the cause-framing is concentrated in a single stereotyped pericope (gradual training) replicated across many suttas while the abiding-framing is distributed and the LOWER fruits (stream-entry/once-return) are NEVER attached to either the jhāna formula or this causal move, then jhāna's causal wiring belongs to the arahant/āsavakkhaya tier, not the lower-fruit tier — lowering the floor for stream-entry/once-return specifically while leaving the arahant question open.

avenues.avenue: Jhāna praised but immediately bounded — the 'not yet enough / I did not rest content' move
avenues.rationale: Several passages (Bodhisatta's recollection of Āḷāra/Uddaka; the 'jhāna is not enough for full liberation' moves) praise the attainments yet explicitly deny they constitute the goal. This is jhāna-praised-yet-not-fetter-cutting in pure form.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Captures the explicit canonical statements that high concentrative attainment, however refined, does not by itself cut fetters — separating attainment from liberation.
avenues.inference_path: If the canon repeatedly praises concentrative attainments and then says 'this does not lead to disenchantment, dispassion, cessation', it treats samādhi as instrumental-but-insufficient — which both (a) confirms jhāna is not the fetter-cutting cause per se and (b) implies the cutting requires an insight act that can in principle ride on less-than-peak concentration. Near-absence of such bounding around the LOWER fruits is also informative.

avenues.lens: Lens 16 — jhāna as pleasant-abiding (diṭṭha-dhamma-sukha-vihāra) vs soteriological requisite. The hypothesis: wherever the canon frames jhāna as an *abiding* (vihāra) — what the Tathāgata/arahant dwells in, a present-life comfort, a "pleasant abiding here and now" — rather than as a *cause* of fetter-cutting or stream-entry, the path-concentration floor is decoupled from full jhāna. avenues.avenue: Stream-entry / once-return causation stated WITHOUT jhāna — the four factors of stream-entry and the right-view/hearing route
avenues.rationale: If the canonical CAUSES of stream-entry are listed as association with good people, hearing the true Dhamma, wise attention, and practice in accordance — with right view arising from 'another's voice' (paratoghosa) — and jhāna is NOT among them, then the lower fruits have an explicitly non-jhānic causal pathway in the canon.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Shows the canon's stated proximate causes for stream-entry and whether the jhāna formula ever appears among them.
avenues.inference_path: A near-total ABSENCE of the jhāna tetrad in the stream-entry-factor lists, combined with presence of hearing/right-view causes, is strong negative-space evidence that the floor for the lower fruits is sub-jhānic. This is the cleanest inference of the lens: the canon names what causes stream-entry, and jhāna is not on the list, so jhāna is not required for stream-entry even if it is a pleasant abiding available to a stream-enterer.

avenues.lens: Lens 16 — jhāna as pleasant-abiding (diṭṭha-dhamma-sukha-vihāra) vs soteriological requisite. avenues.avenue: The persons-typology: doer-to-a-measure vs fulfiller in concentration (the question's own anchor) — and whether the lower fruits sit at the 'measure' level
avenues.rationale: AN 3.86/3.87 and AN 9.12 (Saupadisesa) grade the trainee: stream-enterer and once-returner are 'doers to a measure' (mattaso kāri) in concentration; the fulfiller in concentration is the non-returner. This is the canon's explicit GRADIENT of the concentration requirement keyed to fruit.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Pins the exact rung at which 'full' concentration becomes required — confirming the lower fruits demand only partial/measured samādhi while non-return (and the brahmavihāra/jhāna-correlated states) demand fulfillment.
avenues.inference_path: If mattaso-kāri (partial concentration) is sufficient for stream-entry and once-return, and only the non-returner is the concentration-fulfiller, then the canon itself sets the lower-fruit floor BELOW the fulfilled-concentration (= jhāna-correlated) level. This directly answers the question's quantitative core: the measure for the lower fruits is explicitly less than full. Pairing this with the jhāna↔non-returner correlation (next avenue) localizes full jhāna to the non-return tier.

The hypothesis: wherever the canon frames jhāna as an *abiding* (vihāra) — what the Tathāgata/arahant dwells in, a present-life comfort, a "pleasant abiding here and now" — rather than as a *cause* of fetter-cutting or stream-entry, the path-concentration floor is decoupled from full jhāna. avenues.avenue: The jhāna ↔ non-returner / Pure-Abodes correlation — where full jhāna IS causally wired, it is wired to non-return, not the lower fruits
avenues.rationale: AN 9.36 (Jhānasutta) and the cosmology-of-rebirth texts tie the jhānas to the form/formless realms and to non-return (rebirth in the Pure Abodes). Where jhāna is made the platform 'jhānaṃ nissāya āsavānaṃ khayo', the text then routes the not-fully-liberated practitioner to non-return, not to a lower fruit.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Shows the canon's positive jhāna-as-platform statements terminate in non-return / Pure-Abode rebirth, i.e. the jhāna lane and the non-return tier are the same lane.
avenues.inference_path: If every locus that DOES make jhāna a liberation-platform pairs it with non-return / the destruction of the five lower fetters / Pure-Abode rebirth, then full jhāna's causal soteriological role is concentrated at the non-return tier — precisely where the persons-typology says concentration is FULFILLED. This converges with the mattaso-kāri gradient and pushes the stream-entry/once-return floor below jhāna.

Every locus where jhāna is praised-yet-not-causally-wired to liberation, every place it appears as the fruit/dwelling of one already accomplished, and every place the path-to-awakening sequence is stated WITHOUT jhāna as its concentration term, lowers the inferred floor. avenues.avenue: Bare-insight / wisdom-liberated persons who reach the goal without the full set of jhānic attainments — the pannavimutti vs ubhatobhagavimutti split
avenues.rationale: The seven-persons scheme (MN 70 Kītāgiri) and the Susīma Sutta (SN 12.70) describe the paññāvimutta who is liberated WITHOUT the peaceful formless liberations and, on the wisdom-liberated reading, possibly without the full jhāna set — 'na ca kāyena phusitvā viharati' (he does not dwell having touched [the liberations] with the body). This is the canon's strongest internal handle for a less-concentration path.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Identifies canonical person-types liberated without the full attainment-ladder, and the Susīma claim that liberation-by-wisdom precedes/needn't include the formless attainments ('first the knowledge of the stability of the Dhamma, afterward the knowledge of Nibbāna').
avenues.inference_path: If the canon recognizes fully-liberated persons who lack the peaceful formless abidings (ubhatobhāgavimutta requires them; paññāvimutta does not), then even at the ARAHANT tier the concentration floor is contested-but-lowerable — and a fortiori the lower fruits sit below it. The Susīma 'dhammaṭṭhiti-ñāṇa before nibbāna-ñāṇa' line, read with the monks denying the formless attainments, is the canon hinting at a dry route; commentary's reframing of it (the 'these are dry-insight arahants' gloss) is itself evidence of the gap the question names.

The decisive evidence is distributional and contrastive: which verb governs jhāna (viharati 'dwells' vs a causal/proximate-cause idiom), and whether the lower fruits ever co-occur with the jhāna formula.
avenues.avenue: Verb-governance distributional test — does 'jhana' get a DWELLING verb or a CAUSING verb?
avenues.rationale: A purely grammatical census: the jhāna formula is almost always closed by 'upasampajja viharati' (having attained, he DWELLS) — a dwelling verb. The causal verbs (abhininnameti 'directs', nissāya 'depending on', padaka 'as a base') are the cause-frame. The ratio of dwelling-closure to causal-closure across the corpus is a direct metric for the lens.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Quantifies how often jhāna is grammatically an abode (viharati) vs a cause/base (abhininnameti / nissāya / padaka).
avenues.inference_path: If 'upasampajja viharati' overwhelmingly dominates the jhāna formula's closure and the causal idioms are rarer and clustered in the gradual-training pericope and commentary, the canon's default grammar treats jhāna as a place to dwell, not a lever to pull. The very rarity of the causal closure relative to the dwelling closure is the inference: abiding is the unmarked, default sense; cause is the marked, contextual one. Note: 'padaka' / 'padakajjhana' (basis-jhāna) is largely a COMMENTARIAL term — its near-absence in mūla vs presence in aṭṭhakathā is itself a finding (commentary supplies the causal wiring the canon leaves loose).

avenues.lens: Lens 16 — jhāna as pleasant-abiding (diṭṭha-dhamma-sukha-vihāra) vs soteriological requisite. The hypothesis: wherever the canon frames jhāna as an *abiding* (vihāra) — what the Tathāgata/arahant dwells in, a present-life comfort, a "pleasant abiding here and now" — rather than as a *cause* of fetter-cutting or stream-entry, the path-concentration floor is decoupled from full jhāna. The decisive evidence is distributional and contrastive: which verb governs jhāna (viharati 'dwells' vs a causal/proximate-cause idiom), and whether the lower fruits ever co-occur with the jhāna formula.
avenues.avenue: Commentarial gap-fill: upacara/appana, padaka-jjhana, vipassana-only — the 0-canon constructs the question names
avenues.rationale: The whole modern debate hinges on whether the commentary INVENTS the sub-jhānic access-concentration to fill the floor the canon leaves open. A substring census of upacāra / appanā / khaṇika / padakajjhāna / sukkhavipassaka with a canon-vs-commentary split directly measures the gap.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Whether these terms occur in mūla at all (expected: near-zero) vs their density in aṭṭhakathā/ṭīkā — quantifying exactly how much the floor-filling is a commentarial overlay.
avenues.inference_path: If upacāra-samādhi, sukkhavipassaka, and padakajjhāna register ~0 in mūla and richly in commentary, the commentary is demonstrably supplying a concentration floor the canon did not specify. That asymmetry is the lens's punchline: the canon gives a gradient (mattaso → paripūra) and an abiding-frame for jhāna; the commentary retrofits a graded sub-jhānic ladder (parikamma→upacāra→appanā) to operationalize 'how much.' The size of the commentarial term-cloud is a proxy for how under-determined the canonical floor was.

avenues.avenue: Right-concentration's DEFINITION — is sammasamadhi defined AS the four jhanas, and is that definition path-causal or descriptive?
avenues.rationale: Sammāsamādhi is canonically defined as the four jhānas (DN 22, SN 45.8). But MN 117 redefines 'noble right concentration with its supports' as one-pointedness equipped by the other seven path factors — a relational, not a fixed-altitude, definition. The tension between the jhāna-definition and the 'unification-supported-by-the-path' definition is central.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Whether right concentration is rigidly the jhāna tetrad or, per MN 117, cittekaggatā made noble by its path-context — which would admit unification below jhāna depth if the other factors are present.
avenues.inference_path: If sammāsamādhi can be defined as path-equipped one-pointedness (MN 117) rather than only as the four jhānas, then the path's concentration factor is satisfiable by unification-in-context, not necessarily by full absorption. Where the jhāna-definition is descriptive (here is the high form) rather than gating (you must have this to proceed), the floor for the factor — and thus for the lower fruits — is lowered. The co-existence of both definitions in the canon is itself evidence the requirement is graded, not fixed.

avenues.avenue: Joy/pleasure lineage of liberation — the sukha→samadhi→yathabhutañana causal cascade where the AFFECT (not the absorption depth) does the work
avenues.rationale: The Upanisā/dependent-liberation cascade (SN 12.23, AN 11.1-ff) chains gladness→rapture→tranquillity→pleasure→samādhi→knowledge-and-vision-of-things-as-they-are→disenchantment→dispassion→liberation. Here 'samādhi' (un-numbered, not specified as four jhānas) is a link, and the proximate cause of yathābhūtañāṇa is 'samāhito' (concentrated), not 'fourth-jhāna'd'.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Whether the canon's natural-causation account of liberation specifies the four jhānas or just 'samādhi/samāhita' as the link before knowing-as-it-is.
avenues.inference_path: If the liberation-cascade names only 'samādhi' / 'samāhita citta' (a concentrated mind) and not the jhāna tetrad as the platform for yathābhūtañāṇa, the canon's own causal physiology of awakening requires concentration-sufficient-for-seeing, not jhāna-as-such. The unspecified 'samādhi' in the most explicitly CAUSAL chain in the canon, set against the explicitly four-fold jhāna in the ABIDING frame, is a strong inference: where the canon talks cause it says 'concentrated'; where it talks abiding it says 'four jhānas.' That asymmetry lowers the floor.

avenues.avenue: Negative space / orthogonal probe — diseases, deathbed, lay attainers, women, quick awakenings: stream-entry reached in contexts where jhana cultivation is implausible
avenues.rationale: The 'dust-free, stainless Dhamma-eye arose in him on that very seat' formula (after a graduated talk) describes stream-entry occurring suddenly, often to laypeople or the gravely ill, with no preceding jhāna cultivation narrated — only a 'ready, malleable, unhindered, glad, confident' mind (the canon's term for the receptive state before insight).
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Maps every sudden-stream-entry episode and the mental-readiness vocabulary (kalla/mudu/udagga/pasanna-citta) that precedes it, checking whether jhāna is ever the stated precondition.
avenues.inference_path: If the recurrent stream-entry trigger is a graduated talk producing a 'ready/malleable/gladdened/confident' mind — a momentary receptivity, not a worked-up absorption — and the attainers are laypeople or the sick for whom jhāna mastery is implausible, then the operational floor for stream-entry in the canon's NARRATIVES is a brief unified receptive mind, far below jhāna. The systematic absence of jhāna from these episodes (orthogonal to the doctrinal lists) is independent corroboration: the canon's stories and its lists agree the lower-fruit floor is sub-jhānic. Conversely, if even one Dhamma-eye episode is jhāna-gated, that bounds the claim.

avenues.avenue: The five-factored / mundane-vs-supramundane and 'concentration of the noble eightfold path' counter-frame
avenues.rationale: The Paṭisambhidāmagga / Abhidhamma develop a supramundane path-moment samādhi (the concentration co-arising in the path/fruit mind) distinct from mundane jhāna. The 'momentary one-pointedness of the path-mind' is, on the dry-insight reading, the concentration that actually accompanies fetter-cutting — and it is a citta-moment, not a sustained absorption.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Whether the canon/Abhidhamma distinguish a path-moment supramundane concentration from sustained jhāna, and whether the fetter-cutting samādhi is the former.
avenues.inference_path: If the actual fetter-cutting concentration is theorized as a supramundane path-moment unification (lokuttara magga-samādhi), then full mundane jhāna is not the thing doing the cutting at all — it is at most a pleasant abiding and a possible (not necessary) springboard. This is the Abhidhammic vindication of a sub-jhānic floor: the concentration that matters at the moment of stream-entry is a momentary unification of the path-citta. The canon-vs-Abhidhamma-vs-commentary split on this term-cloud shows how much of the 'lowered floor' is canonical vs constructed.

avenues.lens: Lens 16 — jhāna as pleasant-abiding (diṭṭha-dhamma-sukha-vihāra) vs soteriological requisite. The hypothesis: wherever the canon frames jhāna as an *abiding* (vihāra) — what the Tathāgata/arahant dwells in, a present-life comfort, a "pleasant abiding here and now" — rather than as a *cause* of fetter-cutting or stream-entry, the path-concentration floor is decoupled from full jhāna. Every locus where jhāna is praised-yet-not-causally-wired to liberation, every place it appears as the fruit/dwelling of one already accomplished, and every place the path-to-awakening sequence is stated WITHOUT jhāna as its concentration term, lowers the inferred floor. Conversely, every place jhāna sits inside the causal chain to āsavakkhaya raises it. The decisive evidence is distributional and contrastive: which verb governs jhāna (viharati 'dwells' vs a causal/proximate-cause idiom), and whether the lower fruits ever co-occur with the jhāna formula.
avenues.avenue: Where jhana is explicitly the comfort the Buddha RECOMMENDS for present ease (not progress) — rest, dwelling-at-ease, abiding pleasantly even by arahants
avenues.rationale: Texts where the Buddha or arahants enter jhāna/cessation simply to abide pleasantly (the 'I sleep well', the entering of fruition-attainment / cessation for present comfort) show jhāna functioning as rest/refreshment for the already-done, decoupled from any further fetter work.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Episodes of jhāna/cessation entered for ease by the accomplished — pure abiding-function with zero causal-soteriological claim attached.
avenues.inference_path: If arahants enter jhāna and even cessation for present comfort with no further liberative claim, then at the top of the path jhāna is purely an abiding — which retroactively supports reading its lower-path appearances as primarily abiding too, unless explicitly causal. The clearer the 'enters-for-comfort, nothing-to-attain' frame, the more the burden shifts to the cause-frame texts to prove jhāna is REQUIRED rather than merely available; and that burden is hardest to meet at the lower-fruit tier where, by the negative-space avenue, jhāna is simply absent.

avenues.lens: LENS 17 — concentration as a CAUSED fruit of virtue-and-gladness, not a willed prerequisite. If the canon's natural-causation series (upanisa) makes samādhi arise by itself out of non-remorse → gladness → rapture → tranquillity, then the "doer-to-a-measure" (mattaso kārī) of the stream-enterer/once-returner need not be a separately-cultivated jhānic attainment at all: it is precisely the concentration that the virtue and gladness of one who has seen the Dhamma generates as a by-product. The whole jhāna-vs-dry-insight debate may be mis-framed if the canon's own causal grammar treats samādhi as effect, not entry-ticket. The strategy is to map every place the canon says samādhi is CAUSED/arises (rather than is developed/entered), measure how tightly that caused-samādhi is bound to sīla and to the stream-enterer's profile, and read the "measure" of AN 3.86 as the floor that this causation reliably produces.
avenues.avenue: The core upanisā / natural-causation chain itself (SN 12.23 Upanisā, AN 10.1-5, AN 11.1-5) — locate every link and especially the pivot link 'of one who is happy the mind becomes concentrated' (sukhino cittaṃ samādhiyati). This is the load-bearing sentence: samādhi here is intransitive-passive, an effect of sukha, not a transitive cultivation.
avenues.rationale: This series is the single strongest canonical statement that samādhi is caused, and its verb morphology (passive/middle -samādhiyati 'becomes concentrated') is itself the evidence: the canon does not say one 'makes' (transitive) the concentration here, it says it 'happens'. The link sits ABOVE knowledge-and-vision in SN 12.23, i.e. caused-samādhi is upstream of insight, not a separate parallel track.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Every occurrence of -samādhiyati in this passive/resultative frame, co-located with sukha/pīti/passaddhi, is a vote that the canon's own grammar treats concentration as effect. Counting how many distinct suttas carry the full ladder vs a truncated one shows how routinized this causal reading is.
avenues.inference_path: If the verb is overwhelmingly the middle/passive -samādhiyati in these contexts (vs the agentive samādhiṃ bhāveti / jhānaṃ samāpajjati of the formal-jhāna pericope), then the canon lexically distinguishes caused-samādhi from cultivated-jhāna — which means the mattaso-kārī 'measure' could be satisfied by the former without the latter. Even a NEAR-ABSENCE of agentive verbs in the stream-enterer contexts, set against their PRESENCE in non-returner/jhāna contexts, is the gradient the question asks for, drawn straight from morphology.

The strategy is to map every place the canon says samādhi is CAUSED/arises (rather than is developed/entered), measure how tightly that caused-samādhi is bound to sīla and to the stream-enterer's profile, and read the "measure" of AN 3.86 as the floor that this causation reliably produces.
avenues.avenue: avippaṭisāra (non-remorse) as the ROOT cause and its tight binding to sīla — AN 10.1 (Kimatthiya), AN 11.1, the 'what is the purpose of wholesome virtues?' chain. Trace avippaṭisāra to its base in sīla and to its terminus in samādhi.
avenues.rationale: AN 10.1/11.1 make avippaṭisāra the named PURPOSE-fruit of virtue and the named CAUSE of gladness; this is the canon explicitly saying 'virtue → non-remorse → … → samādhi'. The stream-enterer is precisely the one with unbroken sīla (the four limbs of stream-entry include aryan virtues, ariyakanta-sīla).
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Each chain that runs sīla → avippaṭisāra → … → samādhi without inserting a 'now sit and attain jhāna' step demonstrates that the canon routes virtue to concentration causally, not through a separate effortful absorption stage.
avenues.inference_path: If the stream-enterer is defined by perfected ariyakanta-sīla (search that term), and sīla is the canonically-named upstream cause of caused-samādhi, then the stream-enterer ALREADY satisfies the causal precondition for 'the measure' of samādhi — the floor is the concentration that perfected virtue reliably throws off. A hit linking ariyakanta-sīla to samādhi closes the loop between the mattaso-kārī gradient and the upanisā mechanism without ever invoking upacāra-samādhi.

avenues.lens: LENS 17 — concentration as a CAUSED fruit of virtue-and-gladness, not a willed prerequisite. If the canon's natural-causation series (upanisa) makes samādhi arise by itself out of non-remorse → gladness → rapture → tranquillity, then the "doer-to-a-measure" (mattaso kārī) of the stream-enterer/once-returner need not be a separately-cultivated jhānic attainment at all: it is precisely the concentration that the virtue and gladness of one who has seen the Dhamma generates as a by-product. The strategy is to map every place the canon says samādhi is CAUSED/arises (rather than is developed/entered), measure how tightly that caused-samādhi is bound to sīla and to the stream-enterer's profile, and read the "measure" of AN 3.86 as the floor that this causation reliably produces.
avenues.avenue: The mattaso-kārī gradient locus and its immediate verbal neighborhood — AN 3.86, AN 3.87, AN 9.12 (Saupādisesa), Puggalapaññatti. Read what governs 'samādhismiṃ mattaso kārī' grammatically: kārī is agentive ('a doer'), so even here the canon may frame the LOWER fruit's concentration as something done only 'to a measure', i.e. minimally, while sīla is done in-full.
avenues.rationale: This is the gradient the question hinges on. The juxtaposition silesu paripūrakārī (fulfiller in virtue) vs samādhismiṃ mattaso kārī (doer-to-a-measure in concentration) for the SAME stream-enterer is the canon quantifying the relationship: full virtue, partial concentration. Lens 17 reads that 'partial' as exactly the caused by-product of the full virtue.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The exact distribution of paripūra-kārī vs mattaso-kārī across the three trainings for each of the seven/nine person-types pins down which faculty is required in-full at stream-entry (virtue) and which is permitted partial (concentration, wisdom).
avenues.inference_path: If virtue is uniformly paripūra (full) at the lowest fruit while concentration is uniformly mattaso (measured/partial), and the upanisā chain makes full-virtue CAUSE some samādhi, then the simplest reading is: the 'measure' of samādhi present at stream-entry is not independently cultivated to a jhānic threshold — it is whatever full virtue causes. This directly answers 'how much concentration does the lower fruit require': as much as perfected sīla-plus-gladness produces, no nominated jhāna floor. A near-absence of any verse pairing the lower fruit with a NAMED jhāna number reinforces this.

The whole jhāna-vs-dry-insight debate may be mis-framed if the canon's own causal grammar treats samādhi as effect, not entry-ticket. The strategy is to map every place the canon says samādhi is CAUSED/arises (rather than is developed/entered), measure how tightly that caused-samādhi is bound to sīla and to the stream-enterer's profile, and read the "measure" of AN 3.86 as the floor that this causation reliably produces.
avenues.avenue: Other CAUSED-samādhi formulae beyond the canonical upanisā — every place samādhi is the predicate of an arising-verb (uppajjati, hoti, saṃvattati) rather than a cultivation-object. e.g. 'samāhito yathābhūtaṃ pajānāti' (concentrated, one knows as it is) and its converse, plus 'pamuditassa pīti jāyati'.
avenues.rationale: Broadening past the named Upanisā sutta catches the same causal grammar wherever it recurs (it is a stock pericope embedded in many suttas, e.g. the gradual-training endings, anussati suttas, AN sets on five/ten benefits). The density of the resultative frame across the corpus measures how DEFAULT the caused-samādhi model is.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: A high count of resultative-frame samādhi across heterogeneous suttas (recollection, generosity, virtue, breath) shows caused-samādhi is the canon's normal mode and effortful-entry jhāna is the marked, specialized case — inverting the commentary's framing where upacāra is the stepping-stone TO the real thing.
avenues.inference_path: If caused-samādhi appears in dozens of non-jhāna contexts (recollection of the Buddha, generosity, virtue) as enough to ground 'knows as it is', then concentration sufficient for insight is canonically reachable WITHOUT the jhāna pericope. That is an inference for the dry/bare-insight side drawn not from absence but from the POSITIVE breadth of the caused-samādhi formula. Conversely, if every resultative-frame instance still terminates in the four jhānas by name, that is an inference for the jhāna-required side.

avenues.lens: LENS 17 — concentration as a CAUSED fruit of virtue-and-gladness, not a willed prerequisite. If the canon's natural-causation series (upanisa) makes samādhi arise by itself out of non-remorse → gladness → rapture → tranquillity, then the "doer-to-a-measure" (mattaso kārī) of the stream-enterer/once-returner need not be a separately-cultivated jhānic attainment at all: it is precisely the concentration that the virtue and gladness of one who has seen the Dhamma generates as a by-product. The whole jhāna-vs-dry-insight debate may be mis-framed if the canon's own causal grammar treats samādhi as effect, not entry-ticket. The strategy is to map every place the canon says samādhi is CAUSED/arises (rather than is developed/entered), measure how tightly that caused-samādhi is bound to sīla and to the stream-enterer's profile, and read the "measure" of AN 3.86 as the floor that this causation reliably produces.
avenues.avenue: The recollection (anussati) and devotional caused-samādhi suttas — AN 6.10 (Mahānāma), AN 11.11-13, the six recollections where a noble disciple (often explicitly a stream-enterer / Mahānāma is a once-returner) recollecting the Buddha/Dhamma/Saṅgha/sīla/cāga/devatā produces, by that gladness, a mind that 'becomes concentrated'. This is caused-samādhi attached SPECIFICALLY to a named lower-fruit attainer.
avenues.rationale: AN 6.10 is the smoking gun: Mahānāma the Sakyan (a stream-enterer/once-returner per tradition) asks how a noble disciple who has reached the fruit dwells, and the Buddha gives the six recollections, each ending 'the mind becomes upright … gladness arises … of one gladdened rapture … the body becomes tranquil … he feels pleasure … of one in pleasure the mind becomes concentrated (sukhino cittaṃ samādhiyati)'. The concentration of the ALREADY-attained lower-fruit disciple is here explicitly the caused product of devotional gladness — not jhāna practice.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Direct textual proof that a named lower-fruit attainer's everyday samādhi is generated by recollection-gladness. The phrase 'sukhino cittaṃ samādhiyati' applied to a sotāpanna/sakadāgāmī is the canon describing the very 'measure' of concentration the lower fruits carry.
avenues.inference_path: This is the strongest single inference for Lens 17: the canon hands us a stream-enterer-grade practitioner and tells us his concentration comes from gladness, not from jhāna-sitting. If the recollection suttas NEVER ask such a disciple to also attain the four jhānas to be 'a doer-to-a-measure', the measure is the recollection-caused samādhi. A co-occurrence of buddhānussati + samādhiyati + ariyasāvaka, with NO jhāna pericope appended, would settle that the lower-fruit floor is sub-jhānic-yet-canonical (no upacāra label needed).

avenues.lens: LENS 17 — concentration as a CAUSED fruit of virtue-and-gladness, not a willed prerequisite. If the canon's natural-causation series (upanisa) makes samādhi arise by itself out of non-remorse → gladness → rapture → tranquillity, then the "doer-to-a-measure" (mattaso kārī) of the stream-enterer/once-returner need not be a separately-cultivated jhānic attainment at all: it is precisely the concentration that the virtue and gladness of one who has seen the Dhamma generates as a by-product. The whole jhāna-vs-dry-insight debate may be mis-framed if the canon's own causal grammar treats samādhi as effect, not entry-ticket. The strategy is to map every place the canon says samādhi is CAUSED/arises (rather than is developed/entered), measure how tightly that caused-samādhi is bound to sīla and to the stream-enterer's profile, and read the "measure" of AN 3.86 as the floor that this causation reliably produces.
avenues.avenue: Stream-entry profile suttas — the four limbs (sotāpattiyaṅga), the four factors-for-stream-entry, and whether samādhi/jhāna appears among the REQUIREMENTS. Search the defining lists to see what concentration, if any, is named as constitutive of the lower fruits.
avenues.rationale: If the canonical definition of what makes a stream-enterer (the four limbs: confirmed faith in Buddha/Dhamma/Saṅgha + ariyan virtue; or the four factors: associating with good people, hearing Dhamma, wise attention, practice in accordance) contains NO jhāna and NO numbered samādhi, that absence is dispositive for what the lower fruit requires.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The constitutive lists for stream-entry and their contents. The presence of ariyakanta-sīla but absence of any samādhi-grade in the four limbs is the canon telling us virtue, not concentration, is the non-negotiable component of the lowest fruit.
avenues.inference_path: A NEAR-ABSENCE inference: if the four sotāpattiyaṅgas name virtue and faith but never a jhāna or a samādhi-measure, then jhāna is not constitutive of stream-entry — and the only concentration in the picture is whatever the named factors (ariyan virtue → non-remorse → gladness) causally yield. This converts the silence into positive support for the caused-fruit reading and against a jhāna prerequisite for the lowest fruit. Cross-checking that yoniso manasikāra (wise attention), not jhāna, is the named cognitive factor reinforces an insight-led, virtue-grounded, caused-concentration route.

avenues.lens: LENS 17 — concentration as a CAUSED fruit of virtue-and-gladness, not a willed prerequisite. The whole jhāna-vs-dry-insight debate may be mis-framed if the canon's own causal grammar treats samādhi as effect, not entry-ticket. The strategy is to map every place the canon says samādhi is CAUSED/arises (rather than is developed/entered), measure how tightly that caused-samādhi is bound to sīla and to the stream-enterer's profile, and read the "measure" of AN 3.86 as the floor that this causation reliably produces.
avenues.avenue: The agentive-cultivation counter-vocabulary, mapped as a CONTRAST set — every place samādhi/jhāna IS framed as effortfully developed/entered (samādhiṃ bhāveti, jhānaṃ samāpajjati, jhānaṃ upasampajja viharati, ārammaṇe cittaṃ ṭhapeti). Mapping where this marked agentive frame attaches tells us whether it clusters on the higher fruits / full-fledged meditators.
avenues.rationale: To argue caused-samādhi is the lower-fruit floor, Lens 17 needs the CONTRAST: where does the canon use the effortful-attainment language, and does it co-locate with non-returner / arahant / formal jhāna-master profiles rather than with sotāpanna? The distribution is the argument.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Whether agentive jhāna-attainment language clusters away from the stream-enterer (e.g. on the kāyasakkhī, ubhatobhāgavimutta, jhāyī, the non-returner as samādhi-fulfiller) — confirming a division of labor: caused-samādhi suffices low, cultivated-jhāna marks high.
avenues.inference_path: If 'samādhismiṃ paripūrakārī' (the FULFILLER in concentration = the non-returner per AN 3.86) co-occurs with the agentive jhāna pericope while 'mattaso kārī' (the lower fruits) never does, the canon's own division between caused-measure and cultivated-fullness lines up exactly with the stream-enterer/non-returner boundary. That is a structural inference: the non-returner is the one who has turned the by-product into a project; the stream-enterer just has the by-product. Even an orthogonal hit — the anāgāmī's link to jhāna via the pure-abodes / rūpa rebirth — supports it (jhāna becomes load-bearing only at the fruit where it is the fulfilled training).

avenues.lens: LENS 17 — concentration as a CAUSED fruit of virtue-and-gladness, not a willed prerequisite. If the canon's natural-causation series (upanisa) makes samādhi arise by itself out of non-remorse → gladness → rapture → tranquillity, then the "doer-to-a-measure" (mattaso kārī) of the stream-enterer/once-returner need not be a separately-cultivated jhānic attainment at all: it is precisely the concentration that the virtue and gladness of one who has seen the Dhamma generates as a by-product. The whole jhāna-vs-dry-insight debate may be mis-framed if the canon's own causal grammar treats samādhi as effect, not entry-ticket. The strategy is to map every place the canon says samādhi is CAUSED/arises (rather than is developed/entered), measure how tightly that caused-samādhi is bound to sīla and to the stream-enterer's profile, and read the "measure" of AN 3.86 as the floor that this causation reliably produces.
avenues.avenue: Samādhi defined as a FACULTY/POWER that grows by repetition vs. as a state entered — samādhindriya / samādhibala, and the indriya-vibhaṅga where the concentration-faculty is 'gained by making release the object' (vossaggārammaṇaṃ karitvā labhati samādhiṃ). 'labhati samādhiṃ' is again a getting/receiving verb, not a making verb.
avenues.rationale: The faculty definition (SN 48 Indriya-saṃyutta) sometimes equates samādhindriya with the four jhānas (strong reading) but elsewhere defines it via 'making release the object, one GAINS concentration' (labhati) — a receptive verb. The tension between these two definitions is itself the canon's unsettledness about whether concentration is a got-fruit or a built-attainment.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Which definition of the concentration-faculty dominates, and whether the 'gains by making release the object' (insight-flavored, caused) definition appears independently of the 'four jhānas' (cultivated) definition.
avenues.inference_path: If samādhindriya has a release-object/gaining definition that does NOT route through the four jhānas, then the canon licenses a concentration-faculty grown by the soteriological orientation itself (toward nibbāna/release) rather than by absorption drills — i.e. a caused, insight-coupled samādhi. The stream-enterer, who has 'entered the stream' toward release, would possess exactly this release-oriented samādhindriya as a fruit of stream-entry, not a precondition. A hit where samādhindriya is listed as merely PRESENT (not perfected) in the trainee maps onto mattaso-kārī.

avenues.lens: LENS 17 — concentration as a CAUSED fruit of virtue-and-gladness, not a willed prerequisite. The whole jhāna-vs-dry-insight debate may be mis-framed if the canon's own causal grammar treats samādhi as effect, not entry-ticket. The strategy is to map every place the canon says samādhi is CAUSED/arises (rather than is developed/entered), measure how tightly that caused-samādhi is bound to sīla and to the stream-enterer's profile, and read the "measure" of AN 3.86 as the floor that this causation reliably produces.
avenues.avenue: The momentary / non-distraction senses of samādhi — sammā-samādhi defined functionally as cittassa ekaggatā (one-pointedness) and the question whether 'right concentration' in the eightfold path of a stream-enterer must be the four jhānas or can be the unification that accompanies any wholesome citta. Search the path-factor definitions and the Abhidhamma's ekaggatā-as-universal.
avenues.rationale: MN 117 (Mahācattārīsaka) defines noble right-concentration as one-pointedness EQUIPPED WITH the seven path-factors (sa-upaniso saparikkhāro) — concentration defined by its companionship with right view etc., i.e. relationally/causally, not by a jhāna-depth number. And the absence of 'upacāra' / 'khaṇika' from the canon (per the brief, 0 canon for upacāra) is itself a finding: the commentary's sub-jhānic access-concentration has no canonical lexeme.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: That noble right-concentration is canonically defined by its path-factor companionship (caused/relational) rather than purely by jhāna depth; and that the commentarial access-concentration vocabulary is absent from the live CST canon layer.
avenues.inference_path: Two inferences. (1) If MN 117 defines ariya-sammā-samādhi as ekaggatā EQUIPPED WITH right view/intention/etc., then the concentration the path requires is one DEFINED by its causes (the other path factors), which is exactly Lens 17's caused-samādhi — the stream-enterer who has right view has the companionship that constitutes noble concentration. (2) A confirmed near-zero for upacārasamādhi / khaṇikasamādhi in the canon vs many hits in commentary/ṭīkā is a layer-signature inference: the sub-jhānic measure that fills the gap is a commentarial construct, so the canon's 'measure' must be read from canonical materials (caused-samādhi, mattaso-kārī) alone, not back-projected from upacāra.

avenues.lens: LENS 17 — concentration as a CAUSED fruit of virtue-and-gladness, not a willed prerequisite. If the canon's natural-causation series (upanisa) makes samādhi arise by itself out of non-remorse → gladness → rapture → tranquillity, then the "doer-to-a-measure" (mattaso kārī) of the stream-enterer/once-returner need not be a separately-cultivated jhānic attainment at all: it is precisely the concentration that the virtue and gladness of one who has seen the Dhamma generates as a by-product. The whole jhāna-vs-dry-insight debate may be mis-framed if the canon's own causal grammar treats samādhi as effect, not entry-ticket. The strategy is to map every place the canon says samādhi is CAUSED/arises (rather than is developed/entered), measure how tightly that caused-samādhi is bound to sīla and to the stream-enterer's profile, and read the "measure" of AN 3.86 as the floor that this causation reliably produces.
avenues.avenue: Gladness/joy lexicon as the ENGINE of caused-samādhi — pāmojja, pīti, pasāda, vedā (atthaveda/dhammaveda), somanassa, and especially the dhamma-born joy (dhammapīti) that a stream-enterer has from seeing the Dhamma. Map joy → concentration links wherever they occur, including in seemingly non-meditative (devotional, ethical) suttas.
avenues.rationale: Joy is the mechanical link in every causal chain (gladness → rapture → tranquillity → samādhi). If the stream-enterer is canonically characterized by a special dhamma-joy (seeing the deathless, the four limbs include joyous confidence/avecca-pasāda), then the joy that drives caused-samādhi is precisely a hallmark of the lower fruit.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: How tightly dhamma-joy / aveccappasāda (a defining limb of stream-entry) is wired to the samādhiyati outcome across the corpus, and whether joy-driven concentration is presented as enough for the lower-fruit's tasks.
avenues.inference_path: If aveccappasāda (confirmed confidence — a stream-entry limb) is a species of pasāda/pāmojja, and pāmojja is the canonical cause of samādhiyati, then the stream-enterer possesses, by definition of the fruit, the engine of caused-samādhi. The 'measure' of concentration at stream-entry is therefore not under-cultivated jhāna but fully-running dhamma-joy producing concentration as exhaust. Even an orthogonal hit — joy from generosity (cāga) producing samādhi in a layperson — extends the caused-samādhi route to non-meditators, widening the dry-path case.

avenues.lens: LENS 17 — concentration as a CAUSED fruit of virtue-and-gladness, not a willed prerequisite. If the canon's natural-causation series (upanisa) makes samādhi arise by itself out of non-remorse → gladness → rapture → tranquillity, then the "doer-to-a-measure" (mattaso kārī) of the stream-enterer/once-returner need not be a separately-cultivated jhānic attainment at all: it is precisely the concentration that the virtue and gladness of one who has seen the Dhamma generates as a by-product. The whole jhāna-vs-dry-insight debate may be mis-framed if the canon's own causal grammar treats samādhi as effect, not entry-ticket. The strategy is to map every place the canon says samādhi is CAUSED/arises (rather than is developed/entered), measure how tightly that caused-samādhi is bound to sīla and to the stream-enterer's profile, and read the "measure" of AN 3.86 as the floor that this causation reliably produces.
avenues.avenue: Negative/contrast engine — kukkucca (worry/remorse) and vippaṭisāra as the BLOCKERS of caused-samādhi, and the five hindrances as what virtue-born non-remorse removes. The mechanism is symmetric: remorse blocks samādhi, non-remorse releases it.
avenues.rationale: The hindrances are 'corruptions of mind that weaken wisdom'; non-remorse from virtue dissolves the worry-hindrance and (with faith) the doubt-hindrance — both of which the stream-enterer has structurally removed (a sotāpanna has abandoned doubt, vicikicchā, one of the five lower fetters). So the stream-enterer has canonically cleared the very hindrances that block caused-samādhi.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The mapping of which hindrances the stream-enterer has structurally removed (doubt for certain; worry to the degree virtue is perfected) and how hindrance-removal is the gateway clause to the jhāna/samādhi formula.
avenues.inference_path: If a sotāpanna has abandoned vicikicchā (doubt) and his perfected ariyan virtue suppresses kukkucca (worry), then two of the five hindrances are structurally down for the stream-enterer — and since hindrance-removal is the canonical pre-condition of samādhi arising, the stream-enterer is hindrance-cleared enough for caused-samādhi to flow. This infers the FLOOR from the fetter/hindrance accounting: the lower fruit's concentration-readiness is a function of which obstructions the fruit itself removes, not of a separately-logged jhāna count. A co-occurrence of 'doubt abandoned' + 'mind becomes concentrated' would be decisive.

avenues.avenue: Layer-signature contrast — upacāra-samādhi, khaṇika-samādhi, appanā-samādhi, parikamma as COMMENTARIAL fillers. Confirm their distribution is commentary/ṭīkā-heavy and canon-light, and read how the Aṭṭhakathā glosses the upanisā-samādhi and the mattaso-kārī measure (does it reach for upacāra to explain the 'measure'?).
avenues.rationale: The whole question turns on whether the commentary's tripartite parikamma/upacāra/appanā scheme is read back into the canonical 'measure'. If the Aṭṭhakathā glosses 'samādhismiṃ mattaso kārī' as 'upacāra-samādhi', that is the commentary supplying the sub-jhānic floor the canon never named — and naming the layer is naming the move.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Whether the commentary explicitly equates the canonical 'measure' with upacāra-samādhi, and the canon-vs-commentary distribution of the access/momentary/absorption tripartition.
avenues.inference_path: If upacāra/khaṇika/appanā are near-zero in the canon strata and dense in Aṭṭhakathā/ṭīkā, AND the Aṭṭhakathā on AN 3.86 / Puggalapaññatti glosses mattaso-kārī with upacāra, then the answer to 'how much concentration' splits cleanly by layer: the canon gives only a caused gradient (Lens 17's reading), and the quantified sub-jhānic floor is a commentarial overlay. This is the central inference the whole study needs — it locates the 'access concentration' answer in the layer that invented it, and leaves the canonical floor as the caused-samādhi of the virtuous, glad stream-enterer. Even a near-absence in canon is positive evidence here.

The strategy is to map every place the canon says samādhi is CAUSED/arises (rather than is developed/entered), measure how tightly that caused-samādhi is bound to sīla and to the stream-enterer's profile, and read the "measure" of AN 3.86 as the floor that this causation reliably produces.
avenues.avenue: Orthogonal probe — caused-samādhi for LAYPEOPLE and in generosity/giving (dāna, cāga) and the death-bed / Anāthapiṇḍika contexts, where samādhi-by-gladness reaches people who plainly never sat the four jhānas. AN on giving, the deva-rebirth-by-confidence suttas.
avenues.rationale: If even householders generate the gladness → tranquillity → concentration sequence through generosity and confirmed confidence (and many are stream-enterers, e.g. Anāthapiṇḍika), then caused-samādhi is documented in populations the jhāna-prerequisite reading cannot easily accommodate. The orthogonal subject (giving) hides an inference about the concentration floor.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: That the gladness-to-concentration causal sequence is attested for lay, generosity-based practice — extending caused-samādhi well beyond the meditation hall and onto named lower-fruit laypeople.
avenues.inference_path: If a layperson stream-enterer's concentration is repeatedly sourced to giving-gladness and confidence-gladness rather than to jhāna attainment, then the canon's own population of lower-fruit attainers is dominated by people whose 'measure' of samādhi is the caused kind. That is a demographic inference: the modal sotāpanna in the suttas is not depicted attaining four jhānas, so the floor that fits the actual cast is caused-samādhi. This is the strongest orthogonal support — it argues from WHO the stream-enterers are, not from concentration vocabulary directly. A near-absence of jhāna-attainment ascribed to named lay sotāpannas would be telling.

The strategy is to map every place the canon says samādhi is CAUSED/arises (rather than is developed/entered), measure how tightly that caused-samādhi is bound to sīla and to the stream-enterer's profile, and read the "measure" of AN 3.86 as the floor that this causation reliably produces.
avenues.avenue: Sequencing/causation direction suttas that put insight BEFORE deep concentration or make them mutually conditioning — AN 4.92-94 (samatha/vipassanā pairings), AN 4.170 (the four modes incl. vipassanā-first / yuganaddha), SN 12.23's placement of samādhi BELOW yathābhūta-ñāṇadassana, and the dhammayoga vs jhāyī tension (AN 6.46 Mahācunda).
avenues.rationale: AN 4.170 explicitly canonizes vipassanā-then-samatha and yoked modes — the canon admits insight can lead. SN 12.23 places samādhi as the upanisā of knowledge-and-vision, but the chain CONTINUES to nibbidā/virāga, so samādhi here is mid-chain, caused by sukha and itself a support for further insight — not a terminal jhānic project. AN 6.46 shows the canon refereeing a dispute between meditators (jhāyī) and Dhamma-experts (dhammayoga), conceding both are rare and valuable.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: That the canon licenses multiple sequencings including insight-leading and yoked, and positions the upanisā-samādhi as an intermediate caused link rather than a gate one must fully open first.
avenues.inference_path: If AN 4.170's four modes are canonical, then a jhāna-first absorption requirement is NOT the only authorized route, and a vipassanā-leading practitioner's samādhi can be the caused/yoked kind that arises alongside insight. Combined with SN 12.23 making samādhi the CAUSE of knowledge-and-vision (so it precedes insight causally but is itself caused by sukha), the picture is a causal lattice, not a ladder with a jhāna gate. The inference: 'how much concentration the lower fruit requires' is answered by 'enough to support yathābhūta-ñāṇa', and that quantum is the caused-samādhi of the glad, virtuous mind — not a nominated jhāna. The dhammayoga/jhāyī even-handedness (AN 6.46) is an explicit canonical refusal to make jhāna the sole path, which is itself an inference against the strong jhāna-required reading.

avenues.lens: LENS 17 — concentration as a CAUSED fruit of virtue-and-gladness, not a willed prerequisite. The whole jhāna-vs-dry-insight debate may be mis-framed if the canon's own causal grammar treats samādhi as effect, not entry-ticket. The strategy is to map every place the canon says samādhi is CAUSED/arises (rather than is developed/entered), measure how tightly that caused-samādhi is bound to sīla and to the stream-enterer's profile, and read the "measure" of AN 3.86 as the floor that this causation reliably produces.
avenues.avenue: Gradual-training (anupubbasikkhā) embedding of the causal chain — the long pericope (DN 2 Sāmaññaphala, MN 27, MN 39, etc.) where virtue → sense-restraint → mindfulness → contentment → abandoning hindrances → and ONLY THEN gladness/rapture/tranquillity/sukha 'so concentrated' → jhānas. Examine whether gladness-and-concentration are presented as the FRUIT of the virtue-and-restraint stages that precede the jhānas, i.e. caused before the jhāna formula even begins.
avenues.rationale: In the gradual training, by the time the jhāna formula starts, the practitioner has ALREADY generated gladness → rapture → tranquillity → sukha → concentration through the abandoning of the hindrances (which itself rests on virtue and restraint). The jhāna pericope then RE-states the concentrated mind. So the canon already shows caused-samādhi arising at the hinge between hindrance-abandoning and jhāna — the concentration is caused before it is deepened.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The exact junction where 'samāhitaṃ cittaṃ' first appears in the gradual training and whether it is predicated on the virtue/restraint/hindrance-abandoning chain (caused) rather than on a deliberate absorption technique.
avenues.inference_path: If 'concentrated' (samāhita) first arises in the gradual training as the terminus of the gladness chain that the hindrance-abandoning produced — i.e. BEFORE and as the precondition OF the first jhāna — then the canon itself stages caused-samādhi as upstream of cultivated-jhāna. The stream-enterer, having perfected the virtue/restraint base, stands exactly at that caused-samādhi junction; whether he proceeds INTO the four jhānas is the optional deepening, not the requirement of the fruit. This embeds Lens 17 in the canon's flagship training sequence and infers the floor (caused-samādhi) from where the pericope places it. A near-absence of any jhāna attainment in the stream-entry attainment-narratives, contrasted with its presence before arahantship narratives, would confirm the staging.

avenues.lens: LENS 18 — deliberately orthogonal avenues: where data NOT about meditation still constrains how much concentration awakening (especially the lower fruits) requires. The governing logic: every attested attainer whose circumstances preclude or constrain deep jhāna acts as an upper bound on the concentration-floor for that fruit. Conversely, every attainer described mid-absorption, or every text that ties a fruit to a jhānic prerequisite, raises the floor. A near-absence (e.g. no jhāna-attainment ever predicated of a layperson at the moment of stream-entry) is itself evidence: it shows the canon did not feel the need to install jhāna in the causal slot. The strategy is to harvest the incidental, narrative, biographical, juridical, and demographic material — none of it "about meditation" — and read the concentration-floor off the circumstances of attainment rather than off any doctrinal statement of the measure.
avenues.avenue: Lay stream-enterers and once-returners enumerated by name (the upasaka/upasika rolls) — their sheer number and their householder circumstances bound the floor
avenues.rationale: The canon names a large class of lay attainers (Mahanama, Anathapindika, Visakha, the 500 at various discourses, plus the Janavasabha/Mahaparinibbana tallies of named lay dead reborn as stream-enterers/once-returners). Householders are nowhere described undertaking systematic jhana cultivation as a precondition; if jhana were the causal requirement for these fruits, the canon's own demographics would be incoherent.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Each named lay sotapanna/sakadagami with no jhana attribution is a data point that the lower-two fruits did not, in the redactors' own bookkeeping, require attained jhana. The graded types (sattakkhattuparama, kolankola, ekabiji) attached to lay figures show the fruit was finely sub-classified yet never indexed to jhana-count.
avenues.inference_path: A high count of named lay attainers with zero co-occurring jhana vocabulary in their pericopes licenses the inference that the concentration-floor for stream-entry/once-return sits AT OR BELOW whatever momentary/access steadiness a layperson has — i.e. below first jhana. Near-absence of jhana terms in these rolls is the load-bearing signal.

avenues.lens: LENS 18 — deliberately orthogonal avenues: where data NOT about meditation still constrains how much concentration awakening (especially the lower fruits) requires. The governing logic: every attested attainer whose circumstances preclude or constrain deep jhāna acts as an upper bound on the concentration-floor for that fruit. Conversely, every attainer described mid-absorption, or every text that ties a fruit to a jhānic prerequisite, raises the floor. A near-absence (e.g. no jhāna-attainment ever predicated of a layperson at the moment of stream-entry) is itself evidence: it shows the canon did not feel the need to install jhāna in the causal slot. The strategy is to harvest the incidental, narrative, biographical, juridical, and demographic material — none of it "about meditation" — and read the concentration-floor off the circumstances of attainment rather than off any doctrinal statement of the measure.
avenues.avenue: Mass / on-the-spot stream-entry while listening to a discourse (the 'dhammacakkhu' formula) — attainment with no sitting practice, no posture, no breath, in the time of a talk
avenues.rationale: The dhammacakkhu pericope (Kondanna at the first sermon, Upali, Suppabuddha the leper, Yasa's father, countless lay hearers) records the breakthrough to stream-entry as occurring DURING a graduated talk — temporally too compressed for any jhana-attainment routine, with hearers seated to listen, not to meditate.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Every occurrence ties the lower-fruit breakthrough to a cognitive event ('whatever is subject to arising is subject to cessation') reached through hearing, with the mind merely 'ready, malleable, free of hindrances' (kallacitta...) — a vivisection of the prerequisite that names hindrance-freedom and gladness, NOT jhana.
avenues.inference_path: If the canon's standard recipe for the prerequisite mental state is the kallacitta/muducitta/vinivaranacitta cluster (hindrance-suppressed gladness) and NEVER 'having emerged from the fourth jhana', then the redactors located the floor at hindrance-suppression-plus-clarity, which the commentary itself glosses as upacara-grade. The orthogonal inference: the SETTING (a talk, listeners seated, food about to be served at Yasa's father's house) physically excludes a jhana session, so the floor must be reachable without one.

Conversely, every attainer described mid-absorption, or every text that ties a fruit to a jhānic prerequisite, raises the floor. A near-absence (e.g. no jhāna-attainment ever predicated of a layperson at the moment of stream-entry) is itself evidence: it shows the canon did not feel the need to install jhāna in the causal slot. The strategy is to harvest the incidental, narrative, biographical, juridical, and demographic material — none of it "about meditation" — and read the concentration-floor off the circumstances of attainment rather than off any doctrinal statement of the measure.
avenues.avenue: Deathbed / dying / acutely ill attainers — bodies in pain, no capacity for absorption posture, yet attaining or consolidating fruits
avenues.rationale: Several figures attain or secure fruits at death or in extreme illness (Dighavu the lay-follower in SN, Anathapindika dying, the monk in Khema/Channa-type accounts, the Girimananda/Phagguna deathbed teachings). Extreme pain physiologically blocks fine absorption, so a fruit secured under it cannot have required concurrent jhana.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: A fruit (often once-return → non-return, or confirmation of stream-entry) consolidated while the body is wracked with sharp feeling, with the teaching given being contemplative-cognitive (impermanence, the recollections) rather than an instruction to enter jhana.
avenues.inference_path: Pain that 'overwhelms the mind' is the textbook obstacle to jhana, yet fruits land in exactly that condition. This bounds the concentration-floor below sustained absorption: whatever steadiness a dying person in sharp pain can muster is sufficient for at least the consolidation of the lower fruits and even the step to non-return. The co-occurrence of kharavedana with phala-attainment is a direct upper bound.

The strategy is to harvest the incidental, narrative, biographical, juridical, and demographic material — none of it "about meditation" — and read the concentration-floor off the circumstances of attainment rather than off any doctrinal statement of the measure.
avenues.avenue: Speed-of-awakening cases — the 'while the verse was being spoken', 'in that very seat', razor-fast attainments that leave no room for a jhana series
avenues.rationale: Formulae like 'at the conclusion of the verse/exposition, the dhamma-eye arose in many thousands' (gathapariyosane / desanapariyosane / imasmim ca pana veyyakaranasmim bhannamane) compress attainment into the duration of a single utterance. A four-jhana sequence cannot fit inside one verse.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Bulk lower-fruit attainment timed to the END of a spoken unit, often for crowds, with no narrated interval for absorption.
avenues.inference_path: Temporal impossibility is the inference engine: if many attain stream-entry in the seconds it takes to finish a verse, the prerequisite concentration must be a state already available to an attentive listener, not a jhana to be entered. This is an upper bound far below first jhana — it argues the 'measure' for the lowest fruit is momentary-to-access grade. Even the commentary's khippabhinna ('swift') typing of some arahants tacitly concedes the lower fruits can be near-instant.

The strategy is to harvest the incidental, narrative, biographical, juridical, and demographic material — none of it "about meditation" — and read the concentration-floor off the circumstances of attainment rather than off any doctrinal statement of the measure.
avenues.avenue: Women, children, and socially-constrained attainers who lacked monastic seclusion infrastructure
avenues.rationale: Stream-entry and once-return are attributed to women and household dependents (Visakha, Khujjuttara the slave-woman, Samavati's 500 women, Matikamata, the antepura women) who had no access to forest seclusion, no nine-stage jhana training program, and ongoing domestic duties.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: Lower fruits predicated of figures whose social position structurally barred sustained absorption training, often while continuing household/court life.
avenues.inference_path: Khujjuttara (a hunchback slave who attains stream-entry, then teaches the antepura) is the sharpest case: a slave cannot run a jhana retreat. Her attainment bounds the floor below any regime requiring leisure or seclusion. The demographic spread of lay female attainers, read against the total absence of jhana-training narratives for them, licenses inferring the floor is reachable inside ordinary constrained life — i.e. sub-jhanic.

avenues.avenue: The faith-liberated / view-attained / body-witness typology (the seven ariya-puggala) — only some are defined by formless/jhanic touch, the lower ones are NOT
avenues.rationale: AN/MN's seven-persons taxonomy (Kitagiri MN 70, AN 3.21, Puggalapannatti) explicitly says only kayasakkhi and ubhatobhagavimutta 'touch the formless liberations with the body'; saddhanusari, dhammanusari, saddhavimutta, ditthippatta do NOT — yet they are on the path / have entered the fruit.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: The canon's own type-system defines the lower types as those who have penetrated by faith or view WITHOUT the jhanic/formless 'bodily touching' that defines the higher types.
avenues.inference_path: This is near-decisive structurally: the redactors built a taxonomy in which the presence of the (formless) attainments is the differentia of the TOP types, which entails the lower types reach their station without them. So the floor for the saddhanusari/dhammanusari (path-of-stream-entry) and saddhavimutta/ditthippatta (fruit) is by definition below the jhanic-touch line. The Kitagiri statement that the dhammanusari/saddhavimutta still has 'work to do with diligence' but is already incapable of dying without the fruit pins the floor low.

avenues.avenue: Vinaya juridical material — offences, rules, and case-law that incidentally reveal what attained monks/nuns were doing (i.e. NOT necessarily in jhana) and how attainment was claimed/verified
avenues.rationale: The fourth parajika and its analysis enumerate, for the purpose of defining false claims, the full menu of superhuman attainments a monk might falsely assert — jhana, liberations, concentrations, attainments, path, fruit. The ENUMERATION ORDER and groupings show how the redactors mentally bundled (or separated) samadhi-attainments from magga/phala.
avenues.what_a_hit_shows: An authoritative list that places 'jhana' and 'samadhi/samapatti' as distinct claimable items ALONGSIDE 'magga' and 'phala' rather than as their precondition.
avenues.inference_path: If the Vinaya treats 'I have attained jhana' and 'I have attained the path/fruit' as separately-falsifiable claims (each its own offence), the redactors conceived path-attainment as not entailing jhana-attainment — otherwise a false path-claim would automatically be a false jhana-claim and the separate listing would be redundant. The juridical taxonomy is orthogonal evidence that magga/phala and jhana were conceptually decoupled.
